Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate at Body Temperature (T = 310 K)

$$E = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{z \times 96485} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$$

$$E = \frac{5925.4}{z \times 96485} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$$

$$E = \frac{0.0614}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right) \;\; \text{(volts)}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right) \;\; \text{mV}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Potential for K⁺ (Numerical Worked Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU numerical problem**. Practice it until you can solve it in under 3 minutes.

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM
- $z = +1$, $T = 37°$C

**Step 1 — Identify the form to use:**

$$E_{K^+} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right) \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$E_{K^+} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 4 — Take the log:**

$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = \log_{10}(2.857 \times 10^{-2}) = 0.4559 - 2 = -1.5441$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$E_{K^+} = 61.5 \times (-1.5441) = -94.96 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{K^+} \approx -94.96 \text{ mV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the **standard K⁺ Nernst potential** quoted in physiology textbooks. The negative sign indicates that the *inside* of the cell is **negative** with respect to the outside when the membrane is selectively permeable to K⁺ alone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Potential for Na⁺ (Quick Numerical)

**Given:** $[Na^+]_{in} = 10$ mM, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM, $z = +1$

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$E_{Na^+} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{10}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{145}{10} = 14.5$$

**Step 3 — Logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(14.5) = 1.1614$$

**Step 4 — Final:**

$$E_{Na^+} = 61.5 \times 1.1614 = +71.43 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na^+} \approx +60 \text{ mV (approximately, using standard textbook values)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The positive sign shows that if the membrane were selectively permeable *only* to Na⁺, the inside of the cell would become **positive** relative to the outside. This is the driving force behind the rapid **depolarization phase** of the action potential, when Na⁺ channels open and $V_m$ shoots toward $E_{Na^+}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Resting Membrane Potential via GHK Equation

**Given** standard mammalian values at 37°C:
- $P_K = 1$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$
- $[K^+]_{out} = 4$, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$, $[Na^+]_{in} = 10$
- $[Cl^-]_{out} = 110$, $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$

**Step 1 — Write the GHK equation:**

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{(1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(110)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$(1)(4) = 4$$
$$(0.04)(145) = 5.8$$
$$(0.45)(4) = 1.8$$
$$\text{Numerator} = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$

**Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$(1)(140) = 140$$
$$(0.04)(10) = 0.4$$
$$(0.45)(110) = 49.5$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 140 + 0.4 + 49.5 = 189.9$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{11.6}{189.9} = 0.0611$$

**Step 5 — Logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.0611) = -1.214$$

**Step 6 — Final multiplication:**

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.214) = -74.66 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx -75 \text{ mV (typical resting potential for mammalian neuron)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Notice that the **K⁺ term alone** would give a ratio of 4/140 = 0.0286, yielding $E_K \approx -95$ mV. But because of the small Na⁺ permeability (0.04), the ratio is slightly higher (0.0611), pulling $V_m$ up to −75 mV. This confirms the **Goldman's principle**: the resting potential lies *between* $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$, but closer to the more permeable ion's Nernst potential.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Cardiac Output from Heart Rate and Stroke Volume

**Given:** $HR = 72$ bpm, $SV = 70$ mL

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$CO = HR \times SV$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$CO = 72 \times 70 = 5040 \text{ mL/min}$$

$$\boxed{CO \approx 5.04 \text{ L/min}}$$

This matches the well-known textbook value of **~5 L/min** for a healthy adult at rest.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Mean Arterial Pressure

**Given:** Systolic BP = 120 mmHg, Diastolic BP = 80 mmHg

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$MAP = DP + \frac{1}{3}(SP - DP)$$

**Step 2 — Compute pulse pressure:**

$$SP - DP = 120 - 80 = 40 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — One-third of pulse pressure:**

$$\frac{40}{3} \approx 13.33 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 4 — Final:**

$$MAP = 80 + 13.33 = 93.33 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$\boxed{MAP \approx 93 \text{ mmHg}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Traps

| Trap | Correct Approach | Typical Error |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting the **sign of $z$** in the denominator | For cations $z = +1$ or $+2$; for anions $z = -1$ | Putting $z = +1$ for Cl⁻ |
| Using **wrong concentration placement** | Use the form that gives the textbook sign ($E_K < 0$ requires ratio $[out]/[in] < 1$) | Swapping intracellular/extracellular |
| Using **273 K** instead of 310 K in Nernst calculation | Always use **310 K** for body temperature problems | Using room temperature constant |
| Forgetting to convert **log₁₀ vs ln** | Use 61.5 mV (base-10) shortcut; or 25.7 mV for natural log at 37°C | Mixing constants |
| GHK equation **Cl⁻ sign flip** | In the GHK form given above, **Cl⁻ concentrations are inverted** (because Cl⁻ is an anion, moving it inward = positive charge moving out) | Putting Cl⁻ in the same direction as cations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram 1 — Cardiovascular Circulation (System-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Body Tissues<br>Deoxygenated Blood] -->|Vena Cava| B[Right Atrium]
    B -->|Tricuspid Valve| C[Right Ventricle]
    C -->|Pulmonary Valve| D[Pulmonary Artery]
    D --> E[Lungs<br>Gas Exchange CO2 out, O2 in]
    E -->|Pulmonary Veins| F[Left Atrium]
    F -->|Mitral Valve| G[Left Ventricle]
    G -->|Aortic Valve| H[Aorta]
    H -->|Arteries and Arterioles| I[Systemic Capillaries<br>at Body Tissues]
    I --> A
    H -.->|Branches| J[Coronary Arteries<br>Heart's Own Blood Supply]
    J --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:** The blue (deoxygenated) loop is the *pulmonary* circulation; the red (oxygenated) loop is the *systemic* circulation. The coronary artery branch shows that the heart muscle *itself* requires blood supply.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram 2 — Cardiac Conduction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SA Node<br>70 to 80 bpm<br>Primary Pacemaker] -->|Internodal Tracts| B[AV Node<br>100 ms Delay]
    B -->|Bundle of His| C[Bundle Branches<br>Left and Right]
    C -->|Purkinje Fibers| D[Ventricular Myocardium<br>Contraction]
    D -->|Mechanical Systole| E[Blood Ejection<br>into Aorta and Pulmonary Artery]
    E -->|Diastole| F[Resting Phase<br>All Cells Repolarize]
    F --> A
    B -.->|Backup Pacemaker| G[AV Junctional Rhythm<br>40 to 60 bpm]
    C -.->|Emergency Backup| H[Ventricular Escape<br>20 to 40 bpm]
```

**Engineering interpretation:** This is essentially a **clock-distribution network** in digital electronics terms. The SA node is the master clock; the AV node is a delay buffer; the bundle branches and Purkinje fibers are high-speed signal traces ensuring synchronized firing.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram 3 — Respiratory Airflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nasal Cavity<br>Filter, Humidify, Warm] --> B[Pharynx]
    B --> C[Larynx<br>Vocal Cords]
    C --> D[Trachea]
    D --> E[Primary Bronchi<br>Left and Right]
    E --> F[Bronchioles]
    F --> G[Alveolar Ducts]
    G --> H[Alveoli<br>300 Million Sacs<br>Surface Area 70 sq m]
    H <-->|Diffusion| I[Pulmonary Capillaries<br>CO2 out, O2 in]
    I --> J[Left Heart<br>Pumps to Body]
    J --> K[Systemic Tissues<br>Cells Use O2, Produce CO2]
    K -->|Venous Return| L[Right Heart]
    L --> E
    M[Diaphragm Contraction] -->|Negative Pressure| H
    H -->|Expiration| M
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram 4 — Resting Potential Generation (Cellular Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Na K ATPase Pump<br>3 Na out, 2 K in per ATP] -->|Maintains Gradients| B[High K inside<br>High Na outside<br>High Cl outside]
    B --> C[Membrane Permeability<br>At Rest: P_K High, P_Na Low]
    C --> D[K Ions Diffuse Outward<br>Down Concentration Gradient]
    D --> E[Impermeant Anions Stay Inside<br>Proteins, Phosphates, Sulfates]
    E --> F[Charge Separation Builds<br>Inside becomes Negative]
    F --> G[Electrical Force Pulls K Back In]
    G --> H{Equilibrium?}
    H -->|Yes, for K alone| I[Nernst Potential E_K = -94 mV]
    H -->|No, other ions still leak| J[Net Resting Potential<br>V_m = -70 to -90 mV]
    J --> K[GHK Equation Models This Balance]
    K --> L[Stable Resting State<br>Cell Ready to Fire]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram 5 — Equivalent Circuit of the Cell Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OutsideECF [Extracellular Fluid]
        O[ECF Node]
    end
    subgraph Membrane [Lipid Bilayer plus Channels]
        RK[K Channel<br>Resistance R_K] 
        RNa[Na Channel<br>Resistance R_Na]
        RCl[Cl Channel<br>Resistance R_Cl]
        CAP[Membrane Capacitance<br>C_m = 1 uF per sq cm]
        BAT[Na K ATPase<br>EMF Source E_NaK]
    end
    subgraph InsideICF [Intracellular Fluid]
        I[ICF Node]
    end
    O --- RK --- I
    O --- RNa --- I
    O --- RCl --- I
    O --- CAP --- I
    BAT --- O
    BAT --- I
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The Hodgkin-Huxley model of the cell membrane is a **parallel RC network** with voltage sources (Nernst batteries) in series with variable resistors (ion channels). Biomedical instrumentation (patch-clamp amplifiers) directly measures currents through individual channels in this network.

---

## 4.6 ASCII Pressure-Volume Loop (Wiggers'-Style Summary)

```
        Pressure (mmHg)
        ^
   120  |        *Ventricular Ejection* (Aortic valve open)
        |       /                        \
        |      /                          \
   80   |-----*--<-- Aortic valve closes   \
        |     |  Isovolumetric Relaxation   \
   25   |     |  Mitral valve opens          \
        |     |    Passive Filling  --------*  <- End Diastolic Point
        |     |  Atrial Systole (kick)     /
        |     *-- Mitral valve closes    /
        |       Isovolumetric Contraction
        |       Aortic valve opens  ----->
        +------------------------------------> Volume (mL)
            50   70   100   120   140
            ESV        EDV
                  ^------ SV = 70 mL
```

The **Pressure-Volume (PV) loop** is the single most important diagram in cardiovascular physiology. The width of the loop = stroke volume; the area enclosed = external work done by the ventricle per beat.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

Define **resting membrane potential**. List the three principal factors responsible for its generation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **resting membrane potential (RMP)** is the steady-state electrical potential difference (~ −70 to −90 mV, inside negative) that exists across the plasma membrane of a living cell when it is not being stimulated by an external signal.

The three principal factors responsible for its generation are:

1. **Ionic concentration gradients** established by the active Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP).
2. **Differential membrane permeability** at rest — the membrane is highly permeable to K⁺ but only minimally permeable to Na⁺ and Cl⁻.
3. **Presence of impermeant intracellular anions** (proteins, organic phosphates) that cannot follow K⁺ outward, thereby creating a charge separation.

(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for the three factors list; 1 mark for correct numerical value of RMP.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Differentiate between **pulmonary** and **systemic circulation** in the cardiovascular system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pulmonary Circulation | Systemic Circulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Path** | Right heart → Lungs → Left heart | Left heart → Body → Right heart |
| **Purpose** | Oxygenation of blood, CO₂ removal | Delivery of O₂/nutrients, waste pickup |
| **Pressure** | Low (25/8 mmHg) | High (120/80 mmHg) |
| **Vessel Walls** | Thinner, more compliant | Thicker, muscular, more elastic |
| **Blood Type Carried** | Deoxygenated (arteries) and Oxygenated (veins) — *opposite of systemic* | Oxygenated (arteries) and Deoxygenated (veins) |

(1 mark for path; 1 mark for pressure; 1 mark for the unique "vein/artery blood type reversal" in pulmonary circulation.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the equilibrium potential of a single ion species across a selectively permeable membrane. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Using the Nernst equation, calculate the equilibrium potential for **K⁺** across a mammalian cell membrane at 37°C, given: $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM. Comment on the significance of the sign and magnitude. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the principle of electrochemical equilibrium (1 mark):**
At equilibrium, the net free energy change for moving an ion across the membrane is zero. The electrical work done by the field exactly balances the chemical work done by the concentration gradient.

**Step 2 — Write the expression for electrical work (1 mark):**

$$W_{elec} = zFE$$

where $z$ is the ion's valence, $F$ is Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol), and $E$ is the membrane potential.

**Step 3 — Write the expression for chemical (osmotic) work (1 mark):**

$$W_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

where $R$ is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

**Step 4 — Apply the equilibrium condition (1 mark):**

$$zFE + RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for E and express the final Nernst equation (2 marks):**

$$E = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Convert to the base-10 form at body temperature (1 mark):**

$$E = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) \text{ mV at 37°C}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the given values (1 mark):**
$z = +1$, $T = 37$°C = 310 K, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM.

**Step 2 — Apply the Nernst equation (1 mark):**

$$E_{K^+} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}}\right) = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{140}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the concentration ratio (1 mark):**

$$\frac{140}{4} = 35$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm (1 mark):**

$$\log_{10}(35) = 1.5441$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 61.5 (1 mark):**

$$E_{K^+} = 61.5 \times 1.5441 = 94.96 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6 — Assign the correct sign and comment (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{E_{K^+} \approx -94.96 \text{ mV}}$$

**Significance of the sign:** The negative sign indicates that the **inside of the cell is electrically negative** with respect to the outside when the membrane is selectively permeable to K⁺ alone. This is the fundamental reason living cells exhibit a negative resting potential.

**Significance of the magnitude:** The magnitude (~−95 mV) is close to the experimentally observed resting potential of most excitable cells, confirming that K⁺ permeability is the dominant factor governing the resting state.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice B]`

**(a)** With a neat labeled block diagram, explain the **sources of bioelectric potentials** in living cells. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **mechanics of breathing** with reference to pressure gradients, lung compliance, and airway resistance. Derive the relationship between these quantities. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
Bioelectric potentials are transmembrane voltages generated by differential ion distributions across the cell membrane, actively maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and passively modulated by selective ion channels.

**Step 2 — Cell membrane structure (1 mark):**
The cell membrane is a **lipid bilayer** (~7 nm thick) studded with three types of proteins: **ion channels** (passive conduits), **ion pumps** (active transporters), and **receptors** (signal transducers).

**Step 3 — Three sources of bioelectric potentials (3 marks):**
1. **Diffusion potentials** — arise from concentration gradients of ions across a selectively permeable membrane. Quantified by the **Nernst equation**.
2. **Equilibrium (Donnan) potentials** — arise from the presence of impermeant ions (e.g., intracellular proteins) on one side of the membrane, creating a fixed charge imbalance.
3. **Active transport potentials** — generated by the energy-consuming **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** that establishes the ionic gradients against which diffusion potentials are measured.

**Step 4 — Role of the Na⁺/K⁺ pump (1 mark):**
Pumps 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP, creating steep gradients (Na⁺ high outside, K⁺ high inside).

**Step 5 — Equivalent circuit and clinical relevance (1 mark):**
The membrane behaves as a parallel RC circuit (R ~ channels, C ~ lipid bilayer, EMF ~ Nernst batteries). Resting potential ~ −70 to −90 mV; ECG, EMG, EEG all measure aggregates of these microscopic bioelectric events.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the driving force of breathing (1 mark):**
Air flows in and out of the lungs because of **pressure gradients** between the alveolar pressure ($P_{alv}$) and the atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm}$). Air moves from high to low pressure.

**Step 2 — Explain lung compliance (2 marks):**
**Compliance ($C$)** is defined as the change in lung volume per unit change in transpulmonary pressure:

$$C = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$$

A stiff lung (e.g., pulmonary fibrosis) has **low compliance**; an overly distensible lung (e.g., emphysema) has **high compliance**. Normal value ~ 0.2 L/cmH₂O.

**Step 3 — Explain airway resistance (1 mark):**
**Airway resistance ($R$)** is the opposition to airflow offered by the conducting airways (trachea, bronchi, bronchioles). Normal value ~ 1–2 cmH₂O·s/L. Constriction (asthma) increases R dramatically.

**Step 4 — Derive the governing equation (2 marks):**
The instantaneous airflow rate $\dot{V}$ is given by the **equation of motion for the respiratory system**:

$$P_{alv} - P_{atm} = \frac{\dot{V}}{C} + R\dot{V}$$

This is essentially a **series RC circuit analog** where the elastance (1/C) acts as a capacitor-like term and the resistance R acts as a resistor.

**Step 5 — Apply to inspiration and expiration (1 mark):**
During **inspiration**, diaphragm contraction makes $P_{alv} < P_{atm}$, so $\dot{V}$ is positive (inward). During **expiration**, relaxation reverses the gradient, and air flows outward.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Cardiovascular and Bioelectricity):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in Nernst equation (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget that the *inside* of the cell is the reference. A positive value of E means inside is *positive* relative to outside — but this is the case for Na⁺ and Ca²⁺, not K⁺.
> 2. **Wrong constant (–1 mark):** Using 26.7 mV (for 25°C) instead of 61.5 mV (for 37°C) in base-10 form. Body temperature is 37°C = 310 K, ALWAYS.
> 3. **Confusing the GHK equation direction for Cl⁻ (–1 mark):** In the standard GHK form, the chloride concentrations are **inverted** relative to cations because Cl⁻ is a negatively charged ion.
> 4. **Forgetting the AV node delay (–1 mark):** Many students miss the fact that the AV node introduces a **~100 ms delay** to allow atrial emptying — this is a *feature*, not a bug, and is essential for efficient cardiac function.
> 5. **Lung volume confusion (–1 mark):** Residual Volume (RV) **cannot** be measured by a spirometer — only TLC, VC, IC, FRC can be inferred indirectly.
> 6. **Cardiac output units (–0.5 mark):** Always express CO in **L/min**, not mL/beat. If you write "5040 mL/min" without conversion, you lose half a mark for unit inconsistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

### 🔬 Cardiovascular System
- ✅ Heart is a **dual pump**: right side = pulmonary (low pressure, 25/8 mmHg); left side = systemic (high pressure, 120/80 mmHg).
- ✅ **CO = HR × SV ≈ 5 L/min** at rest; can rise to ~25 L/min during exercise.
- ✅ **MAP = DP + ⅓(SP − DP) ≈ 93 mmHg** in a normal adult.
- ✅ Conduction sequence: **SA node → AV node (100 ms delay) → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibers → Ventricular myocardium**.
- ✅ The SA node is the primary pacemaker (~70 bpm) because of **overdrive suppression** of slower latent pacemakers.

### 🫁 Respiratory System
- ✅ Four lung volumes: **TV, IRV, ERV, RV**; four capacities: **IC, FRC, VC, TLC**.
- ✅ **Normal TV = 500 mL; RR = 12–20/min; minute ventilation ≈ 6 L/min**.
- ✅ Equation of motion: $P_{alv} - P_{atm} = \dot{V}/C + R\dot{V}$.
- ✅ **Compliance** is the elastic property; **resistance** is the viscous property.
- ✅ Oxygen diffuses down its partial pressure gradient: alveolar PO₂ (~104 mmHg) → arterial blood (~95 mmHg) → tissue (~40 mmHg).

### ⚡ Bioelectric Potentials
- ✅ **Resting potential** = stable negative voltage (~−70 to −90 mV inside) maintained by Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase.
- ✅ **Nernst equation (37°C, base-10)**: $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV — keep the sign of $z$ correct!
- ✅ **Standard values**: $E_K \approx -94$ mV; $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV; $E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV; $E_{Ca} \approx +129$ mV.
- ✅ **GHK voltage equation** gives the *actual* resting potential as a weighted average of all ion Nernst potentials, weighted by their permeabilities.
- ✅ Resting potential lies **closer to E_K** because $P_K \gg P_{Na}$ at rest.
- ✅ The cell membrane is a **parallel RC network** with **variable resistors** (channels), **capacitor** (lipid bilayer), and **EMF sources** (ion gradients).
- ✅ All clinical bioelectric measurements (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, ERG) are macroscopic summations of millions of microscopic cellular resting-and-action potentials.

### 🧠 Engineering Takeaways
- ✅ **Instrumentation amplifiers** for biopotential recording must have **high input impedance (>10 MΩ)**, **high CMRR (>100 dB)**, and **low noise (<1 μV RMS)**.
- ✅ The **equivalent circuit** of a bioelectrode–skin–body system includes the half-cell potential, electrolyte resistance, and skin impedance in series.
- ✅ **Patient safety**: biomedical instruments must limit **leakage current to <10 μA** (Type CF, direct cardiac application) per IEC 60601.
- ✅ The **Nernst and GHK equations** are the *physical foundation* justifying why we can measure bioelectric signals at all — without ion gradients, there would be no ECG, no EEG, no nerve signals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Action Potential. Electrode Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Action Potential. Electrode Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Action Potential — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** An **Action Potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none electrical signal (a brief reversal of the membrane's electrical polarity) that propagates along the membrane of an excitable cell (neuron, muscle fibre) in response to a stimulus that exceeds the **threshold potential**, governed by the selective permeability of voltage-gated Sodium (Na⁺) and Potassium (K⁺) ion channels and the electrochemical gradients established by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**.

In biophysical terms, it is a self-propagating wave of **depolarization** that travels down the axon at velocities of 1–120 m/s, encoding information in its frequency rather than its amplitude (the all-or-none principle).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Balloon & Pin"

Imagine a stretched water balloon (the cell membrane) held under a thin rubber sheet that has tiny gated holes:

- **Resting state:** Tiny K⁺ "leak" channels keep the inside slightly negative (like air pressure pushing inward). This is the **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) = −70 mV**.
- **Stimulus arrives:** A mechanical pin (threshold stimulus) pushes the gate, popping the Na⁺ channels wide open. Positive Na⁺ ions rush in → inside becomes positive (**Depolarization**).
- **Auto shut-off:** Within a millisecond, Na⁺ gates slam shut, and K⁺ channels fling open. Positive K⁺ exits → inside becomes negative again (**Repolarization**).
- **Overshoot:** The system over-corrects, dipping briefly below −70 mV (**Hyperpolarization / After-potential**) before the pump restores equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard values students MUST memorize for KTU 2024 exams:
> - **RMP = −70 mV** (neuron)
> - **Threshold = −55 mV**
> - **Peak AP = +30 to +40 mV**
> - **Nernst Potential for K⁺ (E_K) ≈ −90 mV**
> - **Nernst Potential for Na⁺ (E_Na) ≈ +60 mV**
> - **Body Temperature reference = 310 K (37 °C)**

### 1.3 Electrode Theory — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Bioelectrode Theory** describes the electrochemical behaviour at the interface between a metal conductor and a biological electrolyte (tissue/skin). It governs the **half-cell potential (E₀)**, the **electrode-skin interface impedance (Z)**, the phenomenon of **polarization**, and the **equivalent circuit model** that determines the fidelity with which biopotential signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are transduced into measurable voltages.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Translator at a Multilingual Press Conference"

Picture a press conference where a doctor (the medical instrument) is trying to listen to a patient speaking in the body's "chemical language" (ionic currents). The **electrode** acts as a **translator** that converts these ionic currents into electronic signals the amplifier can read. However, every translator has a slight "accent" (the half-cell potential) and gets tired/distorted (polarization) when asked to translate too quickly. A good biomedical electrode minimizes this accent and distortion.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** — *"Understand the origins of bioelectric signals and the principles of biopotential electrodes used in medical instrumentation."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Sequential Phases of an Action Potential

A complete AP cycle consists of the following logical stages:

1. **Resting State (Phase 4):** The membrane is polarized at −70 mV. Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are **closed** (activation gate closed, inactivation gate open). K⁺ leak channels maintain the negative interior.
2. **Depolarization (Phase 0):** A stimulus raises the membrane potential to the **threshold (−55 mV)**. Na⁺ activation gates open rapidly → Na⁺ influx → membrane potential shoots up to **+30 mV**.
3. **Repolarization (Phase 1 & 2):** Na⁺ inactivation gates close (stopping Na⁺ influx). Voltage-gated K⁺ channels open slowly → K⁺ efflux → membrane potential falls back toward RMP.
4. **Hyperpolarization / Undershoot (Phase 3):** K⁺ channels close sluggishly; excess K⁺ efflux drives the potential briefly below RMP (≈ −80 mV).
5. **Return to Resting (Phase 4 again):** The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (using 1 ATP) actively transports 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in, restoring the ionic gradients.

> [!NOTE]
> **All-or-None Law:** Once the threshold is crossed, the AP fires at FULL amplitude regardless of stimulus strength. A stronger stimulus increases the **frequency** of APs, not the amplitude.

### 2.2 Refractory Periods — The Cell's "Cooling Time"

- **Absolute Refractory Period:** No new AP can be generated no matter how strong the stimulus (Na⁺ inactivation gates are closed).
- **Relative Refractory Period:** A new AP can be generated, but requires a stronger-than-normal stimulus (K⁺ channels are still open, requiring more depolarization).

### 2.3 Propagation of the Action Potential

The AP propagates along the axon in an **unidirectional** manner (downstream) because the region just behind the AP is in its **absolute refractory period**.

- **Continuous Propagation:** In unmyelinated axons (1–2 m/s).
- **Saltatory Conduction:** In myelinated axons, the AP "jumps" between **Nodes of Ranvier** (50–120 m/s) — the myelin sheath acts as an insulator, and voltage-gated channels are concentrated at the nodes.

### 2.4 Electrode–Electrolyte Interface — Core Concepts

1. **Half-Cell Potential (E₀):** When a metal (M) is immersed in its ion solution (M^n+), a potential difference develops at the interface due to the **tendency of metal atoms to ionize** and ions to deposit.
2. **Polarization:** When current flows, the half-cell potential changes, distorting the measurement.
   - **Perfectly Polarizable Electrode:** Current is carried by charging/discharging the double layer (capacitive behaviour). No actual charge crosses the interface. Example: **Platinum (Pt).**
   - **Perfectly Non-Polarizable Electrode:** Current freely crosses the interface via reversible electrochemical reactions, with NO change in half-cell potential. Example: **Ag/AgCl electrode.**
3. **Electrode–Skin Interface Impedance:** Composed of the **Epidermis (high impedance, ~10–100 kΩ·cm²)** in series with the **dermis (low impedance)**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \left( \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}} \right)$ | **Nernst Equation** — Equilibrium potential for a single ion | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}} \right)$ | Nernst equation simplified at 37 °C (310 K) | mV |
| 3 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right)$ | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** for RMP | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | **Ohm's Law for Ion Channels** | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $v = \lambda \cdot f$ | Action Potential propagation velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $Z = \frac{1}{j \omega C_d} + R_s + R_{ct}$ | **Electrode Impedance Model** (Double-layer cap + spreading + charge-transfer resistance) | Ohms (Ω) |
| 7 | $C_d = \varepsilon \frac{A}{d}$ | **Double-Layer Capacitance** at electrode interface | Farads (F) |
| 8 | $\tau = RC$ | **Time constant** of electrode-skin interface | seconds (s) |

> Constants: **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K), **F** = 96,485 C/mol (Faraday's constant), **T** = 310 K (body temperature), **z** = ion valence, **C_d** = double-layer capacitance, **R_ct** = charge-transfer resistance, **R_s** = spreading resistance, **ε** = permittivity of electrolyte, **d** = double-layer thickness, **A** = electrode area.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiac Pacemakers:** Use the all-or-none principle to time artificial depolarization.
- **ECG/EEG/EMG machines:** Rely on Ag/AgCl electrodes (non-polarizable) to faithfully transduce skin potentials.
- **Neural Prosthetics & Cochlear Implants:** Use **microelectrodes** (e.g., Michigan/Utah arrays) that must be small enough to record single-unit APs.
- **Defibrillators:** Exploit the absolute refractory period to depolarize the entire myocardium simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (for Action Potential)

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium membrane potential for a single permeable ion. The derivation follows directly from **electrochemical thermodynamics** — equating the electrical work done in moving one mole of ions across the membrane with the chemical work derived from the concentration gradient.

**Step 1: Electrical Work Done in Moving One Mole of Ions**

When a charge of **zF** coulombs (where z = ion valence, F = Faraday's constant) moves across a potential difference **E**, the electrical work performed is:

$$W_{elec} = zFE$$

**Step 2: Chemical Work (Free Energy of Diffusion)**

The free energy change when 1 mole of solute moves from concentration $[C]_1$ to $[C]_2$ is given by the logarithm of the concentration ratio. This is the **chemical work**:

$$W_{chem} = RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]_1}{[C]_2} \right)$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium Condition**

At electrochemical equilibrium, the net work done is **zero**. The electrical and chemical forces balance each other, so:

$$zFE + RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right) = 0$$

**Step 4: Solving for E**

Rearranging for the equilibrium potential E:

$$zFE = -RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right)$$

$$E = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right)$$

**Step 5: Converting Natural Log to Base-10 Log**

Using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E = -\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right)$$

**Step 6: Converting to SI Units (mV) at 37 °C (T = 310 K)**

Plugging in R = 8.314, T = 310, F = 96,485, the constant evaluates to:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

Therefore, the final **Nernst Equation in the KTU exam-friendly form** is:

$$E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right) \quad \text{(in mV)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

**Given:** $[K^+]_{inside} = 140 \text{ mM}$, $[K^+]_{outside} = 5 \text{ mM}$, $z = +1$, T = 37 °C.

**Solution:**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \left( \frac{5}{140} \right)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10} (0.03571)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4472)$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -89 \text{ mV} \approx -90 \text{ mV}}$$

This matches the standard textbook value, confirming our derivation.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for Na⁺

**Given:** $[Na^+]_{inside} = 15 \text{ mM}$, $[Na^+]_{outside} = 145 \text{ mM}$, $z = +1$, T = 37 °C.

**Solution:**

$$E_{Na} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \left( \frac{145}{15} \right)$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10} (9.667)$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times (0.9853)$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +60.6 \text{ mV} \approx +60 \text{ mV}}$$

---

### 3.4 Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation — Extended Derivation

The simple Nernst equation accounts for only ONE ion. But in reality, a real cell membrane is permeable to multiple ions simultaneously. The **GHK equation** combines Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻ contributions weighted by their relative permeabilities ($P_{ion}$).

The general form (without derivation, since it requires the constant-field assumption) is:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right)$$

At 37 °C, this becomes:

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right) \quad \text{(mV)}$$

**Standard Permeability Ratios (Squid Axon):**

$$\frac{P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl}}{1.0 : 0.04 : 0.45}$$

This equation accurately predicts the **Resting Membrane Potential of −70 mV** when these ratios are used.

---

### 3.5 Derivations for Electrode Half-Cell Potential

The **half-cell potential (E₀)** of a metal M in contact with its ion $M^{n+}$ at unit activity is defined by the **Nernst equation applied to the electrode reaction**:

$$M \rightleftharpoons M^{n+} + n e^-$$

Following the same equilibrium logic as before:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{[M^{n+}]}{[M]} \right)$$

Where:
- $E^0$ = Standard half-cell potential (at 1 M, 25 °C, 1 atm)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $[M] = 1$ (activity of pure solid metal)

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E = E^0 + \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10} \left( [M^{n+}] \right) \quad \text{(mV)}}$$

**Example: Standard potentials at 25 °C:**
- $Ag/AgCl$: $E^0 = +222$ mV
- $Cu/Cu^{2+}$: $E^0 = +337$ mV
- $Zn/Zn^{2+}$: $E^0 = -763$ mV

These half-cell potentials form the basis of the **EMG/ECG/EEG offset voltage** that instrumentation amplifiers must reject using **CMRR > 80 dB**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electrode Types

| Property | Ag/AgCl (Surface) | Pt (Microelectrode) | Stainless Steel (Needle) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polarizability** | Non-polarizable (best) | Polarizable (worst) | Partially polarizable |
| **Half-cell potential** | +222 mV (stable) | Variable | Variable |
| **DC offset drift** | Minimal (< 1 mV/hr) | High | Moderate |
| **Motion artifact** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Impedance at 1 Hz** | Low (~1–5 kΩ·cm²) | High (~100 kΩ) | Moderate |
| **Biocompatibility** | Excellent (long-term) | Excellent | Good |
| **Clinical use** | ECG, EEG, EMG | Neural recording, stimulation | EMG (single fibre) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Action Potential Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus Applied] --> B{Membrane Potential > -55 mV?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Voltage-Gated Na+ Channels Open]
    C --> D[Na+ Influx / Depolarization]
    D --> E[Peak at +30 mV]
    E --> F[Na+ Inactivation Gates Close]
    F --> G[Voltage-Gated K+ Channels Open]
    G --> H[K+ Efflux / Repolarization]
    H --> I[Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    I --> J[Na+/K+ ATPase Pump Restores Gradients]
    J --> K[Resting Potential -70 mV]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 Action Potential Phase Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 4: Resting -70 mV] --> P2[Phase 0: Depolarization to +30 mV]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 1: Peak Inversion]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 2: Repolarization]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 3: Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    P5 --> P1
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2 fill:#ffcccc
    style P4 fill:#ccffcc
    style P5 fill:#ffffcc
```

### 4.3 Electrode-Skin Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vs[Biopotential Source Vs] --> Re1[Epidermis Resistance Re]
    Re1 --> Ce1[Epidermis Capacitance Ce]
    Ce1 --> Rd[Rd Dermis Resistance]
    Rd --> E0[Half-Cell Potential E0]
    E0 --> Cdl[Double Layer Capacitance Cdl]
    Cdl --> Rct[Charge Transfer Resistance Rct]
    Rct --> Amp[Differential Amplifier]
```

### 4.4 Propagation Mechanism (Saltatory Conduction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node of Ranvier 1: AP Generated] -->|Myelin Insulator| N2[Node of Ranvier 2: AP Regenerated]
    N2 -->|Myelin Insulator| N3[Node of Ranvier 3: AP Regenerated]
    N3 -->|Myelin Insulator| N4[Node of Ranvier N: AP Regenerated]
    style N1 fill:#ff9999
    style N2 fill:#ff9999
    style N3 fill:#ff9999
    style N4 fill:#ff9999
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Biopotential Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Physiological Source] --> T[Transducer Biopotential Electrode]
    T --> A[Pre-Amplifier High CMRR]
    A --> F[Filter Band-Pass 0.05 to 1500 Hz]
    F --> O[Output Display / Recorder]
    style T fill:#ffcc99
    style A fill:#99ccff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define action potential. What is the threshold potential value for a typical neuron?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An action potential is a rapid, transient, all-or-none depolarization and repolarization of the membrane potential of an excitable cell, caused by the sequential opening of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels.
>
> **Threshold potential for a typical neuron = −55 mV.**
> Below this value, no action potential fires; once crossed, a full-amplitude AP is generated. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for threshold value, 1 mark for all-or-none mention)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between polarizable and non-polarizable electrodes with examples.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Polarizable | Non-Polarizable |
> |---|---|---|
> | Charge transfer | Capacitive (no actual charge transfer) | Faradaic (charge crosses interface) |
> | Half-cell potential stability | Changes with current | Remains stable |
> | Example | Platinum (Pt) | Ag/AgCl electrode |
>
> Non-polarizable electrodes are preferred for biopotential recording because they produce negligible polarization and lower motion artifacts. (1 mark per correct row + 1 mark for preference)

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium potential of a permeable ion across a cell membrane. Calculate the Nernst potential for K⁺ at 37 °C, given $[K^+]_{inside} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{outside} = 5$ mM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various phases of an action potential. Discuss the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Derivation (7 marks)**
>
> **Step 1 — Define Electrical Work (1 mark):**
> The electrical work done in moving 1 mole of ions across a potential E with charge zF is:
>
> $$W_{elec} = zFE$$
>
> **Step 2 — Define Chemical Work (1 mark):**
> The free-energy change due to concentration gradient is:
>
> $$W_{chem} = RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right)$$
>
> **Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition (1 mark):**
> At equilibrium, total work = 0:
>
> $$zFE + RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right) = 0$$
>
> **Step 4 — Solving for E (2 marks):**
>
> $$E = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right)$$
> $$E = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right)$$
>
> Substituting T = 310 K, R = 8.314, F = 96,485:
>
> $$E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}} \right) \text{ mV}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
>
> $$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{5}{140} \right) = 61.5 \times (-1.447) \approx -89 \text{ mV}$$
>
> The negative sign indicates the inside is negative relative to outside at equilibrium.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Phases of Action Potential (7 marks)**
>
> **Block Diagram (2 marks):**
> [Draw the 5-phase block diagram from Section 4.2]
>
> **Phase Explanation (4 marks):**
> - **Phase 4 (Resting, −70 mV):** Na⁺/K⁺ pump maintains ion gradients; K⁺ leak channels dominate. (1 mark)
> - **Phase 0 (Depolarization, → +30 mV):** Stimulus crosses threshold (−55 mV); Na⁺ channels open; rapid Na⁺ influx. (1 mark)
> - **Phase 1–2 (Repolarization):** Na⁺ inactivation gates close; K⁺ channels open; K⁺ efflux restores negative interior. (1 mark)
> - **Phase 3 (Hyperpolarization, −80 mV):** K⁺ channels close slowly; brief overshoot below RMP. (1 mark)
>
> **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Pump (1 mark):**
> Uses 1 ATP to pump 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in (electrogenic). Maintains long-term ionic gradients, accounting for ~ −4 mV of the resting potential.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the equivalent circuit model of the electrode-skin interface. What is polarization? Differentiate between perfectly polarizable and non-polarizable electrodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation, explain how the resting membrane potential is determined by multiple ion permeabilities. State the Nernst potentials for K⁺ and Na⁺ at 37 °C. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Electrode Theory (7 marks)**
>
> **Equivalent Circuit (3 marks):**
> The electrode-skin interface is modelled as:
> 1. $E_0$ — Half-cell potential (DC source)
> 2. $C_d$ — Double-layer capacitance (electrode-electrolyte interface)
> 3. $R_{ct}$ — Charge-transfer resistance (Faradaic path)
> 4. $R_s$ — Spreading resistance of the tissue
>
> These elements are connected in series/parallel as in the Section 4.3 block diagram.
>
> **Polarization (2 marks):**
> Polarization is the deviation of the electrode's half-cell potential from its equilibrium value when DC current flows. It is caused by accumulation of charge at the interface and concentration changes in the surrounding electrolyte.
>
> **Polarizable vs Non-Polarizable (2 marks):**
>
> | Property | Perfectly Polarizable | Perfectly Non-Polarizable |
> |---|---|---|
> | Charge transfer | None (only capacitive) | Reversible Faradaic |
> | Potential stability | Changes with current | Remains constant |
> | Example | Platinum | Ag/AgCl |
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation (7 marks)**
>
> **Equation (3 marks):**
>
> $$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right) \text{ mV}$$
>
> **Explanation (2 marks):**
> The GHK equation shows that the resting membrane potential is a weighted average of the individual Nernst potentials, weighted by the membrane's relative permeabilities to each ion. At rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na}$, so $V_m$ is close to $E_K$.
>
> **Nernst Potentials (2 marks):**
> - $E_K \approx -90$ mV
> - $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV
> - Resulting $V_m \approx -70$ mV (matches experimental RMP)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Nernst Equation:** Forgetting the negative sign OR placing concentrations in the wrong order (inside vs. outside). KTU 2024 examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this.
> 2. **Missing the Constant 61.5:** Writing the equation as $\frac{RT}{zF}$ instead of converting to log₁₀ and mV at 37 °C. The 61.5 mV constant is **mandatory** in the KTU answer key.
> 3. **Confusing Polarizable with Biocompatible:** Platinum is polarizable but biocompatible. Ag/AgCl is non-polarizable. Do NOT confuse these.
> 4. **Skipping the Pump:** The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase is required to explain the **long-term maintenance** of the gradient; without it, the diffusion gradients collapse within minutes.
> 5. **Threshold Confusion:** Threshold = **−55 mV**, NOT −70 mV. The −70 mV is the **resting** potential. Examiners note this as a **favourite trick question**.
> 6. **Forgetting to Convert to mV:** The GHK equation at body temperature gives the answer in mV only if the constant 61.5 is used.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Action Potential (AP):** Rapid, all-or-none, self-propagating depolarization-repolarization cycle.
- [x] **RMP = −70 mV**, **Threshold = −55 mV**, **Peak = +30 mV** — these three numbers are non-negotiable.
- [x] **Five Phases:** Resting → Depolarization (Na⁺ in) → Repolarization (K⁺ out) → Hyperpolarization → Recovery.
- [x] **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump:** 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, 1 ATP consumed; electrogenic (net negative charge efflux).
- [x] **All-or-None Law:** Amplitude is constant; frequency encodes stimulus strength.
- [x] **Refractory Periods:** Absolute (no AP possible) → Relative (AP possible with stronger stimulus).
- [x] **Saltatory Conduction:** AP "jumps" between Nodes of Ranvier in myelinated axons; faster.
- [x] **Nernst Equation:** $E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$ mV at 37 °C.
- [x] **E_K ≈ −90 mV, E_Na ≈ +60 mV, E_Cl ≈ −70 mV.**
- [x] **GHK Equation:** Weighted average of Nernst potentials; predicts RMP using permeability ratios.
- [x] **Half-Cell Potential (E₀):** Potential developed at metal-electrolyte interface; depends on metal and ion concentration.
- [x] **Polarizable Electrodes (Pt):** No charge transfer; unstable E₀ under DC current.
- [x] **Non-Polarizable Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Stable E₀; charge transfer via AgCl ⇌ Ag⁺ + Cl⁻; **preferred for clinical ECG/EEG/EMG.**
- [x] **Electrode-Skin Impedance:** Epidermis dominates at low frequencies (10–100 kΩ·cm²); gel electrolyte reduces it.
- [x] **Motion Artifact:** Caused by mechanical disturbance of the electrode-electrolyte double layer; minimized by Ag/AgCl.
- [x] **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Required to reject the high E₀ offset and amplify the small biopotential (typically < 5 mV).
- [x] **CMRR > 80 dB** is mandatory for clinical biopotential amplifiers to reject common-mode 50/60 Hz interference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nernst Equation and Various Types of Electrodes. Bioelectric Signals - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Nernst Equation and Various Types of Electrodes. Bioelectric Signals - ECG # 📘 MODULE 1: BIOELECTRIC SIGNALS, ELECTRODES & ECG ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Nernst Equation — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nernst Equation (KTU Syllabus Definition):** > The Nernst Equation is a fundamental electrochemical relationship that quantifies the **equilibrium reduction potential (E)** of a half-cell (electrode) relative to the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE), as a function of the **temperature**, the **number of transferred electrons**, and the **activities (concentrations)** of the oxidized and reduced species participating in the redox reaction. In plain words: when an electrode is dipped in a body fluid (electrolyte), the voltage it produces is not constant — it depends on **how many ions are floating around** and **how hot the solution is**. The Nernst Equation is the "rulebook" that tells us exactly what voltage to expect. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Concentration Pressure" Analogy Imagine a container split by a permeable membrane, with hot and cold water on each side. Heat (energy) naturally flows from hot → cold. Similarly, at an electrode–electrolyte interface, **ions want to flow** from a region of **high concentration** to a region of **low concentration** to achieve balance. This ion flow produces a tiny voltage. The Nernst Equation is essentially a "**thermodynamic pressure gauge**" that calculates this voltage. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):** > - **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C/mol** (charge per mole of electrons) > - **Universal Gas Constant (R) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - **Body Temperature (T) = 310 K** (37 °C) — frequently used in KTU numericals > - **Nernst Factor at 37°C = 61.5 mV** (per electron at body temperature) ### 1.3 Bioelectrodes — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bioelectrode (KTU Definition):** > A bioelectrode is a **transducer** that converts ionic bioelectric potentials (ion flow in body fluids) into measurable electronic potentials (electron flow in measurement instruments), thereby establishing a conductive interface between the biological tissue and the electronic measuring system. ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Translator" Analogy Think of your body speaking one "language" (ions flowing in salty fluid) and your oscilloscope speaking another (electrons in copper wires). A bioelectrode is the **interpreter** at the United Nations — it sits at the boundary, takes the ionic "speech" from the body, and translates it into an electronic signal the machine can read. Without it, the two systems would be **linguistically isolated**. ### 1.5 Bioelectric Signals — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bioelectric Signal:** > A bioelectric signal is a **time-varying electrical voltage or current** generated by the **physiological activity of excitable cells** (nerve, muscle, cardiac tissue) due to the movement of ions across semi-permeable cell membranes, producing action potentials that can be recorded non-invasively or invasively from the body surface. ### 1.6 ECG (Electrocardiogram) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** > The ECG is a graphical recording of the **electrical activity of the heart** over time, captured using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. It represents the sum of the action potentials of millions of cardiac myocytes during depolarization and repolarization cycles. ### 1.7 Intuitive Analogy — The "Concert Microphone" Analogy When a large orchestra plays, you don't record each violin individually — you place a few microphones in the hall to capture the **overall sound**. The ECG works the same way: the heart is the orchestra, the body surface electrodes are the microphones, and the ECG paper is the recording. The **P-QRS-T waveform** is the "audible signature" of the heart's electrical symphony. > [!NOTE] > **Common Bioelectric Signals Recorded in Practice:** > - **ECG** — Heart (Electrocardiography) > - **EEG** — Brain (Electroencephalography) > - **EMG** — Muscles (Electromyography) > - **EOG** — Eye movement (Electro-oculography) > - **ERG** — Retina (Electroretinography) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Nernst Equation — Operational Logic The Nernst equation is derived from the **thermodynamic principle** that at equilibrium, the change in Gibbs free energy of a redox reaction is zero. The electrode potential depends on three primary factors: 1. **Standard Electrode Potential (E⁰)** — The built-in "personality" of the electrode material (a constant tabulated in chemistry tables). 2. **Concentration Ratio ([Ox]/[Red])** — The "concentration pressure" driving ions across the interface. 3. **Temperature (T)** — Heat energy affects how vigorously ions move. ### 2.2 General Nernst Equation (Universal Form) $$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ Where: - **E** = Electrode potential at given conditions (V) - **E⁰** = Standard electrode potential (V) - **R** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **T** = Absolute temperature (K) - **n** = Number of electrons transferred in the half-reaction - **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - **Q** = Reaction quotient = activities of products / reactants ### 2.3 Nernst Equation for a Metal–Metal Ion Electrode For a general half-reaction: **M^(n+) + ne⁻ ⇌ M** $$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [M^{n+}]$$ For a general redox: **Ox + ne⁻ ⇌ Red** $$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$ ### 2.4 The "Log Base 10" Form (Most Used in KTU Numericals) Converting natural log to log base 10: **ln x = 2.303 log₁₀ x** $$E = E^{0} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$ ### 2.5 The "Nernst Slope" at Body Temperature (37°C / 310 K) Substituting T = 310 K into the coefficient: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ This gives the **clinical / biomedical** form of the Nernst equation used for bioelectrodes: $$E = E^{0} + \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]} \text{ (mV at 37°C)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nernst Slope / Nernst Factor at 37°C:** > - For **n = 1** (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻): slope = **61.5 mV/decade** > - For **n = 2** (e.g., Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺): slope = **30.77 mV/decade** > This is THE most tested value in KTU biomedical papers. ### 2.6 The Hydrogen Electrode Reference The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is defined to have E⁰ = 0.000 V at all temperatures, with **[H⁺] = 1 M** and **H₂ gas at 1 atm**. All other electrode potentials are measured against it. ### 2.7 Classification of Bioelectrodes (KTU Syllabus) > [!NOTE] > **Two Broad Classes of Bioelectrodes:** **(A) Perfectly Polarizable Electrodes:** - No actual charge crosses the electrode–electrolyte interface. - Current is carried by **capacitive charging/discharging** of the double layer. - Behaves like a pure capacitor. - Example: **Platinum (Pt) electrode** used in pacemakers. **(B) Perfectly Non-Polarizable Electrodes:** - Current freely crosses the interface with **no overpotential**. - Electrode potential remains stable regardless of current. - Example: **Ag/AgCl electrode** — the gold standard for ECG/EEG. **(C) Real (Imperfect) Electrodes** — the practical case; exhibit both polarizable and non-polarizable behaviour. ### 2.8 Common Types of Bioelectrodes (Detailed) | **Electrode Type** | **Construction** | **Application** | |---|---|---| | **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** | Ag wire coated with AgCl layer | ECG, EEG, EMG (surface) | | **Platinum (Pt)** | Pure Pt metal | Stimulation, pacemakers | | **Stainless Steel** | Alloy needle | Disposable EMG needles | | **Carbon / Graphite** | Carbon rod | Defibrillation, pH sensors | | **Calomel (Hg/Hg₂Cl₂)** | Mercury + mercurous chloride paste | Lab reference electrode | | **Microelectrodes (glass)** | Fine glass micropipette (tip < 1 µm) | Single-cell recording | | **Needle electrodes** | Fine metallic needle inserted into muscle | EMG, nerve conduction | ### 2.9 ECG Theory — The 12-Lead System > [!IMPORTANT] > **ECG Lead = A specific "view" of the heart's electrical activity from a pair of electrodes (vector).** The standard clinical ECG uses **12 leads** divided into: 1. **3 Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven):** Lead I, Lead II, Lead III 2. **3 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger):** aVR, aVL, aVF 3. **6 Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson):** V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 ### 2.10 Einthoven's Triangle A virtual equilateral triangle formed by placing electrodes on: - **Right Arm (RA)** - **Left Arm (LA)** - **Left Leg (LL)** The heart is assumed to lie at the **electrical center** of this triangle. The Einthoven relations are: $$\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$$ $$V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$$ ### 2.11 ECG Waveform Components (Must Memorize) | **Wave/Segment** | **Physiological Meaning** | **Typical Duration** | **Typical Amplitude** | |---|---|---|---| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | 0.06 – 0.11 s | ≤ 0.25 mV | | **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay | 0.12 – 0.20 s | — | | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 0.06 – 0.10 s | 1.0 – 1.5 mV | | **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (all cells depolarized) | 0.10 – 0.12 s | Isoelectric | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 0.16 s | 0.1 – 0.5 mV | | **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity | 0.36 – 0.44 s | — | | **U wave** | Late repolarization of Purkinje fibers | 0.04 s | < 0.1 mV | ### 2.12 Engineering Utility - **Nernst Equation**: Foundation of all ion-selective sensors, pH meters, blood gas analyzers, and neural microelectrode design. - **Bioelectrodes**: The gateway hardware for every electrophysiological diagnostic device (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG). - **ECG**: The single most-used diagnostic test in cardiology; non-invasive, cheap, and provides vital data on arrhythmias, ischemia, and myocardial infarction. ## 2.13 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table) | **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Used For** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = E^{0} + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Universal Nernst equation | Volts | Any redox half-cell | | 2 | $E = E^{0} + \frac{61.5}{n} \log \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Biomedical Nernst at 37°C | mV | Bioelectrodes, ion sensors | | 3 | $\text{Nernst Slope} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}$ | Sensitivity per decade | mV | Sensor calibration | | 4 | $\Delta E = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[C_1]}{[C_2]}$ | Concentration cell potential | V | pH glass electrode | | 5 | $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ | Einthoven's law | mV | ECG lead math | | 6 | $\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{1500}{\text{RR in small boxes}}$ | Quick HR from ECG paper | bpm | ECG paper analysis | | 7 | $\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{60}{\text{RR in seconds}}$ | Standard HR formula | bpm | ECG analysis | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation ### Step 1 — Start with Gibbs Free Energy For any electrochemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure, the change in Gibbs free energy is related to the electrical work done: $$\Delta G = -nFE$$ Where: - **ΔG** = change in Gibbs free energy (J) - **n** = number of moles of electrons transferred - **F** = Faraday's constant (C/mol) - **E** = cell potential (V) ### Step 2 — Relate to Standard Conditions Under standard conditions (1 M, 1 atm, 25°C): $$\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$$ ### Step 3 — Use the Thermodynamic Relation for Non-Standard Conditions The change in Gibbs free energy under non-standard conditions is given by: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln Q$$ Where **Q** is the reaction quotient (ratio of product activities to reactant activities). ### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify Substituting the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3: $$-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln Q$$ Dividing both sides by **−nF**: $$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ This is the **Nernst Equation** in its natural log form. ### Step 5 — Convert to Common Logarithm (Base 10) Using the identity: $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$ $$E = E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$ For a generic redox: **Ox + ne⁻ → Red**, the reaction quotient is $Q = \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$, so: $$E = E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$$ Or equivalently (the most commonly used form): $$E = E^{0} + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$ ### Step 6 — Evaluate the Nernst Constant at 37°C (Body Temperature) At T = 310 K, the constant evaluates to: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485}$$ Breaking this down: $$\begin{aligned} 2.303 \times 8.314 &= 19.147 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\ 19.147 \times 310 &= 5935.6 \text{ J/mol} \\ \frac{5935.6}{96485} &= 0.06153 \text{ V} = 61.53 \text{ mV} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore, the **biomedical Nernst equation** simplifies to: $$E = E^{0} + \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]} \text{ (mV at 37°C)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - If **[Ox] > [Red]**: log term is positive → E becomes more positive (oxidation favored). > - If **[Ox] < [Red]**: log term is negative → E becomes more negative (reduction favored). > - If **[Ox] = [Red]**: E = E⁰ (standard condition). ## 3.2 Worked Example — Concentration Cell Potential **Problem:** A silver electrode is placed in a 0.1 M AgNO₃ solution, and a reference Ag/AgCl electrode is in 0.01 M AgNO₃. Both at 25°C. E⁰(Ag⁺/Ag) = +0.799 V. Calculate the cell potential. **Solution (Step-by-Step):** Step 1 — Identify given values: - $[Ag^{+}]_{\text{anode}} = 0.1$ M, $[Ag^{+}]_{\text{cathode}} = 0.01$ M - n = 1, T = 298 K, E⁰ = +0.799 V Step 2 — Apply the Nernst equation to each half-cell: $$E_{\text{anode}} = 0.799 + \frac{0.0591}{1} \log(0.1) = 0.799 - 0.0591 = 0.7399 \text{ V}$$ $$E_{\text{cathode}} = 0.799 + \frac{0.0591}{1} \log(0.01) = 0.799 - 0.1182 = 0.6808 \text{ V}$$ Step 3 — Compute the cell potential: $$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = 0.6808 - 0.7399 = -0.0591 \text{ V}$$ The negative sign indicates the assumed direction is reversed. ## 3.3 ECG Vector Analysis Using Einthoven's Law **Problem:** A patient's ECG shows: Lead I = +0.7 mV, Lead II = +1.0 mV. Calculate Lead III. **Solution:** Using Einthoven's law: $$V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$$ $$1.0 = 0.7 + V_{III}$$ $$V_{III} = 1.0 - 0.7 = +0.3 \text{ mV}$$ Lead III = +0.3 mV. > [!TIP] > **For KTU exams: Always remember to mention the lead polarity sign convention:** > - Lead I = LA − RA > - Lead II = LL − RA > - Lead III = LL − LA ## 3.4 Heart Rate Calculation from ECG **Problem:** The R–R interval on an ECG paper (standard speed 25 mm/s) is measured as 20 small boxes (each small box = 0.04 s). Calculate the heart rate. **Solution:** $$\text{RR duration} = 20 \times 0.04 = 0.80 \text{ s}$$ $$\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{60}{0.80} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$ Or using the shortcut: $$\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **ECG Paper Standard (MUST know for KTU):** > - Paper speed: **25 mm/s** > - 1 small box = **1 mm = 0.04 s = 0.1 mV** (vertically) > - 1 large box = **5 mm = 0.20 s = 0.5 mV** ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Selection Guide | **Application** | **Best Electrode** | **Reason** | |---|---|---| | Routine surface ECG (resting) | Ag/AgCl | Low noise, stable half-cell potential | | Long-term Holter monitoring | Ag/AgCl gel | Skin-friendly, low polarization | | Pacemaker stimulation | Pt-Ir alloy | Polarizable, biostable | | Single neuron recording | Glass micropipette | Sub-micron tip, high impedance | | Defibrillation paddles | Stainless steel | High current capacity | | Reference electrode in lab | Calomel (Hg/Hg₂Cl₂) | Very stable E⁰ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete ECG Acquisition System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HEART
Electrical Source] --> B[BODY TISSUE
Volume Conductor] B --> C[SURFACE ELECTRODES
Ag/AgCl x 10] C --> D[LEAD NETWORK
Einthoven / Wilson] D --> E[FRONT-END AMPLIFIER
Gain = 1000] E --> F[FILTER STAGE
0.5 Hz - 150 Hz] F --> G[ADC
12-bit, 500 Hz] G --> H[MICROCONTROLLER
DSP] H --> I[DISPLAY / RECORDER
ECG Paper / LCD] H --> J[ALGORITHM
HR / Arrhythmia Detect] J --> K[ALARM / PRINTER] style A fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF style C fill:#4ECDC4,color:#FFFFFF style E fill:#FFD93D,color:#000000 style I fill:#95E1D3,color:#000000 style K fill:#F38181,color:#FFFFFF ``` **Description:** The heart generates ionic currents that travel through body tissue (volume conductor) to the skin surface. Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes transduce ionic potentials into electronic voltages. The lead network (Einthoven's triangle) combines signals from specific electrode pairs. The signal is amplified (typically 1000×), filtered (to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise), digitized, processed by a DSP algorithm, and finally displayed on an LCD or printed on thermal ECG paper. ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Bioelectrode Interface with Skin ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INNER BODY
Ionic Domain] --> B[ELECTRODE-ELECTROLYTE
INTERFACE
Half-Cell Potential EH] B --> C[ELECTRODE METAL
Conductor] C --> D[LEAD WIRE
to Amplifier] D --> E[AMPLIFIER
High Impedance] F[SKIN-ES JUNCTION] --> A G[SWEAT + ELECTRODE GEL] --> B style A fill:#A8E6CF,color:#000000 style B fill:#FF8B94,color:#FFFFFF style E fill:#FFD93D,color:#000000 ``` **Description:** This is the critical interface where ionic conduction in the body must be converted to electronic conduction in the wire. The **half-cell potential (E_H)** arises at the electrode–electrolyte interface and is described by the Nernst equation. A high-input-impedance amplifier (typically > 10 MΩ) is required to avoid loading this interface and distorting the signal. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — ECG Waveform Components (Time–Amplitude Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ECG_WAVEFORM[ECG COMPLEX TIMELINE] A1[BASELINE] --> A2[P WAVE
Atrial Depolarization] A2 --> A3[PR SEGMENT
AV Nodal Delay] A3 --> A4[Q WAVE
Septal Depolarization] A4 --> A5[R WAVE
Main Ventricular Depolarization] A5 --> A6[S WAVE
Late Ventricular Depolarization] A6 --> A7[ST SEGMENT
Ventricular Plateau] A7 --> A8[T WAVE
Ventricular Repolarization] A8 --> A9[U WAVE
Purkinje Repolarization] A9 --> A10[BASELINE] end style A2 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF style A5 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#000000 style A8 fill:#FFD93D,color:#000000 ``` **Description:** Sequential time-axis representation of one cardiac cycle as visible on a standard ECG tracing. The P wave is small (atria), QRS is tall and sharp (ventricles), and T wave is rounded (recovery). The whole cycle spans roughly 0.8 seconds at 75 bpm. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Einthoven's Triangle and 12-Lead Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD RA[RA
Right Arm] -->|Lead I
LA minus RA| LA[LA
Left Arm] LA -->|Lead III
LL minus LA| LL[LL
Left Leg] RA -->|Lead II
LL minus RA| LL LL -.->|aVF| V1[Augmented Leads
aVR aVL aVF] RA -.->|aVR| V1 LA -.->|aVL| V1 V1 -->|Ref Electrode| V2[Wilson Central Terminal] V2 -->|V1 to V6| V3[Precordial Leads
Chest] V3 --> V4[V1 V2 V3 V4 V5 V6] style RA fill:#95E1D3,color:#000000 style LA fill:#95E1D3,color:#000000 style LL fill:#95E1D3,color:#000000 style V3 fill:#FF8B94,color:#FFFFFF ``` **Description:** Einthoven's triangle is formed by the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL). The three bipolar leads (I, II, III) are the edges of this triangle. The augmented leads (aVR, aVL, aVF) are derived from the same electrodes using Goldberger's network. The 6 precordial leads (V1–V6) are unipolar chest leads referenced to Wilson's Central Terminal. ## 4.5 Mermaid Decision Tree — Electrode Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD S[SELECT ELECTRODE] --> Q1{Measurement Type?} Q1 -->|Surface| Q2{Signal Frequency?} Q1 -->|Invasive| Q3{Target Tissue?} Q1 -->|Stimulation| Q4{Stimulus Type?} Q2 -->|Low ECG EEG| R1[Ag or Ag-AgCl] Q2 -->|High EMG| R2[Stainless Steel] Q3 -->|Single cell| R3[Glass Micropipette] Q3 -->|Muscle fiber| R4[Needle Electrode] Q4 -->|DC Pacemaker| R5[Pt-Ir Alloy] Q4 -->|Defibrillation| R6[Stainless Steel Paddles] R1 --> X[APPLY ELECTRODE GEL] R2 --> X R3 --> Y[FILL WITH ELECTROLYTE] R4 --> X style R1 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#FFFFFF style R5 fill:#FFD93D,color:#000000 ``` **Description:** A simplified engineering decision tree for selecting the appropriate bioelectrode for a given biomedical application. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Nernst Equation and explain its significance in biomedical instrumentation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Nernst Equation relates the equilibrium electrode potential of a half-cell to the standard electrode potential, temperature, and ionic concentrations: $$E = E^{0} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$ **Significance in Biomedical Instrumentation (2 marks):** 1. **Foundation of bioelectrode theory** — explains how ionic potentials in body fluids are converted to measurable voltages at electrode–electrolyte interfaces. 2. **Basis of ion-selective sensors** — pH electrodes, blood gas analyzers, and ion sensors (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) all use the Nernst equation for calibration. 3. **Determines sensor sensitivity** — the Nernst slope (61.5 mV/decade at 37°C for n=1) defines the minimum detectable ion concentration change. 4. **Critical for ECG/EEG/EMG electrode design** — understanding polarization and half-cell potentials (e.g., Ag/AgCl) requires the Nernst equation. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List the components of a normal ECG waveform and state the physiological origin of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Component** | **Physiological Origin** | **Duration** | |---|---|---| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (SA node firing) | 0.06–0.11 s | | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 0.06–0.10 s | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 0.16 s | | **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay | 0.12–0.20 s | | **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (all cells depolarized) | 0.10–0.12 s | | **QT interval** | Total ventricular electrical activity | 0.36–0.44 s | | **U wave** | Repolarization of Purkinje fibers | 0.04 s | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the Nernst Equation from thermodynamic principles. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an Ag/AgCl bioelectrode. Discuss why it is preferred for ECG recording. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a) — Derivation of the Nernst Equation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy of an Electrochemical Cell:** For a reversible electrochemical reaction, the electrical work done is: $$W_{elec} = -nFE$$ Therefore, the change in Gibbs free energy: $$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2 — Under standard conditions:** $$\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 3 — Non-standard Gibbs free energy:** $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln Q \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute (1) and (2) into (3):** $$-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 5 — Divide both sides by −nF:** $$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q \quad \text{...(Nernst Equation)}$$ **Step 6 — Convert ln to log₁₀:** $$E = E^{0} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$ For a generic redox couple: **Ox + ne⁻ → Red**, $Q = \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$, so: $$E = E^{0} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$ **Step 7 — Evaluate at body temperature (37°C = 310 K):** $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ Biomedical Nernst equation: $$E = E^{0} + \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]} \text{ mV}$$ **Significance:** This equation forms the basis of all bioelectrode design and ion sensor calibration in biomedical instrumentation. --- #### **Model Solution to (b) — Ag/AgCl Electrode (7 marks)** **Construction (3 marks):** - A **silver (Ag) wire** forms the metallic core. - The wire is **electrolytically coated** with a thin layer of **silver chloride (AgCl)** by electrolysis in HCl solution. - The electrode is immersed in a **potassium chloride (KCl) electrolyte** gel held against the skin by an adhesive pad. - A lead wire connects the Ag core to the measuring instrument. **Diagram (in Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Measuring Instrument] --> B[Ag Wire Conductor] B --> C[AgCl Layer
Coating] C --> D[KCl Electrolyte Gel] D --> E[Skin Surface] E --> F[Body Tissue
Ionic Domain] style B fill:#C0C0C0,color:#000000 style C fill:#D3D3D3,color:#000000 style D fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000000 style E fill:#FFCC99,color:#000000 ``` **Working (2 marks):** The half-reaction at the electrode is: $$AgCl_{(s)} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Ag_{(s)} + Cl^{-}_{(aq)}$$ The half-cell potential depends on Cl⁻ ion activity: $$E = E^{0}(AgCl/Ag) - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log [Cl^{-}]$$ For a saturated KCl solution, [Cl⁻] is constant, producing a **stable reference potential of +0.222 V vs SHE**. **Why Preferred for ECG (2 marks):** 1. **Low polarization** — behaves almost as a perfect non-polarizable electrode, minimizing motion artifacts. 2. **Stable half-cell potential** — does not drift with small current flow, essential for low-frequency ECG signals (0.5–150 Hz). 3. **Low noise** — DC offset is steady; high signal-to-noise ratio for mV-level ECG signals. 4. **Non-toxic and skin-friendly** — safe for long-term Holter monitoring. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the origin of bioelectric potentials with reference to the Nernst equation. Discuss resting and action potentials. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the standard 12-lead ECG system with a block diagram of the ECG machine. Explain the significance of each lead. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a) — Bioelectric Potentials (7 marks)** **Origin of Bioelectric Potentials (3 marks):** All bioelectric potentials arise from the **selective permeability of cell membranes to ions** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) and the resulting **concentration gradients** across the membrane. The Nernst equation describes the equilibrium potential for a single ion: $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}$$ For a cell with multiple permeant ions, the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** combines them. The difference between the intracellular and extracellular potentials is the **membrane potential**. **Resting Potential (2 marks):** - At rest, the membrane is **selectively permeable to K⁺** (K⁺ channels open). - K⁺ diffuses out of the cell down its concentration gradient. - The Nernst potential for K⁺ at typical body concentrations: $E_{K} \approx -90$ mV. - The **resting membrane potential** ≈ **−70 mV** (inside negative relative to outside). - Maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in). **Action Potential (2 marks):** - **Phase 0 — Depolarization:** Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → Na⁺ rushes in → membrane potential rises to +30 mV (Nernst potential for Na⁺). - **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization:** Na⁺ channels inactivate; transient K⁺ efflux. - **Phase 2 — Plateau:** Ca²⁺ channels open (in cardiac cells); balances K⁺ efflux. - **Phase 3 — Repolarization:** K⁺ channels fully open; membrane returns to −70 mV. - **Phase 4 — Resting:** Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores ionic gradients. The summed action potentials of millions of cells are what the ECG records at the body surface. --- #### **Model Solution to (b) — 12-Lead ECG System (7 marks)** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[10 ELECTRODES
4 Limbs + 6 Chest] --> B[LEAD SELECTION NETWORK
12 Leads] B --> C[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER
CMRR 100 dB] C --> D[BANDPASS FILTER
0.5 to 150 Hz] D --> E[50 Hz NOTCH FILTER] E --> F[ADC
12-bit 1 kHz] F --> G[MCU / DSP
QRS Detection] G --> H[DISPLAY
LCD / Thermal Printer] G --> I[STORAGE
SD Card / Memory] style C fill:#FFD93D,color:#000000 style H fill:#95E1D3,color:#000000 ``` **12-Lead Significance (4 marks):** | **Lead Group** | **Leads** | **Significance** | |---|---|---| | **Bipolar Limb (Einthoven)** | I, II, III | Measure potential difference between two limb electrodes; form Einthoven's triangle | | **Augmented Limb (Goldberger)** | aVR, aVL, aVF | Magnify unipolar limb signals by 1.5×; view heart in frontal plane | | **Precordial (Wilson)** | V1 – V6 | Unipolar chest leads across the precordium; view heart in horizontal plane | - **Lead I** (LA − RA): Views the **lateral wall** of the left ventricle. - **Lead II** (LL − RA): Best for detecting **P waves** and rhythm analysis; parallel to cardiac axis. - **Lead III** (LL − LA): Views the **inferior wall**. - **aVR**: Views the heart from the **right shoulder** (cavity view). - **aVL**: Views the **high lateral wall**. - **aVF**: Views the **inferior wall**. - **V1–V2**: **Septal** view (right ventricle). - **V3–V4**: **Anterior wall** of left ventricle. - **V5–V6**: **Lateral wall** of left ventricle. The 12 leads together provide a **three-dimensional electrical map** of the heart. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the sign convention in the Nernst Equation** — students often flip the log ratio. **Memorize: It is [Oxidized]/[Reduced] in the numerator, NOT the other way.** > > 2. **Using 25°C (298 K) instead of 37°C (310 K) in biomedical Nernst calculations** — KTU biomedical questions ALWAYS use 37°C body temperature. The correct Nernst factor is **61.5 mV**, not 59.1 mV. > > 3. **Confusing "polarizable" and "non-polarizable" electrodes** — Pt electrodes are polarizable (used for stimulation); Ag/AgCl is non-polarizable (used for recording). > > 4. **Mis-assigning ECG waveform components** — QRS is ventricular, NOT atrial. P wave is atrial, NOT ventricular.
