The T wave is **repolarization**, not depolarization. > > 5. **Drawing Einthoven's triangle as a non-equilateral triangle** — it MUST be drawn as equilateral, with the heart at the electrical center. > > 6. **Skipping units in derivations** — KTU examiners explicitly allocate 1 mark for correct units in numerical problems. > > 7. **Writing the Nernst equation with n=1 when the question is about Ca²⁺ or Mg²⁺** — for divalent ions, **n=2**, and the slope is **30.77 mV/decade** at 37°C. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Nernst Equation** is the foundational equation for all bioelectrode analysis. Universal form uses natural log; the **clinical / biomedical form at 37°C** uses log₁₀ with **61.5/n mV** as the coefficient. - **Nernst Slope = 61.5 mV/decade (n=1)** at body temperature — memorize this number. - **Faraday Constant F = 96,485 C/mol**; **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**. - **Three electrode classes**: perfectly polarizable (Pt), perfectly non-polarizable (Ag/AgCl), and real (imperfect). - **Ag/AgCl is the GOLD STANDARD for ECG, EEG, EMG** — stable E_H, low noise, low polarization. - **Calomel (Hg/Hg₂Cl₂)** is a lab reference electrode, NOT used on patients (mercury toxicity). - **ECG Lead I = LA − RA; Lead II = LL − RA; Lead III = LL − LA** — sign convention is crucial. - **Einthoven's Law: V_II = V_I + V_III** — must be stated explicitly in derivations. - **12 ECG leads = 3 bipolar + 3 augmented + 6 precordial**. - **P wave = Atrial depolarization** (~0.1 s, <0.25 mV). - **QRS complex = Ventricular depolarization** (~0.08 s, ~1 mV). - **T wave = Ventricular repolarization** (~0.16 s, ~0.3 mV). - **PR interval (0.12–0.20 s)** measures AV node conduction. - **QT interval (0.36–0.44 s)** measures total ventricular activity. - **ECG paper speed: 25 mm/s; 1 small box = 0.04 s = 0.1 mV; 1 large box = 0.20 s = 0.5 mV**. - **Heart Rate = 1500 / (number of small boxes between R–R peaks)** — quick KTU-friendly formula. - **Resting membrane potential ≈ −70 mV** (K⁺ dominance, Nernst-K ≈ −90 mV). - **Action potential peak ≈ +30 mV** (Na⁺ dominance, Nernst-Na ≈ +60 mV). - **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** is the multi-ion generalization of the Nernst equation for living cells. - **Polarizable electrodes** behave like capacitors; **non-polarizable** behave like resistors — important for choosing between recording and stimulation electrodes. - **Microelectrodes** (glass micropipettes) have tips <1 µm and impedances >10 MΩ; used for **single-cell recording**. - **Skin-electrode interface** introduces half-cell potential E_H, which must be **balanced out** by the instrumentation amplifier (typically using a **right-leg driver circuit** in ECG). - **CMRR of ECG amplifier should be > 80 dB** (ideally 100 dB) to reject 50 Hz mains interference.

topics.title: EMG and ERG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMG and ERG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electromyography (EMG) — Definition

**Electromyography (EMG)** is the bioelectrical measurement technique used to record, monitor, and analyze the **electrical action potentials** generated by skeletal muscle fibers during voluntary or electrically-stimulated contraction. The signal obtained is termed the **Electromyogram (also EMG)**, and it represents the spatiotemporal summation of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) detected by surface or needle electrodes placed on/within the muscle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> *EMG is the recording of the bioelectric activity of a muscle, while the instrument used is called an Electromyograph. The signal reflects the depolarization–repolarization cycle of excitable muscle cell membranes in response to neural stimulation.*

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Microphone Inside Your Muscle"

Imagine a giant sports stadium full of fans. Each fan is a **muscle fiber**, and each time a fiber fires (a brief "yell" of electrical activity), it produces a tiny burst of energy. A single fiber is inaudible, but when thousands fire together, the combined roar can be recorded by a microphone. The **EMG electrode is the microphone**, and the **muscle action potential is the cheer**. By analyzing the loudness (amplitude), frequency (rate of firing), and synchronization (recruitment), a physician can tell whether the muscle is healthy, weak, fatigued, or diseased — exactly the way a sound engineer can characterize a cheering crowd.

### 1.1.2 Types of EMG Recording

| Parameter | Surface EMG (SEMG) | Needle/Intramuscular EMG |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrode placement** | On skin over muscle | Inserted into muscle belly |
| **Pickup area** | Wide (multiple motor units) | Localized (1–10 fibers) |
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Minimally invasive |
| **Bandwidth** | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** | **20 Hz – 5 kHz** |
| **Amplitude** | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **100 µV – 10 mV** |
| **Use** | Kinesiology, ergonomics, rehab | Clinical neurophysiology |

---

## 1.2 Electroretinography (ERG) — Definition

**Electroretinography (ERG)** is the diagnostic ophthalmic technique that measures the **electrical response of the retina** to a controlled light stimulus. The recorded waveform, the **electroretinogram**, is generated by the cumulative activity of retinal neurons (photoreceptors, bipolar cells, Müller glia, ganglion cells) and reflects the integrity of the phototransduction cascade and post-receptoral processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> *ERG is the recording of the bioelectric potential changes occurring in the retina in response to a flash of light. It is a corneal/contact-lens-recorded mass response of the retina and forms an objective test of retinal function.*

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Solar-Panel Test"

Picture a **field of solar panels** wired in series. When the sun (light stimulus) suddenly strikes the field, each panel generates a small voltage. A master voltmeter at the panel bank terminals measures the **summed electrical response** — even though we cannot probe every individual panel. If one row of panels is damaged, the master reading falls. The retina behaves identically: light hits the photoreceptors, and the **corneal electrode** measures the **algebraic sum of all retinal currents**. Diseases (retinitis pigmentosa, cone-rod dystrophy, diabetic retinopathy) cause characteristic drops or shape changes, just as damaged panels would.

### 1.2.2 Standard Clinical ERG Protocol (ISCEV)

The **International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision (ISCEV)** defines a standardized 6-condition ERG:

| Condition | Adaptation | Stimulus | Component evaluated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dark-adapted 0.01 (rod) | 20 min dark | 0.01 cd·s·m⁻² | Rod system |
| Dark-adapted 3.0 (combined rod–cone) | 20 min dark | 3.0 cd·s·m⁻² (white) | Combined response |
| Dark-adapted 3.0 oscillatory potentials | — | — | Inner retinal activity |
| Dark-adapted 10.0 (strong flash) | 20 min dark | 10 cd·s·m⁻² | a-wave (photoreceptors) |
| Light-adapted 3.0 (cone) | 10 min light | 3.0 cd·s·m⁻² (white, 30 Hz) | Cone system |
| Light-adapted 3.0 flicker (30 Hz) | 10 min light | 3.0 cd·s·m⁻² (30 Hz) | Cone pathway |

---

## 1.3 Clinical & Engineering Significance

- **EMG** aids in the diagnosis of **myopathies** (muscle disorders), **neuropathies** (peripheral nerve disorders), **ALS**, **carpal tunnel syndrome**, and is fundamental to **prosthetic limb control** and **biofeedback rehab**.
- **ERG** is the gold-standard **objective** test for inherited retinal dystrophies (e.g., **retinitis pigmentosa**, **Leber congenital amaurosis**), and is used in drug-toxicity screening (e.g., **hydroxychloroquine retinopathy**).

> [!NOTE]
> Both EMG and ERG are **microvolt-level bioelectric signals** corrupted by 50/60 Hz mains interference, motion artefacts, and baseline drift. They therefore demand **high-CMRR differential amplification**, **active shielding**, and **notch filtering** — topics integrally tied to the Module 1 syllabus on biopotential electrodes and amplifiers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) — Origin of EMG

A **motor unit (MU)** is defined as one **alpha motor neuron** + **all the muscle fibers it innervates**. The MUAP is the spatially weighted sum of all single-fiber action potentials (SFAPs) of the fibers belonging to one motor unit. The surface-detected EMG is the **algebraic sum of many MUAPs** firing asynchronously, multiplied by the recruitment pattern and firing-rate statistics of the active pool of motor neurons.

### 2.1.1 Decomposition into Physiological Stages

1. **Central command** (cortex → corticospinal tract) issues a voluntary drive.
2. **Alpha motor neuron** in the spinal cord anterior horn fires at rate $f_i$ (typically 6–25 Hz).
3. **Neuromuscular junction** transmits an end-plate potential, triggering an SFAP in each muscle fiber.
4. **SFAP propagation** along the muscle fiber membrane at conduction velocity $v$ (typically 2–6 m/s).
5. **Volume conduction** through tissue of conductivity $\sigma$ carries the potential to the electrode.
6. **Electrode–skin interface** transduces ionic current to electronic voltage with transfer impedance $Z_e$.
7. **Amplifier chain** provides high CMRR, gain, and band-pass filtering.
8. **Display / DSP** computes RMS, mean frequency, or median frequency for clinical interpretation.

### 2.1.2 Why EMG Frequency Content is Limited

The **Fourier content of a single SFAP** depends on the rise-time $\tau_r$ and propagation velocity $v$. The dominant spectral peak occurs around:

$$
f_{peak} \approx \frac{0.8 \, v}{\pi \, r_{fiber}}
$$

For typical fiber radius $r_{fiber} \approx 40 \,\mu m$ and $v \approx 4 \, m/s$:

$$
f_{peak} \approx \frac{0.8 \times 4}{\pi \times 40 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 25.5 \text{ kHz (single fiber)}
$$

However, due to **summation, cancellation, and tissue low-pass filtering**, the usable bandwidth of the surface EMG is reduced to **20 Hz – 500 Hz**, a critical specification for filter design.

---

## 2.2 Retinal Origin of the ERG

Light hyperpolarizes photoreceptors (rods and cones), reducing the release of glutamate at their synapses. This cascade generates the standard ERG waveform, whose components are generated by specific cell types:

| Wave | Origin | Peak latency (ms) | Sign (cornea +) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **a-wave** | Photoreceptor (rod/cone) hyperpolarization | 5–15 | Negative |
| **b-wave** | ON-bipolar cells + Müller glial K⁺ current | 30–70 | Positive |
| **c-wave** | RPE (retinal pigment epithelium) | 200–400 | Positive |
| **d-wave** | OFF-bipolar / OFF-cone response (long flash) | ~80–150 | Positive |
| **OPs** | Inner retina (amacrine) | 100–150 | High-frequency oscillations superimposed on b-wave |
| **Scotopic threshold response (STR)** | Rod ON-bipolar / AII amacrine | 100–120 | Positive |

> [!NOTE]
> The **cornea is referenced as positive**. By convention, an upward deflection means the cornea becomes more positive relative to the reference (forehead/earlobe).

---

## 2.3 Instrumentation Block Diagram (Unified)

Although EMG and ERG measure different physiological events, their **front-end electronics are architecturally identical**. The textbook (Khandpur-style) standard block chain is:

**Electrodes → Differential Pre-amplifier (INA-grade) → Isolation amplifier (patient safety) → Band-pass filter (BPF) → 50/60 Hz notch filter → Main amplifier (variable gain) → Rectifier → Smoother/Integrator → Display (CRO/PC/DAQ)**

### 2.3.1 Stage-by-Stage Justification

- **Differential pre-amp** (gain $A_1 \approx 1000$, CMRR ≥ **100 dB** at 50/60 Hz) rejects common-mode line noise.
- **Isolation amplifier** (ADuM-series, transformer-coupled, $\geq$ 5 kV isolation) provides patient safety and breaks the ground loop.
- **Band-pass filter** sets the bioelectric bandwidth: EMG (20 Hz – 500 Hz), ERG (0.1 Hz – 300 Hz for flash ERG, DC–3000 Hz for pattern ERG).
- **Notch filter** at 50/60 Hz with Q ≈ 5 to suppress mains hum.
- **Rectifier + Integrator** produces the **envelope** of the EMG (used in biofeedback and prosthetic control).
- **Display/DAQ** records the waveform with at least 12-bit resolution at sampling rate ≥ 2 × highest frequency in the signal (Nyquist).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (EMG & ERG)

| **#** | **Quantity / Equation** | **Formula** | **Typical / KTU value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMG amplitude range (surface) | $V_{EMG,surface}$ | **50 – 5000** | $\mu V$ |
| 2 | EMG amplitude range (needle) | $V_{EMG,needle}$ | **100 – 10000** | $\mu V$ |
| 3 | EMG clinical bandwidth | $\Delta f_{EMG}$ | **20 – 500** (clinical) / 20 – 5000 (research) | Hz |
| 4 | ERG bandwidth | $\Delta f_{ERG}$ | **0.1 – 300** (flash) / DC – 3000 (pattern) | Hz |
| 5 | ERG b-wave amplitude (normal) | $V_{b}$ | **300 – 600** | $\mu V$ |
| 6 | ERG a-wave amplitude (normal) | $V_{a}$ | **100 – 250** | $\mu V$ |
| 7 | Peak spectral frequency of SFAP | $f_{peak} = \dfrac{0.8\,v}{\pi\,r}$ | depends on $v,r$ | Hz |
| 8 | Required pre-amp CMRR | $CMRR_{dB}$ | **≥ 100** | dB |
| 9 | Total system gain | $G_{tot} = A_1 \cdot A_2$ | $10^4$ – $10^6$ (target 5 V output) | dimensionless |
| 10 | Differential gain (INA) | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,k\Omega}{R_G}$ | AD620 / INA128 family | V/V |
| 11 | Input-impedance of pre-amp | $Z_{in}$ | **≥ 10 MΩ** (preferably ≥ 100 MΩ at signal freq.) | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | Noise of bio-amp (RTI) | $e_n$ | **< 1 µV RMS** in 0.1–500 Hz | V |
| 13 | Bipolar vs monopole surface electrode | $L_{bipolar}$ | **20 mm inter-electrode** | mm |
| 14 | Motor unit firing rate | $f_{MU}$ | **6 – 25** | Hz |
| 15 | Muscle fiber conduction velocity | $v$ | **2 – 6** | m/s |
| 16 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | ≥ 1000 Hz for EMG, ≥ 1000 Hz for ERG | Hz |
| 17 | Skirt of band-pass filter | roll-off | **≥ 24 dB/oct** (4th-order Butterworth) | dB/oct |
| 18 | ERG flash intensity (ISCEV standard) | $I_{flash}$ | **3.0 cd·s·m⁻²** | cd·s·m⁻² |
| 19 | Adaptation time (dark/light) | $t_{ad}$ | **20 min dark / 10 min light** | min |
| 20 | Pattern ERG check size | spatial freq. | **0.8° ± 0.2°** (15' × 11') | arc-min |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar `|` symbol has been replaced by `\vert` or `\;` spacing to preserve Markdown table integrity, as mandated by the engine's Table Integrity Safeguard.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Two Systems

- **EMG-driven prosthetics** use RMS-EMG to proportionally control DC motors in artificial hands.
- **Ergonomic EMG fatigue analysis** uses the shift in **median frequency** with contraction time to predict muscle exhaustion.
- **ERG** is indispensable in **gene-therapy clinical trials** for inherited retinal disorders (e.g., **voretigene neparvovec / Luxturna**) where the b-wave amplitude is a primary FDA-recognized endpoint.
- **Multifocal ERG (mfERG)** maps local cone responses across the macula, allowing topographical diagnosis of **macular dystrophies**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Surface EMG Amplitude as a Function of Recruitment and Firing Rate

The recorded surface EMG voltage $V_{EMG}(t)$ is the algebraic sum of $N$ active motor-unit action potential trains, each filtered by tissue and electrode transfer functions.

### Step 1 — Single motor unit contribution

Let one motor unit generate a stereotyped MUAP waveform $m(t)$. When it fires at times $t_k$, the contribution is:

$$
u_i(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} m(t - t_k)
$$

The sum over all active motor units:

$$
V_{EMG}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=0}^{K_i - 1} m(t - t_{i,k})
$$

### Step 2 — Conversion to RMS value (rectify + integrate over window $T$)

$$
V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} V_{EMG}^{\,2}(t)\, dt}
$$

### Step 3 — Statistical simplification

Assuming the $N$ MUAPs are statistically independent and each MU fires at mean rate $f_i$, the **central-limit theorem** yields:

$$
V_{RMS}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} m^2(\tau) \, d\tau \; = \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \, E_m
$$

where $E_m = \int m^2(\tau) d\tau$ is the **MUAP energy** constant.

### Step 4 — Substitute constant energy and rewrite

$$
V_{RMS}^{2} = E_m \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i
$$

### Step 5 — Final compact form

$$
\boxed{\,V_{RMS} = \sqrt{E_m \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i} = \sqrt{E_m \cdot N \cdot \bar{f}}\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The surface EMG RMS scales with the **square root** of the product of (a) the number of active motor units ($N$ = recruitment) and (b) their mean firing rate ($\bar f$). This is the central principle of the **Henneman size principle** and underlies **force-EMG linearity at low forces** (because both $N$ and $f_i$ grow together).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Total Amplifier Gain (EMG Signal Conditioning)

### Step 1 — Define the input signal range

Surface EMG peak amplitude: $V_{in,p} = 5 \text{ mV} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$

### Step 2 — Define the desired output

ADC full-scale input: $V_{out,p} = 5 \text{ V}$

### Step 3 — Compute total voltage gain

$$
A_{total} = \frac{V_{out,p}}{V_{in,p}} = \frac{5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000
$$

### Step 4 — Distribute gain across two stages

Choose $A_1 = 10$ (pre-amp, high-CMRR INA) and $A_2 = 100$ (main isolation amp + filter). Then:

$$
A_{total} = A_1 \times A_2 = 10 \times 100 = 1000 \;\checkmark
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the pre-amp resistor for an INA128 instrumentation amplifier

For INA128, $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G}$. Solving for $R_G$:

$$
R_G = \frac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{A_1 - 1} = \frac{49{,}400}{10 - 1} = 5488.89 \;\Omega \;\;\approx 5.49 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

Select the standard E96 value $R_G = 5.49$ k$\Omega$ (or 5.6 k$\Omega$ with minor gain error).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CMRR Requirement for a Biopotential Amplifier

### Step 1 — Define common-mode interference

Mains common-mode voltage at the body: $V_{CM} = 1$ mV (typical, from capacitive coupling to 230 V mains).

### Step 2 — Differential signal of interest (EMG)

Minimum detectable EMG: $V_{DM} = 50 \,\mu\text{V}$ (very weak contraction).

### Step 3 — Define allowed residual after common-mode rejection

Target residual $\le 10\%$ of minimum signal: $V_{resid} \le 5 \,\mu\text{V}$.

### Step 4 — Solve for minimum CMRR (linear)

$$
CMRR \ge \frac{V_{CM}}{V_{resid}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to dB

$$
CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = \mathbf{46.06 \; dB}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Even 46 dB would be acceptable for a "good" amplifier; however, **KTU and IEC 60601 mandate ≥ 100 dB** to give 12+ dB safety margin in the presence of larger interference (e.g., 10 mV common-mode during electrosurgical cautery).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Band-Pass Filter Cut-offs for EMG (4th-order Butterworth)

### Step 1 — Choose desired bandwidth

High-pass: $f_H = 20$ Hz. Low-pass: $f_L = 500$ Hz.

### Step 2 — Select filter order and topology

A **4th-order Butterworth** (2 cascaded 2nd-order Sallen–Key stages) gives a maximally flat pass-band and 24 dB/oct roll-off — the **KTU default** because it minimizes pass-band ringing of MUAP transients.

### Step 3 — Calculate Q-factors for 4th-order Butterworth

The pole pairs of a 4th-order Butterworth are at angles 67.5° and 22.5°:

$$
Q_1 = \frac{1}{2 \sin(22.5°)} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.3827} = 1.3066
$$

$$
Q_2 = \frac{1}{2 \sin(67.5°)} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.9239} = 0.5412
$$

### Step 4 — Compute component values for the first stage (LPF at 500 Hz)

Using Sallen–Key unity-gain topology with $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$
R_1 = R_2 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C \cdot \text{scaling}} \;\;
$$

Choose $C = 100$ nF. Then:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 500 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 3183\;\Omega
$$

Select the standard value $R = 3.2$ k$\Omega$ (≈ 0.6% error).

### Step 5 — Compute component values for the high-pass stage (HPF at 20 Hz)

Choose $C = 1 \,\mu F$:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 20 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} = 7958\;\Omega
$$

Select the standard value $R = 8.0$ k$\Omega$ (≈ 0.5% error).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: ERG b-wave Amplitude Under Scotopic and Photopic Conditions

The Granit-inspired **P-III / P-II model** treats the ERG as the algebraic sum of two independent processes: the photoreceptor response P-III (negative) and the bipolar/Müller P-II (positive).

### Step 1 — Express a-wave (P-III) and b-wave (P-II)

$$
a(t) = -A_{III}\, e^{-t/\tau_a}
$$

$$
b(t) = A_{II} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_b}\right) e^{-t/\tau_c}
$$

where $\tau_a, \tau_b, \tau_c$ are the time constants of the a-wave rise, b-wave rise, and b-wave fall, respectively.

### Step 2 — Total ERG

$$
V_{ERG}(t) = a(t) + b(t) = -A_{III}\, e^{-t/\tau_a} + A_{II} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_b}\right) e^{-t/\tau_c}
$$

### Step 3 — Find the peak b-wave time $t_{b,\max}$ by setting $dV/dt = 0$ in the b-wave region (assume P-III has saturated)

Setting $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_b}\right) e^{-t/\tau_c}\right] = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\tau_b} e^{-t/\tau_b} e^{-t/\tau_c} - \dfrac{1}{\tau_c} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_b}\right) e^{-t/\tau_c} = 0
$$

Dividing through by $e^{-t/\tau_c}$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\tau_b} e^{-t/\tau_b} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_c} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_b}\right)
$$

Let $x = e^{-t/\tau_b}$, so $1 - e^{-t/\tau_b} = 1 - x$:

$$
\dfrac{x}{\tau_b} = \dfrac{1 - x}{\tau_c}
$$

$$
x \tau_c = \tau_b (1 - x)
$$

$$
x (\tau_c + \tau_b) = \tau_b
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{\tau_b}{\tau_c + \tau_b}
$$

### Step 4 — Recover $t_{b,\max}$

$$
t_{b,\max} = \tau_b \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\tau_c + \tau_b}{\tau_b}\right) = \tau_b \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_c}{\tau_b}\right)
$$

> **Plug in typical values:** $\tau_b = 30$ ms, $\tau_c = 200$ ms:
> 
> $$t_{b,\max} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{200}{30}\right) = 30 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(7.667) = 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.037 = 61.1 \text{ ms}$$
> 
> This matches the ISCEV-reported normal b-wave peak latency of **60 ± 5 ms** — a perfect consistency check.

### Step 5 — Plug back to get the b-wave peak amplitude

$$
A_{b,\max} = A_{II} \left(1 - \dfrac{\tau_b}{\tau_c + \tau_b}\right) \left(\dfrac{\tau_c}{\tau_c + \tau_b}\right)
$$

$$
A_{b,\max} = A_{II} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_c}{\tau_c + \tau_b} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_c}{\tau_c + \tau_b} = A_{II} \left(\dfrac{\tau_c}{\tau_c + \tau_b}\right)^{\!2}
$$

With the same values:

$$
A_{b,\max} = A_{II} \left(\dfrac{200}{230}\right)^{2} = 0.757 \, A_{II}
$$

Thus the observed b-wave peak is ~75.7% of the intrinsic P-II generator amplitude, with the remainder being "hidden" by the rise–fall dynamics.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Cause-and-Effect on the ERG Waveform

| **Pathology** | **a-wave** | **b-wave** | **OPs** | **Latency** | **Clinical sign** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Retinitis pigmentosa (rod–cone) | ↓ (rod) | ↓ (scotopic) | ↓ | ↑ | Night blindness |
| Cone dystrophy | Normal (scotopic) | ↓ (photopic) | Normal | Normal–↑ | Day blindness, photophobia |
| Leber congenital amaurosis | Severely ↓ | Severely ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | Profound vision loss at birth |
| Central retinal artery occlusion | Variable | ↓ b/a ratio | ↓ | ↑ | Cherry-red spot absent |
| Diabetic retinopathy | Mild ↓ | ↓ | ↓ OPs early | ↑ | Vascular leakage |
| Glaucoma (mfERG) | Normal in full-field | Normal in full-field | Normal | Normal | Local mfERG depression |
| Chloroquine / hydroxychloroquine toxicity | Normal | ↓ (photopic) | — | ↑ | Bull's-eye maculopathy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unified EMG / ERG Front-End Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **signal-conditioning chain** common to both EMG and ERG recording systems, with the bandwidth parameters explicitly noted for each modality.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Muscle or Retina<br/>Source] --> B[Electrodes<br/>Surface or Contact-Lens]
    B --> C[Pre-amp Stage<br/>INA128 / AD620<br/>Gain 10x]
    C --> D[Isolation Amp<br/>ADuM3150<br/>5 kV isolation]
    D --> E[Band-pass Filter<br/>EMG 20-500 Hz<br/>ERG 0.1-300 Hz]
    E --> F[Notch Filter<br/>50 or 60 Hz Q=5]
    F --> G[Main Amplifier<br/>Gain 100x Variable]
    G --> H[Rectifier + Smoother<br/>Optional for EMG envelope]
    H --> I[ADC 12-bit<br/>1 kS/s min]
    I --> J[Display / DAQ<br/>PC or CRO]
```
