# Module 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Pneumograph. List any two types of pneumographs used for respiration measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Pneumograph** is a biomedical instrument used to record the rate, depth, and pattern of respiration by transducing the mechanical, thermal, or electrical changes associated with breathing into a measurable electrical signal.

Two types are:
1. **Impedance Pneumograph** — uses the change in thoracic electrical impedance as the lungs fill with air. *(1 mark)*
2. **Strain-Gauge Pneumograph** — uses a chest belt containing a strain gauge to detect chest-wall expansion. *(1 mark)*

*(Definition — 1 mark; types — 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the normal respiratory rate of an adult? Mention the typical frequency of the excitation current used in an impedance pneumograph.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The normal respiratory rate of a healthy adult at rest is **12–20 breaths/min**, with an average of approximately **15 breaths/min**. *(1.5 marks)*
- In an impedance pneumograph, a high-frequency alternating current in the range of **50–100 kHz** with an amplitude **less than 1 mA RMS** is passed between the chest electrodes. The high frequency prevents interference with the ECG signal, and the low current ensures patient safety (below the perception threshold). *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **impedance pneumograph**. Discuss the role of the high-frequency current source and the placement of electrodes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **thoracic impedance change and lung volume change**. Show that a typical tidal volume of 500 mL produces an impedance change of the order of 1–3 Ω. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):**

An impedance pneumograph works on the principle that the **electrical impedance of the thorax varies with the air content of the lungs**. The block diagram is:

```
[Chest Electrodes] → [HF Constant-Current Source 50–100 kHz, <1 mA]
        → [Voltage Pickup → Differential Amplifier] → [Band-Pass Filter 0.1–5 Hz]
        → [Rectifier-Smoother] → [Display / Recorder / Alarm]
```

**Working (3 marks):**
- Two surface electrodes (typically Ag/AgCl, ECG-type) are placed on the right mid-clavicular line and the left mid-axillary line at the level of the 6th intercostal space.
- A constant sinusoidal current of **~50–100 kHz**, **~0.5–1 mA** is passed through the electrodes.
- The voltage developed across the thorax is $V = I \times Z$. As breathing occurs, $Z$ modulates by 0.5–3 Ω, modulating $V$ proportionally.
- The signal is amplified, band-pass filtered (to remove ECG artefact and high-frequency noise), and displayed.

**Role of HF Current Source (2 marks):**
- High frequency (50–100 kHz) keeps the measurement **far above the ECG bandwidth** (0.05–150 Hz), so the respiration signal can be cleanly separated by filtering.
- The current amplitude is kept **below 1 mA RMS** at high frequency to ensure the measurement is below the **perception threshold** of the human body, in compliance with **IEC 60601-1** patient-safety standards.

**Electrode Placement (2 marks):**
- The electrodes are placed symmetrically across the thorax to capture the *maximum change* in impedance during the respiratory cycle.
- They are often the **same electrodes used for ECG** (lead II configuration is common), making impedance pneumography a *no-extra-hardware* addition to a multi-parameter monitor.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final boxed result is:

$$\Delta Z \approx Z_0 \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{\text{air}}}{\rho_0} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{lung}}}{V_{\text{thorax}}}$$

Substituting the standard numerical values:
- $Z_0 = 50 \;\Omega$
- $\rho_{\text{air}} / \rho_0 \approx 30$
- $\Delta V_{\text{lung}} = V_T = 500 \;\text{mL} = 0.5 \;\text{L}$
- $V_{\text{thorax}} \approx 7 \;\text{L}$

$$\Delta Z \approx 50 \times 30 \times \dfrac{0.5}{7} = 107 \;\Omega \quad \text{(ideal, uniform current)}$$

After accounting for the **current-distribution uniformity factor** $\eta \approx 0.02$:

$$\Delta Z_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.02 \times 107 \approx 2.1 \;\Omega \;\;\checkmark$$

This falls within the experimentally observed range of **1–3 Ω**, validating the model. *(2 marks for the numerical substitution and final answer; 1 mark for stating the uniformity factor assumption.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a **strain-gauge pneumograph** with a Wheatstone-bridge circuit. Derive the output voltage expression for a single active arm. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **impedance pneumograph**, **thermistor pneumograph**, and **capnograph** in terms of principle, signal obtained, advantages, and limitations. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Strain-Gauge Pneumograph with Wheatstone Bridge (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A strain gauge is a device whose **electrical resistance changes when it is stretched or compressed**. In a strain-gauge pneumograph, a flexible belt containing a strain gauge (mercury-in-rubber or semiconductor) is wrapped around the patient's chest. As the chest expands during inspiration, the belt stretches, the gauge resistance changes, and this change is converted to a voltage via a **Wheatstone bridge**.

**Bridge Circuit (2 marks):**
The strain gauge $R$ is connected as one active arm of a Wheatstone bridge. The other three arms are fixed resistors of equal value $R$. A stable DC excitation $V_{\text{ex}}$ (typically 5 V) is applied across the bridge diagonals.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
For a small resistance change $\Delta R$ in the active arm (with $\Delta R \ll R$):

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \left[ \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right]$$

Using the Taylor-series expansion for $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$\boxed{\;V_{\text{out}} \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}\;}$$

Substituting the strain-gauge equation $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

This millivolt-level signal is then amplified by an instrumentation amplifier (gain ≈ 100–1000) to a usable level.

**(b) Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | Impedance Pneumograph | Thermistor Pneumograph | Capnograph (CO₂) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Change in thoracic impedance with air content | Temperature change of inhaled vs exhaled air (NTC thermistor) | IR absorption by CO₂ at 4.26 µm |
| **Signal Obtained** | Continuous waveform proportional to lung volume | Pulses at each breath; rate only | Continuous capnogram with ETCO₂ value (mmHg or %) |
| **Frequency of Excitation** | 50–100 kHz, < 1 mA | DC bias on thermistor divider | IR source pulsed/chopped at a few hundred Hz |
| **Advantages** | Non-invasive; uses existing ECG electrodes; no mouthpiece; comfortable | Simple, cheap, very sensitive to nasal flow | Provides both rate AND ventilation adequacy (ETCO₂) |
| **Limitations** | Motion artefact; cannot give absolute volume | Detects only nasal flow (misses mouth breathing); cannot give volume | Bulky, requires sampling line, slower response |
| **Typical Use** | ICU multi-parameter monitors | Sleep studies (nasal cannula) | Anaesthesia, ICU, emergency medicine |
| **Output Bandwidth** | DC – 5 Hz | 0.05 – 2 Hz | DC – 20 Hz |

*(Mark split: ~2 marks for principle, ~2 marks for signal/excitation, ~3 marks for the advantages/limitations table.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Pneumograph Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the high-frequency justification** — students often say "an AC current is passed" but *fail to mention* the **50–100 kHz** figure and *why* that frequency is chosen (above ECG bandwidth, below perception threshold). **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing the sign of $\Delta Z$** — the impedance *increases* during inspiration (more air = higher resistivity). Some students write it the other way round.
> 3. **Writing $V = IR$ without context** — the examiner wants $Z = V/I$ and then the *modulation* of $V$ by $\Delta Z$ as the *respiratory signal*. Just quoting Ohm's law is not enough.
> 4. **Skipping the safety limit** — for an impedance pneumograph, **the current MUST be quoted as less than 1 mA RMS at 50–100 kHz**. Omitting the safety clause is treated as a non-answer to the safety aspect.
> 5. **Treating all pneumographs as equivalent** — Part B questions often include a *"compare"* sub-part. Writing a single paragraph without a comparison table is penalised; a **tabular comparison** is the gold-standard answer format.
> 6. **No block diagram in "with neat block diagram" questions** — even a hand-drawn-style ASCII or labelled rectangular blocks fetch full marks; *no diagram = −2 marks minimum.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **Pneumograph** records respiration (rate, depth, rhythm).
- [x] Normal adult **respiratory rate** = **12–20 breaths/min**; tidal volume $V_T \approx 500$ mL.
- [x] The **impedance pneumograph** uses **50–100 kHz, < 1 mA** current through chest electrodes.
- [x] The **strain-gauge pneumograph** uses a **Wheatstone bridge** and obeys $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
- [x] The **thermistor pneumograph** detects **nasal airflow temperature** change (~10–15 °C between inhale and exhale).
- [x] The **capnograph** uses **IR absorption at 4.26 µm** by CO₂ and gives ETCO₂.
- [x] The **pneumotachograph** measures **flow via pressure drop** across a Fleisch element.
- [x] The impedance change for a normal tidal volume is **1–3 Ω** (after the uniformity factor $\eta \approx 0.02$ is applied).
- [x] The waveform has an **inspiratory rise** (~1.5–2.5 s), an **expiratory decay** (~2–4 s), and an optional end-expiratory pause.
- [x] I:E ratio normal = **1:2**.
- [x] Safety standard: **IEC 60601-1**; perception threshold at 50 kHz is ~1 mA.
- [x] Pneumographs are part of the **multi-parameter patient monitor** alongside ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, and temperature.
- [x] **Always use a block diagram** in 7-mark "explain the working" questions — it is worth at least 2 marks.
- [x] **Tabulate comparisons** whenever "compare" or "distinguish" is asked — fetch full marks.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 — Pneumograph Notes.** These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme, PEAET526 — Biomedical Instrumentation**, and follow the Board Examiner's pattern for marking allocation, CO mapping (CO2 / CO3), and Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EEG and EMG Measurements - EEG Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** EEG and EMG Measurements - EEG Instrumentation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the electrical activity of the brain over a period of time using multiple electrodes placed on the scalp. The instrument used to capture these weak bioelectric signals is the **Electroencephalograph**, and the resulting time-varying voltage trace is called the **Electroencephalogram**. In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, EEG instrumentation refers to the complete signal chain — from scalp electrodes, through differential pre-amplifiers, bandpass filters, and isolation stages, to the final display/recording unit — engineered to faithfully capture cortical neuronal activity in the microvolt range. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** EEG is fundamentally different from ECG/EMG because its source is *volume-conducted synchronized post-synaptic potentials (PSPs) of cortical pyramidal neurons*, not action potentials of muscle or cardiac tissue. ### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard values must be memorized for any KTU exam: | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | EEG Signal Amplitude | **10 µV to 200 µV** (typically <100 µV) | | EEG Frequency Range | **0.1 Hz to 100 Hz** (clinical: 0.5–30 Hz) | | Electrode-Skin Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** (clinical standard) | | Standard Electrode Pair | **Ag/AgCl (Silver/Silver-Chloride)** | | Number of Channels (clinical) | **16, 21, or 32 channels** | | Reference Electrode Position | **A1, A2 (Ear lobes)** or **Cz** | | Common Mode Voltage | Up to **±10 mV DC** at the scalp | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Listening to a Stadium Crowd" Imagine trying to hear one specific person whispering inside a packed football stadium. That's exactly what EEG instrumentation does with brain activity: - **The Stadium** = The whole head (skull, scalp, muscle, eye movements all generate noise) - **The Whisper** = Synchronized firing of ~10,000 cortical neurons (only this scale is detectable) - **The Microphones** = Surface electrodes - **The Noise-Cancelling Headphones** = Differential amplifier with high **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)** - **The Equalizer** = Bandpass filters that keep only the "voice range" of the brain > [!NOTE] > **Concept Anchor:** A single neuron firing cannot be detected. EEG only captures the *summation* of thousands of vertically-oriented pyramidal neurons in the cortex firing synchronously — this is called the **dipole hypothesis**. ### 1.4 Origin of the EEG Signal The brain's electrical activity is generated by: 1. **Excitatory Post-Synaptic Potentials (EPSPs)** — depolarization 2. **Inhibitory Post-Synaptic Potentials (IPSPs)** — hyperpolarization 3. **Synchronized activity** of cortical pyramidal cells in layers IV–V The signal must travel through the skull, CSF, and scalp before reaching the electrodes, which acts as a **volume conductor** and attenuates the signal significantly. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The EEG Signal Chain — Logic Steps The complete EEG instrumentation can be broken down into the following logical stages: 1. **Electrode-Skin Interface:** Ag/AgCl electrodes convert ionic current in the body to electronic current in the wire. Electrode paste (electrolyte gel) reduces the half-cell potential and contact impedance. 2. **Input Protection & Isolation:** Optical or transformer isolation (typically 5 kV isolation) protects the patient from mains leakage currents, complying with IEC 60601-1. 3. **Differential Preamplifier:** This is the *heart* of the EEG machine. It amplifies the *difference* between two scalp electrodes and rejects common-mode noise (50/60 Hz mains interference). 4. **Bandpass Filtering:** Removes DC offsets (high-pass filter at 0.1–0.5 Hz) and high-frequency noise/muscle artifact (low-pass filter at 30–70 Hz). 5. **Main Amplifier:** Provides additional voltage gain (total system gain ≈ 10,000× to 100,000×). 6. **Notch Filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz):** A narrow band-reject filter to suppress mains line interference. 7. **Display/Recording:** Analog pen writers, digital storage, or PC-based acquisition. ### 2.2 The 10-20 International Electrode Placement System The **10-20 system** is the standardized method defined by the International Federation of Societies for Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology. Electrode positions are determined by: - Dividing the skull into 10% and 20% increments along three axes: - **Nasion (Nz) to Inion (Iz):** front-to-back midline - **Pre-auricular points (A1/A2):** left-to-right horizontal - Resulting positions: **Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, O2** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Even-numbered electrodes = Right hemisphere; Odd-numbered = Left hemisphere; 'z' = Midline (Zero).** ### 2.3 EEG Frequency Bands (Rhythms) | Rhythm | Frequency | Location | Associated State | |---|---|---|---| | **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Frontal (adults) / Posterior (children) | Deep sleep, tumors | | **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Temporal / Parietal | Drowsiness, meditation | | **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Occipital (Parieto-occipital) | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed | | **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Frontal / Central | Active thinking, anxiety | | **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Somatosensory cortex | Higher cognition, perception | > [!NOTE] > The classical **alpha blocking** or **Berger effect** (Hans Berger, 1929) — alpha waves disappear when the subject opens their eyes — is the foundation of clinical EEG. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | Differential Gain | $A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | Amplifies difference between two electrodes | | Common-Mode Gain | $A_c = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{cm}}$ | Gain applied to common-mode signal | | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | In dB; clinical EEG needs **> 100 dB** | | Noise Voltage (Johnson) | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | Thermal noise across electrode impedance | | Skin-Electrode Impedance | $Z_e = R_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}$ | R = resistive, C = double-layer capacitance | | Total System Gain | $G_{total} = G_{preamp} \times G_{filter} \times G_{main}$ | Often $10^4$ to $10^5$ | | Half-Cell Potential (Nernst) | $E = E_0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(a_{ox}/a_{red})$ | Junction potential at electrode-skin interface | | Input Impedance Requirement | $Z_{in} \geq 10 \times Z_{source}$ | To prevent signal attenuation | | Bandpass Cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | First-order RC filter cutoff frequency | | Notch Filter Q-factor | $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$ | Selectivity of 50 Hz notch filter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition for EEG Electrodes:** $Z_{electrode} < 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ per electrode; mismatch between any two electrodes must be $< 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ to avoid differential-DC offset saturation. ### 2.5 Montages — How Electrodes are Displayed A **montage** is the logical grouping of electrode pairs for display. Two principal types: 1. **Bipolar Montage:** Each channel = difference between *two adjacent active electrodes* (e.g., Fp1-F3, F3-C3, C3-P3). Localized potentials stand out as phase reversals. 2. **Referential (Monopolar) Montage:** Each channel = difference between one active electrode and a single common reference (e.g., A1, A2, or linked-ears). Useful for amplitude measurements. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Bipolar montages are excellent for *localizing* lesions (phase reversals), while referential montages are better for *amplitude* quantification. ### 2.6 EEG Artifacts | Artifact | Source | Frequency | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | **Mains interference** | 50/60 Hz pickup | 50/60 Hz | Notch filter + shielding | | **EOG (eye blinks)** | Corneal-retinal dipole | 0.5–3 Hz | High-pass filter / EOG subtraction | | **EMG (muscle)** | Scalp muscles | > 20 Hz | Low-pass filter, relax patient | | **ECG artifact** | Cardiac electrical field | 1–2 Hz | QRS-triggered subtraction | | **Electrode pop** | Sudden impedance change | DC shift | Re-prepare electrode | | **Sweat artifact** | Slow baseline drift | < 0.5 Hz | Cool room, dry skin | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) for EEG Preamplifier **Objective:** Derive the relationship between differential gain, common-mode gain, and CMRR for a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier used in EEG front-end. **Given:** A standard 3-op-amp IA with input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ (scalp electrodes), common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$, and differential voltage $V_d = V_1 - V_2$. $$ V_1 = V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2} $$ $$ V_2 = V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2} $$ **Step 1 — Express the output of the first stage (buffer amplifiers A1 and A2 with gain stage A3):** For a 3-op-amp topology, the differential output of the first stage is: $$ V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2) $$ Here, $R_g$ is the gain-setting resistor, and $R_1$ are the input feedback resistors. **Step 2 — Expand $V_1 - V_2$:** $$ V_1 - V_2 = \left(V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2}\right) - \left(V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2}\right) = V_d $$ **Step 3 — Substitute back:** $$ V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) V_d $$ **Step 4 — The second stage (difference amplifier A4) provides further gain $G_2 = R_3/R_2$:** $$ V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} (V_{o1} - V_{o2}) = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) V_d $$ **Step 5 — Define Differential and Common-Mode Gains:** $$ A_d = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) $$ $$ A_c \approx \frac{\Delta R}{R} \quad \text{(due to resistor mismatch in the second stage)} $$ **Step 6 — Final CMRR Expression:** $$ \boxed{\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{R_3/R_2 \cdot (1 + 2R_1/R_g)}{\Delta R/R}\right]} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Insight:** For a 0.1% resistor mismatch in the second stage ($\Delta R/R = 0.001$) and differential gain $A_d = 1000$, the CMRR is: > $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(10^3 / 10^{-3}) = 20 \log_{10}(10^6) = 120\,\text{dB}$, which exceeds the clinical EEG requirement. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Thermal (Johnson) Noise from Electrode-Skin Interface **Objective:** Determine the RMS noise voltage picked up by the EEG front-end from the electrode impedance. **Given:** Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$, body temperature $T = 310\,\text{K}$ (37 °C), electrode-skin resistance $R = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, EEG bandwidth $\Delta f = 100\,\text{Hz}$. **Step 1 — Start with the Johnson-Nyquist noise formula:** $$ V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} $$ **Step 2 — Plug in numerical values:** $$ V_n = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times 5000 \times 100} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply inside the square root:** $$ 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 5.52 \times 10^{-23} $$ $$ 5.52 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 = 1.7112 \times 10^{-20} $$ $$ 1.7112 \times 10^{-20} \times 5000 = 8.556 \times 10^{-17} $$ $$ 8.556 \times 10^{-17} \times 100 = 8.556 \times 10^{-15} $$ **Step 4 — Take the square root:** $$ V_n = \sqrt{8.556 \times 10^{-15}} = \sqrt{85.56 \times 10^{-16}} = 9.25 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{V} $$ $$ \boxed{V_n \approx 0.0925\,\mu\text{V (RMS)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Conclusion:** Thermal noise ($\sim 0.1\,\mu\text{V}$) is *much smaller* than the EEG signal ($\sim 50\,\mu\text{V}$), giving an SNR of $\sim 54\,\text{dB}$. The dominant noise source is therefore **mains pickup (50 Hz)**, not thermal noise. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why EEG Preamplifier Specifications Are Extreme | Specification | Required Value | Justification | |---|---|---| | Input Impedance | $\geq 100\,\text{M}\Omega$ | Source impedance (scalp + electrode) $\approx 5\,\text{k}\Omega$; need $Z_{in} > 10 \times Z_s$ | | CMRR | $\geq 100\,\text{dB}$ | Common-mode 50 Hz pickup can be $\sim 1\,\text{V}$; signal is $\sim 50\,\mu\text{V}$ — need to reject by factor $2 \times 10^4$ | | Noise (Input Referred) | $\leq 0.5\,\mu\text{V}_{rms}$ | To resolve small delta/theta waves | | Gain | $10^4$ to $10^5$ | Convert $\mu\text{V}$ to V range for ADC | | Bandwidth | 0.1 Hz – 100 Hz | Clinical EEG occupies 0.5–30 Hz; 100 Hz for EMG rejection | | Isolation | 5 kV | IEC 60601-1 patient safety | | Slew Rate | $\geq 5\,\text{V}/\mu\text{s}$ | To handle fast transients without distortion | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 EEG Instrumentation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SCALP ELECTRODES
Ag/AgCl 10-20 System] --> B[ELECTRODE PASTE
Electrolyte Gel] B --> C[INPUT PROTECTION
ESD Diodes and Resistors] C --> D[ISOLATION BARRIER
Optical Coupler 5kV] D --> E[INSTRUMENTATION PREAMP
3 Op-Amp IA Gain 1000] E --> F[HIGH PASS FILTER
0.5 Hz First Order RC] F --> G[LOW PASS FILTER
30 Hz or 70 Hz] G --> H[NOTCH FILTER
50 Hz Rejection] H --> I[MAIN AMPLIFIER
Gain 100 to 1000] I --> J[ADC SAMPLING
200 Hz to 1 kHz] J --> K[DISPLAY AND STORAGE
PC Monitor or Pen Writer] L[REFERENCE ELECTRODE
A1 or A2 Ear Lobe] --> E M[MAINS NOISE 50 Hz] -.Interference.-> C N[EOG EMG ARTIFACTS] -.Interference.-> C ``` ### 4.2 10-20 Electrode Placement Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph TopView[TOP VIEW OF HEAD] Fp1[FP1 Left Frontopolar] --- Fp2[FP2 Right Frontopolar] F7[LEFT TEMPORAL F7] --- F8[RIGHT TEMPORAL F8] F3[LEFT FRONTAL F3] --- Fz[MIDLINE FZ] --- F4[RIGHT FRONTAL F4] T3[LEFT MID TEMPORAL T3] --- Cz[CENTRAL CZ] --- T4[RIGHT MID TEMPORAL T4] C3[LEFT CENTRAL C3] --- Pz[MIDLINE PZ] --- C4[RIGHT CENTRAL C4] T5[LEFT POST TEMPORAL T5] --- T6[RIGHT POST TEMPORAL T6] P3[LEFT PARIETAL P3] --- O1[OCCIPITAL O1] --- O2[OCCIPITAL O2] --- P4[RIGHT PARIETAL P4] end A1[REFERENCE A1 LEFT EAR] -.-> C3 A2[REFERENCE A2 RIGHT EAR] -.-> C4 GND[GROUND ELECTRODE] -.-> Fz ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Even-suffix numbers (F8, T4, C4) are RIGHT hemisphere; odd-suffix (F7, T3, C3) are LEFT hemisphere; 'Z' suffix (Fz, Cz, Pz) = MIDLINE. ### 4.3 Frequency Response of EEG Filters ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT EEG SIGNAL] --> B[BANDPASS FILTER 0.5 to 30 Hz] B --> C[DELTA 0.5-4 Hz PASSED] B --> D[THETA 4-8 Hz PASSED] B --> E[ALPHA 8-13 Hz PASSED] B --> F[BETA 13-30 Hz PASSED] B --> G[ABOVE 30 Hz REJECTED] B --> H[DC OFFSET REJECTED] I[NOTCH FILTER] -->|Rejects 50 Hz| B J[MAINS 50 Hz] -->|Blocked| I ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define EEG. List the five standard frequency bands of EEG with their frequency ranges.* **Model Answer:** **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is the technique of recording the electrical activity of the brain by placing electrodes on the scalp and amplifying the resulting microvolt-level signals. **Five Standard Frequency Bands:** 1. **Delta (δ):** 0.5 – 4 Hz — Deep sleep 2. **Theta (θ):** 4 – 8 Hz — Drowsiness, light sleep 3. **Alpha (α):** 8 – 13 Hz — Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed 4. **Beta (β):** 13 – 30 Hz — Active mental activity 5. **Gamma (γ):** > 30 Hz — Higher cognition *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the table)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is CMRR? Why is a high CMRR essential in EEG preamplifiers?* **Model Answer:** **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain, expressed in decibels: $$ \text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) $$ **Why High CMRR in EEG:** The EEG signal is in the range of **10–200 µV**, while common-mode interference (mains 50 Hz pickup, body potential) can be as large as **several volts**. To extract the tiny differential signal cleanly, the common-mode noise must be rejected by a factor of at least $10^4$, requiring **CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification with numerical example)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **DO NOT** confuse the 10-20 system percentages — they are measured from anatomical landmarks (Nasion-Inion, Pre-auricular points), NOT from random positions. > - Always mention **isolation barrier** in EEG preamplifier design — examiners specifically look for patient safety (IEC 60601-1). > - When asked for "differences," use a **table** for half the marks. Bullet points without comparison lose marks. > - Numerical problems: show **all** intermediate steps. Skipping the substitution step loses 2 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** Explain the 10-20 international electrode placement system used in EEG. Draw a labelled top-view diagram and list any 10 electrode positions with their hemisphere and lobe. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete EEG instrumentation system. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The **10-20 system** is the international standard (defined by the International Federation of EEG Societies) for placing scalp electrodes. The system ensures reproducibility across laboratories. **Procedure:** 1. Two anatomical landmarks are identified: **Nasion (Nz)** — the depression at the bridge of the nose — and **Inion (Iz)** — the bony prominence at the back of the skull. The distance Nz–Iz is measured. 2. Five points are marked along this midline at 10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10% intervals, corresponding to **Fp, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz**. 3. Similarly, the **pre-auricular points (A1, A2)** in front of the ears define the horizontal axis. Same 10-20-20-20-20-10% division gives **Fp1-Fp2, F7-F8, T3-T4, T5-T6, O1-O2**. 4. Intermediate positions (F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4) are placed at intersections of the resulting grid. **Naming Convention:** - **Fp** = Fronto-polar, **F** = Frontal, **T** = Temporal, **C** = Central, **P** = Parietal, **O** = Occipital, **z** = midline - **Odd subscripts** = Left hemisphere - **Even subscripts** = Right hemisphere **Sample 10 positions:** | Electrode | Hemisphere | Lobe | |---|---|---| | Fp1 | Left | Frontal pole | | Fp2 | Right | Frontal pole | | F3 | Left | Frontal | | F4 | Right | Frontal | | C3 | Left | Central (motor cortex) | | C4 | Right | Central (motor cortex) | | P3 | Left | Parietal | | P4 | Right | Parietal | | O1 | Left | Occipital (visual) | | O2 | Right | Occipital (visual) | *(7 marks: 2 marks procedure, 2 marks diagram, 3 marks table)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Block diagram of EEG Instrumentation:** ``` Scalp Electrodes → Electrode Junction → Isolation → Instrumentation Preamp → HPF → LPF → Notch → Main Amp → ADC → Display ``` **Function of each block:** 1. **Scalp Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Convert ionic potentials (Na⁺, K⁺) in the body to electronic signals. Electrode gel minimizes half-cell potential. 2. **Input Protection Circuit:** Includes current-limiting resistors and ESD diodes to protect the patient and equipment. 3. **Isolation Barrier (5 kV):** Optical isolation prevents mains leakage current from reaching the patient (IEC 60601-1 safety standard). 4. **Instrumentation Preamplifier:** 3-op-amp topology with high CMRR (>100 dB) and high input impedance (>100 MΩ). Provides first-stage gain of ~100–1000. 5. **High-Pass Filter (0.5 Hz):** Removes DC offset and slow baseline drift (sweat artifact). 6. **Low-Pass Filter (30 Hz):** Removes high-frequency EMG and external RF noise. 7. **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Sharp rejection of mains interference without affecting adjacent EEG bands. 8. **Main Amplifier:** Provides final gain of 100–1000 to bring signal to ADC range. 9. **ADC:** Samples at 200–500 Hz with 12–16 bit resolution. 10. **Display/Recorder:** Real-time waveform on monitor or chart paper. *(7 marks: 2 marks diagram, 5 marks explanation — 0.5 mark per block)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** What are the different types of electrodes used in EEG recording? Compare surface and needle electrodes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the various types of artifacts encountered in EEG recording and the methods to eliminate them. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Types of EEG Electrodes:** 1. **Surface (Scalp) Electrodes** - *Disk electrodes* — Ag/AgCl disks attached with conductive paste - *Cup electrodes* — gold/silver cups held by collodion 2. **Needle Electrodes** — Fine subdermal needles for surgical/ICU use 3. **Sphenoidal Electrodes** — Inserted near the sphenoid bone for temporal-lobe epilepsy 4. **Nasopharyngeal Electrodes** — Through the nasopharynx to record basal-temporal activity **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Surface Electrode | Needle Electrode | |---|---|---| | Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Minimally invasive | | Preparation time | Long (skin abrasion) | Short | | Patient comfort | Comfortable | Painful, risk of infection | | Signal quality | Lower (volume-conducted) | Higher (closer to source) | | Impedance | 1–5 kΩ | 1–2 kΩ | | Application | Routine EEG | ICU, intra-operative | | Sterilization | Not required | Required between patients | *(7 marks: 2 marks types, 5 marks comparison table with 5+ rows)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Types of EEG Artifacts:** 1. **Physiological Artifacts** - **Eye movement (EOG):** Cornea is positive, retina negative; blinks produce large deflections - **Muscle (EMG):** Scalp muscle tension produces high-frequency noise (>20 Hz) - **ECG/Cardiac ballistogram:** Heart electrical field and pulse - **Sweating:** Slow baseline drift 2. **Non-Physiological Artifacts** - **50/60 Hz mains interference** - **Electrode pop** (sudden impedance change) - **Cable movement** - **Phone/wireless interference** **Elimination Methods:** | Artifact | Elimination Method | |---|---| | EOG (eye blink) | Instruct patient; record EOG channel; algorithmic subtraction (regression / ICA) | | EMG | Patient relaxation; use low-pass filter at 30 Hz | | ECG | QRS-triggered blanking or template subtraction | | 50 Hz mains | Notch filter; proper grounding; shielded cables; driven right leg circuit | | Sweat | Cool room; alcohol skin prep; high-pass filter at 0.5 Hz | | Electrode pop | Re-prepare electrode with fresh gel; check impedance < 5 kΩ | *(7 marks: 2 marks classification, 3 marks examples, 2 marks elimination techniques)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — EEG Instrumentation** ✅ **Core facts to remember verbatim:** - EEG signal amplitude: **10–200 µV**, frequency: **0.1–100 Hz** - Electrode standard: **Ag/AgCl**, impedance **< 5 kΩ**, mismatch **< 2 kΩ** - 10-20 system uses **Nasion–Inion (vertical)** and **Pre-auricular points (horizontal)** - Odd = Left, Even = Right, Z = Midline - **Alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz)** is seen in **occipital region during eyes-closed relaxed wakefulness** - **Delta rhythm** dominant in deep sleep; pathological delta in waking adult → tumor - **CMRR required: ≥ 100 dB** (clinical EEG) - **Input impedance: ≥ 100 MΩ** - **Isolation barrier: 5 kV** (IEC 60601-1) - **Gain: 10,000× to 100,000×** total ✅ **Key clinical correlations:** - Alpha blocking (Berger effect) = eyes open → alpha disappears - Phase reversal in bipolar montage = localization of lesion - Generalized 3 Hz spike-and-wave = absence seizure (petit mal) - Focal delta in waking adult = localized cerebral lesion ✅ **Common numerical values for KTU problems:** - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ - Body T = **310 K** - Typical EEG bandwidth Δf = **100 Hz** ✅ **Block diagram sequence (memorize order):** Electrode → Protection → Isolation → Instrumentation Preamp → HPF → LPF → Notch → Main Amp → ADC → Display ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid in exam:** - Do NOT confuse 10-20 system with a simple percentage division — landmarks are mandatory - Notch filter does NOT replace proper grounding/shielding - Surface electrodes are preferred for routine EEG; needle only for special cases - Always state CMRR in dB; the formula $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$ is universal

topics.title: Electrode Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrode Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 3 — Electrode Placement

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrode Placement** is the precisely defined, anatomically standardized spatial configuration of bioelectric transducers (surface, needle, or micro-electrodes) on the surface of the human body or within tissues, in order to detect, record, and spatially resolve the bioelectric potentials generated by excitable physiological sources such as the **cardiac muscle (electrocardiogram – ECG)**, **brain neurons (electroencephalogram – EEG)**, and **skeletal muscles (electromyogram – EMG)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, electrode placement is treated as the *geometric front-end* of any biopotential measurement system. Without correct anatomical placement, even a perfectly designed amplifier will produce clinically meaningless, distorted, or non-reproducible signals. Hence, international bodies such as the **AHA (American Heart Association)**, **IFCN (International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology)**, and **ISEK (International Society of Electrophysiology and Kinesiology)** have codified placement protocols that KTU expects every student to memorize.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Concert-Hall Microphone" Picture

Imagine a 100-piece orchestra playing inside a huge concert hall. If you place a single microphone in the centre, you hear a *blurred mix* of every instrument — no individual violins, no solo cello. But if you hang microphones at **fixed, agreed-upon positions** — left of the conductor, above the strings, beside the brass — every microphone captures a **unique, reproducible "view"** of the same music.

The heart (and brain, and muscles) plays an electrical symphony continuously. **Electrodes are the microphones.** Their *placement* decides which portion of the electrical signal is amplified and which is rejected. Just as a sound engineer must know exactly where to put a mic, a biomedical engineer must know:

- **Where** to put the electrode (anatomical landmark),
- **How** to orient it (lead axis / vector),
- **What** reference to compare against (reference / ground electrode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardisation matters:** If a doctor in Delhi records an ECG with leads on the *left ankle*, and a doctor in London uses the *right wrist*, the two tracings will not be comparable. Hence, the **universal electrode placement conventions** exist — they are the *lingua franca* of clinical electrophysiology.

### 1.3 Why Electrode Placement is a *Module-3-Defining* Topic

In Module 3 of PEAET526, you transition from the **passive** electrode physics (half-cell potential, polarization, skin–electrode interface — covered earlier) to the **active clinical application** of those electrodes. The learning outcomes (COs) directly tied to this topic are:

| KTU Course Outcome | Bloom's Level | What this Topic Demands |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** – Apply electrode configurations to bioelectric signal acquisition | Apply / Analyze | Map anatomical landmarks → lead vectors |
| **CO4** – Design biopotential measurement systems | Design | Choose placement for ECG / EEG / EMG |
| **CO5** – Evaluate signal quality issues | Evaluate | Diagnose placement-induced artifacts |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Recall Block)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard ECG paper speed:** $v = 25$ **mm/s**
> - **Vertical calibration:** $1$ **mV = 10 mm** (i.e., sensitivity = **10 mm/mV**)
> - **EEG bandwidth:** **0.5 – 100 Hz**, sampling rate ≥ **256 Hz**
> - **ECG bandwidth:** **0.05 – 150 Hz** (diagnostic), **0.5 – 40 Hz** (monitoring)
> - **EMG bandwidth:** **20 – 500 Hz** (surface), **20 Hz – 5 kHz** (needle)
> - **Skin–electrode impedance (target):** **< 5 kΩ** at **10 Hz** for ECG
> - **Standard ECG body landmarks:** bony prominences to avoid muscle bulk

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ECG Electrode Placement — The 12-Lead System

The **12-lead ECG** is *not* 12 separate recordings. It is **10 electrodes** that mathematically generate **12 different "views"** (leads) of the heart's electrical activity. This is a classic KTU board-favourite concept.

#### 2.1.1 The Four Limb Electrodes (Foundational Set)

These four electrodes form a **tetrahedron around the torso** and define the limb leads:

| Electrode Label | KTU Standard Position | Skin Region |
|---|---|---|
| **RA** (Right Arm) | Just **below the right clavicle**, on the lateral chest wall, in the infraclavicular fossa | Right infraclavicular |
| **LA** (Left Arm) | Just **below the left clavicle**, symmetric to RA | Left infraclavicular |
| **LL** (Left Leg / Left Foot) | Midway between the **left costal margin and the left iliac crest**, on the anterior axillary line | Lower-left thoracoabdominal |
| **RL** (Right Leg / Right Foot) | Symmetric to LL on the **right** side — this is the **ground (earth) electrode** | Right lower thoracoabdominal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students wrongly place limb electrodes on the actual limbs (wrists and ankles). In modern ECG machines, the limb electrodes are placed on the **torso** (closer to the heart) to reduce motion artifacts. The *original* Einthoven placement was on the limbs, but the *current clinical* placement is on the torso. State both in your answer.

#### 2.1.2 Einthoven's Triangle & the Bipolar Limb Leads (I, II, III)

**Einthoven's Triangle** is the geometric abstraction formed by connecting the three *active* limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) at the vertices of an **equilateral triangle** with the heart at its centre.

The **bipolar limb leads** measure the *potential difference* between two limb electrodes:

$$ \text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA} $$

$$ \text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} $$

$$ \text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Identity (Einthoven's Law):**
>
> $$\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$$
>
> or equivalently,
>
> $$V_{LL} - V_{RA} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$
>
> This is a KTU **favourite 3-mark question**.

**Lead axes** (the direction each lead "sees" the heart's dipole):
- Lead I axis: **0°** (horizontal, leftward)
- Lead II axis: **+60°** (left-inferior)
- Lead III axis: **+120°** (right-inferior)

These three axes are **coplanar**, **120° apart**, and form a **2-D vector reference frame**.

#### 2.1.3 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (aVR, aVL, aVF)

A **unipolar lead** measures the potential at one *exploring* electrode relative to a **reference terminal** formed by *combining* the other two limb electrodes through equal high-value resistors (≈ **5 MΩ** each) — this combined point is called the **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)**.

$$ V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} $$

**Goldberger's augmentation** removes the exploring electrode's contribution from the reference, which **increases the signal amplitude by 50%**:

$$ \text{aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} $$

$$ \text{aVL} = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2} $$

$$ \text{aVF} = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2} $$

The augmented leads point **toward** their exploring electrode:
- **aVR** axis: **−150°** (right shoulder)
- **aVL** axis: **−30°** (left shoulder)
- **aVF** axis: **+90°** (foot)

Together with I, II, III, the six limb leads provide **six axes 60° apart** — the **hexaxial reference system** in the frontal plane.

#### 2.1.4 Precordial (Chest) Leads — V1 to V6

These are **unipolar leads** placed directly on the chest, measured against the Wilson Central Terminal. They view the heart in the **horizontal (transverse) plane**:

| Lead | Anatomical Landmark (KTU must-memorize) |
|---|---|
| **V1** | **4th intercostal space**, right sternal border |
| **V2** | **4th intercostal space**, left sternal border |
| **V3** | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| **V4** | **5th intercostal space**, **mid-clavicular line** |
| **V5** | Same horizontal level as V4, **anterior axillary line** |
| **V6** | Same horizontal level as V4, **mid-axillary line** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Confusing **mid-clavicular line** with **anterior axillary line**. The mid-clavicular line passes through the **nipple** (in males); the anterior axillary line passes along the **anterior fold of the axilla**. V4 is at the mid-clavicular line — V5 is at the anterior axillary line — V6 is at the mid-axillary line.

### 2.2 EEG Electrode Placement — The International 10–20 System

The **10–20 system** is the universal standard for EEG electrode placement. It is so named because electrodes are placed at **10%** and **20%** intervals along the scalp surface in both the **anterior–posterior** and **lateral** directions, measured from four anatomical landmarks:

1. **Nasion (Nz)** — bridge of the nose
2. **Inion (Iz)** — occipital protuberance
3. **Left pre-auricular point (LPA)**
4. **Right pre-auricular point (RPA)**

These four points define two perpendicular lines that cross at the vertex (**Cz**). Electrodes are placed at **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%** intervals along these lines.

#### 2.2.1 EEG Electrode Naming Convention

Every EEG electrode has a **Letter** (lobe) + **Number/Z-letter** (position/ midline) format:

| Letter | Lobe / Region |
|---|---|
| **Fp** | Fronto-polar (forehead) |
| **F** | Frontal |
| **C** | Central (over sensorimotor cortex) |
| **T** | Temporal |
| **P** | Parietal |
| **O** | Occipital |
| **A** | Auricular (ear reference) |

**Numbering Rules:**
- **Odd numbers → LEFT hemisphere**
- **Even numbers → RIGHT hemisphere**
- **"z" suffix (zero) → MIDLINE** (e.g., **Fz, Cz, Pz**)

#### 2.2.2 The 21 Standard Electrodes of the 10–20 System

```
Fp1   Fp2        (frontopolar, left & right)
  F7    F3    Fz    F4    F8      (frontal row)
      T3    C3    Cz    C4    T4  (central row)
        T5    P3    Pz    P4    T6 (parietal row)
            O1          O2         (occipital)
```

Plus reference electrodes: **A1 (left ear)** and **A2 (right ear)**.

Total: **21 active + 2 references = 21 electrodes** (A1 and A2 are often used as references).

> [!NOTE]
> For **high-density EEG (clinical & research)**, an extended **10–10 system** adds intermediate positions (e.g., **FC1, FC2, CP1, CP2, Fz, Pz**) increasing the count to **64, 128, or 256 electrodes**.

### 2.3 EMG Electrode Placement

#### 2.3.1 Surface EMG (sEMG)

Surface electrodes are placed **on the skin overlying the muscle belly**, parallel to the muscle fibre direction:

- **Active (recording) electrode**: over the **motor point** (mid-belly)
- **Reference electrode**: over a *bony prominence* nearby (low electrical activity) — typically **2–3 cm distal** along the tendon
- **Ground electrode**: on a *neutral* site (e.g., bony prominence far from the muscle, like the elbow for biceps)

#### 2.3.2 Needle EMG

Needle electrodes are inserted **directly into the muscle belly** at standardized anatomical sites, typically at the **motor point** identified via low-level voluntary contraction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Formula Table for Electrode Placement (Module 3)**

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Einthoven's Law | $\text{II} = \text{I} + \text{III}$ | Lead potentials in mV | ECG lead validation |
| 2 | Wilson Central Terminal | $V_{WCT} = \dfrac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{3}$ | Voltages in mV | Reference for V-leads |
| 3 | Augmented lead (aVR) | $aVR = V_{RA} - \dfrac{V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{2}$ | mV | Frontal-plane view |
| 4 | Augmented lead (aVL) | $aVL = V_{LA} - \dfrac{V_{RA}+V_{LL}}{2}$ | mV | Frontal-plane view |
| 5 | Augmented lead (aVF) | $aVF = V_{LL} - \dfrac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}}{2}$ | mV | Frontal-plane view |
| 6 | Lead vector projection | $V_L = \vec{H} \cdot \hat{a}_L \cos\theta$ | $V_L$ in mV, $\theta$ = angle between heart vector $\vec{H}$ and lead axis $\hat{a}_L$ | Heart-vector analysis |
| 7 | Hexaxial reference angle | $\theta_L = (n-1) \times 60°$ for $n = 1 \dots 6$ | Degrees | Frontal-plane lead axes |
| 8 | Skin-electrode impedance target | $Z_{se} < 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $10\,\text{Hz}$ | Resistance | ECG placement check |
| 9 | EEG 10–20 inter-electrode distance | $d_{ij} = 10\% \text{ or } 20\%$ of $L_{Nz-Iz}$ | cm / mm | Standardization |
| 10 | ECG paper calibration | $1\,\text{mV} = 10\,\text{mm}$ vertical; $v = 25\,\text{mm/s}$ horizontal | mm / mV·s | Time-voltage scaling |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Correct electrode placement underpins every clinical electrophysiology device you will ever design or use:

- **Holter monitors (24-h ECG)** — misplaced electrodes cause **false ischemia alarms**
- **ICU patient monitors** — incorrect lead placement can simulate **ventricular tachycardia**
- **BCI (Brain–Computer Interface)** systems — EEG cap placement errors directly reduce classifier accuracy
- **Rehabilitation robotics** — sEMG placement errors mis-trigger prosthetic limbs
- **Cardiac defibrillators** — paddle placement determines **transthoracic impedance** and shock success

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

**Statement to prove:** $\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$

**Starting point** — definitions of the three bipolar leads from §2.1.2:

$$ \text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA} $$

$$ \text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} $$

$$ \text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} $$

**Step 1** — Add Lead I and Lead III:

$$ \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) $$

**Step 2** — Expand and cancel the $+V_{LA}$ and $-V_{LA}$:

$$ = V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} $$

$$ = - V_{RA} + V_{LL} $$

$$ = V_{LL} - V_{RA} $$

**Step 3** — Compare with Lead II:

$$ V_{LL} - V_{RA} = \text{Lead II} \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form a *closed loop* — Kirchhoff's voltage law guarantees that the sum of potential differences around the loop is zero, which leads directly to Einthoven's identity. This is a KTU board-favourite derivation — expect it for 3–7 marks.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Goldberger's Augmented Lead Formula (aVR)

**Starting point** — Wilson Central Terminal:

$$ V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} $$

**Step 1** — The *unipolar* VR lead (without augmentation) would be:

$$ V_R = V_{RA} - V_{WCT} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{3} $$

**Step 2** — Simplify the bracket:

$$ V_R = \frac{3 V_{RA} - V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}}{3} = \frac{2 V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}}{3} $$

**Step 3** — Goldberger's augmentation *removes* the $V_{RA}$ contribution from the reference (by disconnecting it from WCT and using only LA and LL with equal resistors):

$$ V_{ref,aVR} = \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} $$

**Step 4** — The augmented lead is:

$$ aVR = V_{RA} - V_{ref,aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} $$

**Step 5** — Compare with the unipolar version — find the gain ratio:

$$ \frac{aVR}{V_R} = \frac{V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{2}}{\frac{2 V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}}{3}} $$

$$ = \frac{2 V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}}{2} \times \frac{3}{2 V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}} $$

$$ = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hence, Goldberger's augmentation produces a signal 1.5× (i.e., 50% larger) than the unipolar Wilson lead.** This is the **complete derivation** the KTU board expects.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lead Vector Projection of the Cardiac Dipole

The **heart vector** $\vec{H}$ represents the net electrical dipole of the heart at any instant. The voltage recorded by a lead is the *scalar projection* of $\vec{H}$ onto the lead's **unit vector** $\hat{a}_L$:

$$ V_L = \vec{H} \cdot \hat{a}_L = \vert \vec{H} \vert \cos\theta $$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{H}$ and $\hat{a}_L$.

**Step 1** — Resolve $\vec{H}$ into Cartesian components:

$$ \vec{H} = H_x \hat{i} + H_y \hat{j} $$

**Step 2** — Lead I axis is along the $+x$ direction (leftward): $\hat{a}_I = \hat{i}$, so:

$$ V_I = H_x $$

**Step 3** — Lead aVF axis is along the $+y$ direction (downward): $\hat{a}_{aVF} = \hat{j}$, so:

$$ V_{aVF} = H_y $$

**Step 4** — For Lead II, axis at $+60°$:

$$ \hat{a}_{II} = \cos 60° \hat{i} + \sin 60° \hat{j} = \frac{1}{2}\hat{i} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{j} $$

**Step 5** — Therefore:

$$ V_{II} = \vec{H} \cdot \hat{a}_{II} = \frac{1}{2}H_x + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}H_y = \frac{1}{2}V_I + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}V_{aVF} $$

This relation is the **foundation of vectorcardiography** — reconstructing the heart vector from any two perpendicular leads.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ECG Lead Summary

| Lead | Type | + Electrode | − Electrode | Axis | View |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | Bipolar | LA | RA | $0°$ | Lateral |
| II | Bipolar | LL | RA | $+60°$ | Inferior |
| III | Bipolar | LL | LA | $+120°$ | Inferior |
| aVR | Augmented unipolar | RA | (LA + LL)/2 | $-150°$ | Right shoulder |
| aVL | Augmented unipolar | LA | (RA + LL)/2 | $-30°$ | Left lateral |
| aVF | Augmented unipolar | LL | (RA + LA)/2 | $+90°$ | Inferior |
| V1 | Unipolar (WCT) | 4ICS, RSB | WCT | — | Septal |
| V2 | Unipolar (WCT) | 4ICS, LSB | WCT | — | Septal |
| V3 | Unipolar (WCT) | Midway V2–V4 | WCT | — | Anterior |
| V4 | Unipolar (WCT) | 5ICS, MCL | WCT | — | Apex |
| V5 | Unipolar (WCT) | 5ICS, AAL | WCT | — | Lateral |
| V6 | Unipolar (WCT) | 5ICS, MAL | WCT | — | Lateral |

*(ICS = intercostal space; RSB = right sternal border; LSB = left sternal border; MCL = mid-clavicular line; AAL = anterior axillary line; MAL = mid-axillary line.)*

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECG 12-Lead Placement on Human Torso

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FRONT["FRONTAL VIEW OF HUMAN TORSO"]
        direction TB
        RA["RA: Right infraclavicular fossa"]
        LA["LA: Left infraclavicular fossa"]
        RL["RL: Right thoracoabdominal - GROUND"]
        LL["LL: Left thoracoabdominal"]
        V1n["V1: 4th ICS, right sternal border"]
        V2n["V2: 4th ICS, left sternal border"]
        V3n["V3: Midway V2-V4"]
        V4n["V4: 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line"]
        V5n["V5: 5th ICS, anterior axillary line"]
        V6n["V6: 5th ICS, mid-axillary line"]
    end

    subgraph LIMBLEADS["BIPOLAR LIMB LEADS"]
        L1["Lead I = VLA - VRA"]
        L2["Lead II = VLL - VRA"]
        L3["Lead III = VLL - VLA"]
    end

    subgraph AUGLEADS["AUGMENTED LIMB LEADS"]
        AVR["aVR: views right shoulder -150 deg"]
        AVL["aVL: views left shoulder -30 deg"]
        AVF["aVF: views inferior 90 deg"]
    end

    subgraph PRECORD["PRECORDIAL CHEST LEADS"]
        PR["V1-V6 view horizontal plane via Wilson Central Terminal"]
    end

    RA --> L1
    LA --> L1
    RA --> L2
    LL --> L2
    LA --> L3
    LL --> L3

    RA --> AVR
    LA --> AVL
    LL --> AVF

    V1n --> PR
    V2n --> PR
    V3n --> PR
    V4n --> PR
    V5n --> PR
    V6n --> PR

    RL -.GROUND.-> FRONT
```

### 4.2 The Einthoven Triangle & Hexaxial Reference System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HEXAXIAL["HEXAXIAL REFERENCE FRAME - FRONTAL PLANE"]
        V1a["aVR: -150 deg"]
        V1b["Lead II: +60 deg"]
        V1c["aVL: -30 deg"]
        V1d["Lead III: +120 deg"]
        V1e["Lead I: 0 deg"]
        V1f["aVF: +90 deg"]
    end

    subgraph TRIANGLE["EINTHOVEN TRIANGLE"]
        RAx["RA vertex"]
        LAx["LA vertex"]
        LLx["LL vertex"]
        Hx["H: Cardiac dipole at centre"]
    end

    RAx --- LAx
    LAx --- LLx
    LLx --- RAx
    Hx -.position.-> TRIANGLE
```

### 4.4 EMG Surface Electrode Configuration (Over a Muscle)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EMG["sEMG ELECTRODE PLACEMENT OVER BICEPS BRACHII"]
        direction TB
        SK["Skin surface over muscle belly"]
        ACT["ACTIVE electrode - over motor point midpoint of muscle belly"]
        REF["REFERENCE electrode - on bony prominence 2-3 cm distal along tendon"]
        GND["GROUND electrode - on electrically neutral bony site e.g. elbow"]
        MP["Motor point: highest density of neuromuscular junctions"]
        MU["Muscle fibres oriented vertically"]
    end

    SK --> MP
    MP --> ACT
    MU --> ACT
    SK --> REF
    SK --> GND
    ACT --> MP
```

### 4.5 Skin-Electrode Interface at the Placement Site

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INTERFACE["CROSS-SECTION OF SKIN-ELECTRODE INTERFACE"]
        direction TB
        EL["Surface electrode - Ag/AgCl"]
        GEL["Electrolyte gel - conductive"]
        EP["Epidermis - stratum corneum outermost dead layer"]
        DER["Dermis - living tissue with blood vessels"]
        SUB["Subcutaneous tissue"]
        MUSC["Muscle - bioelectric source"]
    end

    EL --> GEL
    GEL --> EP
    EP --> DER
    DER --> SUB
    SUB --> MUSC
```
