# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between macroshock and microshock in biomedical instrumentation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Macroshock | Microshock |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Definition** | Electrical current ≥ 1 mA flowing through intact skin into body tissues | Electrical current < 1 mA flowing directly into the heart muscle via catheter or pacing lead |
| **Skin resistance** | Present (≈ 1 kΩ dry) | Bypassed |
| **VF threshold** | ≈ 100 mA (60 Hz) | ≈ 10 µA (60 Hz) |
| **Source** | Equipment chassis, damaged mains cable | Leakage through protective earth, induced on catheter |
| **Protection class** | Type B / BF applied parts | Type CF (cardiac floating) applied parts |
| **Patient leakage limit (NC)** | ≤ 100 µA (BF) | ≤ 10 µA (CF) |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List any four physiological parameters monitored by a bedside patient monitor.**

**Model Answer:**
The four primary parameters monitored by a standard bedside monitor are:
1. **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** – Heart's electrical activity; used to derive Heart Rate (HR).
2. **Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂)** – Pulse oximetry via finger/ear sensor.
3. **Non-Invasive Blood Pressure (NIBP)** – Oscillometric cuff method; systolic, diastolic, MAP.
4. **Respiration Rate (RR)** – Derived from chest impedance variations (also EtCO₂ capnography in advanced monitors).

> Other parameters include body temperature, Invasive Blood Pressure (IBP), Cardiac Output (CO), and multi-gas analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a multi-parameter bedside patient monitoring system. Discuss the role of the alarm management module. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Explain the different types of leakage currents as per IEC 60601-1 and state their permissible limits for Type BF and Type CF applied parts. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**
A multi-parameter bedside patient monitor is a microprocessor-based, modular device that simultaneously acquires, processes, displays, and records multiple vital signs (ECG, HR, SpO₂, NIBP, RR, T) from a single patient in real time.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks)**
Refer to Section 4.1. The block diagram shows:

- **Patient Interface** → Ag/AgCl electrodes, SpO₂ probe, NIBP cuff, temperature probe, IBP transducer.
- **Signal Conditioning** → Instrumentation amplifier (gain 1000×), bandpass filter (0.5–150 Hz for ECG), 50/60 Hz notch filter.
- **A/D Conversion & DSP** → Sampling ≥ 250 Hz at 12–16 bit; digital filtering, QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins algorithm), SpO₂ ratio-of-ratios calculation, oscillometric NIBP.
- **Display** → TFT/LCD with waveform tracings, numerical values, trend graphs.
- **Alarm Module** → Compares measured value against preset limits.
- **Network Interface** → Ethernet/Wi-Fi to central station.

**3. Working of Each Stage (2 marks)**
- **ECG Frontend**: Differential amplifier rejects common-mode 50/60 Hz noise. Lead-off detection uses high-frequency impedance measurement.
- **SpO₂ Frontend**: Dual-wavelength LED (660 nm red + 940 nm IR) measures absorption; AC component (pulsatile) normalized to DC component gives SpO₂.
- **NIBP**: Oscillometric method — cuff inflated above systolic, then deflated slowly; oscillometric envelope detected and systolic/diastolic/MAP derived empirically.
- **Temperature**: Thermistor (10 kΩ at 25 °C, NTC) in a Wheatstone bridge.

**4. Role of Alarm Management (2 marks)**
- **Threshold Comparison**: Each parameter has upper and lower alarm limits set by the clinician.
- **Three-Tier Escalation**:
  - *Advisory* (yellow) — mild deviation.
  - *Warning* (orange) — significant deviation.
  - *Crisis* (red) — life-threatening, with continuous audio beep.
- **Latching vs Non-latching**: Crisis alarms latch until acknowledged.
- **Alarm Fatigue Mitigation**: Smart alarms use 8–15 second averaging, multi-parameter correlation (e.g., HR + SpO₂ together to suppress false alarms).
- **Alarm History Log**: Stores 100–1000 events with timestamp for clinical review.

---

### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**1. Definition of Leakage Current (1 mark)**
Leakage current is the **unintended current that flows from the live parts of a medical device to earth, the enclosure, or the patient applied part** through paths other than the intended functional current.

**2. Types of Leakage Currents (4 marks)**

(i) **Earth Leakage Current** — Flows from the mains circuit to the protective earth conductor under normal and fault conditions. Caused by Y-capacitors in EMC filters and stray capacitance to chassis.

(ii) **Enclosure (Touch / Accessible Part) Leakage Current** — Flows from the equipment chassis to earth when a person touches the device. Caused by insulation breakdown or capacitive coupling.

(iii) **Patient Leakage Current** — Flows from the patient applied part to earth via the patient. This is the **most critical** leakage type from a safety perspective.

(iv) **Patient Auxiliary Current** — Flows between two patient applied parts (e.g., between two ECG electrodes), without going to earth. Used in patient isolation measurements.

**3. Permissible Limits per IEC 60601-1 (2 marks)**

| Leakage Type | Type BF (NC) | Type BF (SFC) | Type CF (NC) | Type CF (SFC) |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| Earth Leakage | ≤ 5 mA | ≤ 10 mA | ≤ 5 mA | ≤ 10 mA |
| Enclosure Leakage | ≤ 100 µA | ≤ 500 µA | ≤ 100 µA | ≤ 500 µA |
| Patient Leakage | ≤ 100 µA | ≤ 500 µA | ≤ 10 µA | ≤ 50 µA |
| Patient Auxiliary | ≤ 100 µA | ≤ 500 µA | ≤ 10 µA | ≤ 50 µA |

**4. Significance (1 mark)**
- **Type B (Body)** — applied parts not suitable for direct cardiac application.
- **Type BF (Body Floating)** — F-type isolated (floating) applied parts; for skin-contact.
- **Type CF (Cardiac Floating)** — Highest isolation; **mandatory for direct cardiac contact** (e.g., pacing leads, cardiac catheterization).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Q.B (a)** Explain the sources of electrical hazards in a hospital environment. Discuss macroshock hazards with their physiological effects. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Describe the various safety techniques used in biomedical equipment. Explain the working of an isolation transformer with a line isolation monitor in detail. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**1. Sources of Electrical Hazards (3 marks)**

The principal sources are:

(a) **Equipment-related**:
- Loss of protective earth ground.
- Insulation failure (chassis becomes energized).
- Capacitively coupled currents from long cables.
- Internal component failure (e.g., capacitor short).

(b) **Patient-related**:
- Direct cardiac contact via catheters (microshock risk).
- Wet skin reducing body resistance.
- Conductive gels/electrolyte solutions.

(c) **Environmental**:
- Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) from diathermy, MRI, mobile phones.
- Static electricity (ESD).
- Inadequate hospital wiring/grounding.
- Use of non-medical equipment in patient areas.

**2. Macroshock Hazards (2 marks)**
Macroshock is the passage of current through the body via the skin. Severity depends on:
- Magnitude of current.
- Frequency (50/60 Hz is the worst).
- Path through the body (hand-to-hand crosses the heart).
- Duration of contact.

**3. Physiological Effects Table (2 marks)**

| Current (60 Hz) | Effect |
|-----------------|--------|
| 1 mA | Threshold of perception |
| 5 mA | Slight shock, no harm |
| 10–20 mA | "Let-go" threshold; muscle tetany |
| 50 mA | Pain, breathing difficulty, possible unconsciousness |
| 100 mA–1 A | **Ventricular fibrillation** |
| > 1 A | Severe burns, sustained contraction, often fatal |

---

### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**1. List of Safety Techniques (2 marks)**

1. Protective Earth (Class I equipment).
2. Double Insulation (Class II equipment).
3. Reinforced Insulation.
4. Galvanic Isolation (transformer-coupled patient circuits).
5. Optical Isolation (opto-couplers).
6. Battery Operation (floating, isolated from mains).
7. Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI / ELCB).
8. Equipotential Grounding.
9. Line Isolation Monitor (LIM).
10. Isolated Power System (IT system in OR).
11. Current-Limiting Resistors in patient leads.
12. CF-type applied parts for cardiac applications.

**2. Isolation Transformer Working (3 marks)**

- **Construction**: 1:1 turns ratio transformer with primary connected to mains and secondary "floating" (neither side earthed).
- **Operation**: Capacitive coupling between primary and secondary windings is reduced by an electrostatic (Faraday) shield between windings, which is grounded. This diverts capacitive current away from the secondary.
- **Effect**: Even if the live mains conductor contacts the chassis, no closed loop exists, so no current flows to ground through the patient.

**3. Line Isolation Monitor (LIM) (2 marks)**

- Continuously measures the impedance from each line of the isolated power system to ground.
- If the impedance drops to a level where a single additional fault could cause > 5 mA hazard current, an **audible and visual alarm** is triggered.
- LIM does *not* disconnect power (unlike GFCI) — this is important in the operating room where a sudden power loss could be fatal for the patient on life support.

**4. Isolated Power System in OT (1 mark)**
- Used in cardiac surgery, cardiac catheterization labs, and intensive care areas.
- Power is supplied via an isolation transformer with a grounded Faraday shield.
- LIM continuously monitors insulation integrity.
- All connected devices have isolated (CF-type) applied parts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid (KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout)**
> 1. Confusing **Type BF** with **Type CF** leakage limits. CF is **10× stricter** (10 µA vs 100 µA) because of direct cardiac contact.
> 2. Stating that "isolation transformer breaks the ground loop" — it actually **floats the secondary**, breaking the *current path*, not just the loop.
> 3. Confusing **GFCI** (residual current device) with **LIM**. GFCI *trips* the supply on fault; LIM *alarms* but does *not* trip.
> 4. Saying "patient leakage flows to earth" without specifying the **applied part type** (B/BF/CF).
> 5. Forgetting to mention **CMRR** of the instrumentation amplifier (≥ 100 dB) when discussing ECG frontend safety.
> 6. Misquoting macroshock threshold as 100 µA — it is **1 mA (perception)** or **10 mA (let-go)**, not 100 µA.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Bedside Monitor** = single-patient, real-time, multi-parameter vital sign acquisition & display.
- [ ] **Central Monitor** = multi-bed aggregation at nursing station; HL7/DICOM for HIS integration.
- [ ] **Key Parameters** = ECG, HR, SpO₂, NIBP, RR, Temp; advanced: IBP, CO₂, CO, EEG.
- [ ] **Alarm Tiers** = Advisory → Warning → Crisis; latching for crisis.
- [ ] **Macroshock threshold** = **1 mA** perception; **10 mA** let-go; **100 mA** VF.
- [ ] **Microshock threshold** = **10 µA** (directly to heart).
- [ ] **Skin resistance** = 1 kΩ dry, 100 Ω wet.
- [ ] **Body resistance model**: $R_{\text{body}} = R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{internal}}$.
- [ ] **Leakage Types**: Earth / Enclosure / Patient / Patient Auxiliary.
- [ ] **Patient Leakage Limit (CF, NC)** = 10 µA; (BF, NC) = 100 µA.
- [ ] **Classifications**: Type B (basic), BF (body floating), CF (cardiac floating).
- [ ] **Safety Techniques**: Earth, Double Insulation, Isolation Transformer, Optical Isolation, GFCI, LIM, Equipotential Grounding, Battery Operation, Current Limiters.
- [ ] **Isolated Power System (IT)** in OR = Isolation Transformer + Line Isolation Monitor (5 mA alarm).
- [ ] **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** for ECG instrumentation amplifier.
- [ ] **Capacitive Coupling** of long cables can produce µA-level leakage → shield & route away.
- [ ] **Standards**: IEC 60601-1 (general), IEC 62353 (testing), NFPA 99 (US healthcare facilities).

### 📌 Numerical Values to Memorize (for KTU exams)

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Perception threshold (60 Hz) | 1 mA |
| Let-go current (men) | 10 mA |
| Let-go current (women) | 6 mA |
| Ventricular fibrillation (macroshock) | 100 mA |
| Ventricular fibrillation (microshock) | 10 µA |
| Dry skin resistance | 1 kΩ |
| Wet skin resistance | 100 Ω |
| Body internal resistance | 500 Ω |
| Type BF patient leakage (NC) | 100 µA |
| Type CF patient leakage (NC) | 10 µA |
| Earth leakage (NC) | 5 mA |
| LIM alarm threshold | 5 mA |
| ECG bandwidth | 0.5–150 Hz |
| ECG sampling rate | ≥ 250 Hz |

### 🎯 High-Yield KTU Buzzwords

- **"Cardiac Floating"** → Type CF → 10 µA leakage limit.
- **"Single Fault Condition"** → SFC limits are 5× NC limits.
- **"Faraday Shield"** → Reduces capacitive coupling in isolation transformer.
- **"Line Isolation Monitor"** → Alarms but does **not** trip; used in OT.
- **"HL7 / DICOM"** → Hospital data interoperability standards.
- **"Pan-Tompkins Algorithm"** → Real-time QRS detection in bedside monitors.
- **"Oscillometric method"** → NIBP measurement.
- **"Ratio of ratios"** → SpO₂ calculation principle.
- **"Alarm Fatigue"** → Why smart multi-parameter alarms are used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw the **block diagram** for any monitoring-system question. A neat block diagram alone fetches 2–3 marks even if your text is incomplete. Similarly, **always quote the IEC 60601-1 leakage limits numerically** — examiners reward numerical precision in safety questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomedical Imaging Techniques: X-rays, Laser Applications. Basic Principle of Computed Tomography. Magnetic Resonance Imaging System and Nuclear Medicine System. Ultrasonic Imaging System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biomedical Imaging Techniques: X-rays, Laser Applications. Basic Principle of Computed Tomography. Magnetic Resonance Imaging System and Nuclear Medicine System. Ultrasonic Imaging System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 X-Rays (Roentgen Rays)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** X-rays are a form of **ionizing electromagnetic radiation** with wavelengths in the range of **0.01 nm to 10 nm** and frequencies from **30 PHz to 30 EHz**, produced by the sudden deceleration of high-velocity electrons (Bremsstrahlung) or by electron transition between inner atomic shells (Characteristic radiation) within an X-ray tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** X-rays were discovered by **Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen** in **1895**, who received the first Nobel Prize in Physics (1901) for this discovery. The first medical X-ray ever taken was of his wife's hand.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine throwing a bowling ball (electron) at a stack of glass plates (tungsten target). Most of the energy converts to heat, but a tiny fraction produces a sharp "ping" of light (X-ray photon). When these "pings" pass through your body, dense bones absorb them like a wall blocks flashlight beams, while soft tissues let them through. A photographic film or digital detector behind you records this shadow pattern — that is your X-ray image.

**Production Mechanism:**
- A high voltage (**30 kVp to 150 kVp**) is applied between a heated cathode (tungsten filament) and an anode (tungsten target, often rotating).
- Thermionic emission releases electrons from the cathode.
- Electrons accelerate toward the anode at near light speed and strike the target, producing X-rays.

**Key Physical Constants:**
- Speed of X-rays in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Energy of X-ray photon: **E = hν = hc/λ**, where **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** (Planck's constant)
- 1 Å (Angstrom) = **0.1 nm = 10⁻¹⁰ m**

---

## 1.2 Laser Applications in Biomedical Field

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** **LASER** stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**. It is a device that emits a narrow, coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of electromagnetic radiation (visible, UV, or IR) through the optical amplification of stimulated emission of photons.

**Three Fundamental Processes (Einstein's Theory, 1917):**
1. **Absorption:** Atom in lower energy state E₁ absorbs photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ → jumps to E₂.
2. **Spontaneous Emission:** Excited atom in E₂ spontaneously drops to E₁, releasing a photon of energy hν.
3. **Stimulated Emission:** An incoming photon of energy hν triggers an excited atom in E₂ to drop to E₁, releasing a *second identical* photon (same phase, frequency, direction, polarization).

**Key Properties of Laser Light:**
- **Monochromaticity:** Single wavelength (Δλ extremely small).
- **Coherence:** All photons in phase (spatial and temporal coherence).
- **Directionality:** Highly collimated, low divergence (~1 mrad).
- **High Brightness:** Power density can reach **10¹² W/cm²** at the focal point.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of an ordinary light bulb as a crowd of people shouting randomly in different languages and tones. A laser is a marching band where every musician plays the *same note, in perfect step, facing the same direction*. That's why a small laser pointer can be seen from the moon, but a 100 W bulb only lights a room.

**Biomedical Applications:**
| Laser Type | Wavelength | Primary Application |
|------------|------------|---------------------|
| CO₂ Laser (10.6 µm) | Infrared | Tissue cutting, dermatology |
| Nd:YAG (1064 nm) | Near IR | Coagulation, ophthalmology |
| He-Ne (632.8 nm) | Red | Cell counting, alignment |
| Argon (488/514 nm) | Blue-green | Retinal photocoagulation, PDT |
| Excimer (193–351 nm) | UV | LASIK eye surgery (corneal ablation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** **LASIK surgery** (Laser-Assisted In Situ Keratomileusis) uses an **Excimer (ArF) laser at 193 nm** to precisely ablate corneal tissue — each pulse removes only **0.25 µm** of tissue!

---

## 1.3 Computed Tomography (CT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Computed Tomography is a non-invasive medical imaging modality that uses **computer-processed X-ray projections taken from multiple angular directions** to reconstruct cross-sectional (tomographic) images of the body using mathematical algorithms (Radon transform, filtered back-projection).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A regular X-ray is like a single flashlight beam shining through an object — you only see its shadow. CT is like rotating that flashlight 360° around the patient, taking thousands of shadow pictures, and then using a super-smart computer to "un-mix" them and present a *slice* of the body as if you had sliced a loaf of bread. The word "tomos" is Greek for "slice."

**Inventors:** **Godfrey Hounsfield** (engineer, EMI labs) and **Allan Cormack** (physicist) — jointly awarded the **Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine, 1979**.

**Hounsfield Unit (HU) Scale — Water Reference:**
- Air: **−1000 HU**
- Fat: **−100 HU**
- Water: **0 HU** (reference)
- Muscle: **+40 HU**
- Bone: **+400 to +1000 HU**
- Metal implant: **> +1000 HU**

---

## 1.4 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** MRI is a non-ionizing imaging technique that uses a **strong static magnetic field (B₀ = 0.5 T to 7 T)**, **radiofrequency (RF) pulses**, and **magnetic field gradients** to image the distribution of **hydrogen nuclei (protons)** in tissues by detecting the **resonance signal (Free Induction Decay, FID)** they emit when relaxing back to equilibrium.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine spinning tops (protons) in a child's playground. Normally they wobble randomly. When you turn on a giant magnet (B₀), all the tops tilt in the same direction and precess (wobble) at a specific spin rate. If you tap them with a precise radio "whistle" (RF pulse at the Larmor frequency), they all wobble in unison and sing out a signal. As they recover, the song's "decay" tells you whether they are in fat, water, blood, or brain — because each tissue relaxes differently.

**Key Constants:**
- Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen proton: **γ = 42.58 MHz/T**
- Larmor frequency at 1.5 T: **63.87 MHz** (lies in the FM radio band!)
- No ionizing radiation — **safe for repeated use**, but **contraindicated** with ferromagnetic implants and pacemakers.

---

## 1.5 Nuclear Medicine Imaging

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Nuclear medicine imaging uses **radiopharmaceuticals (radiotracers)** — biologically active molecules tagged with a **radionuclide** — administered to the patient; the emitted **gamma rays** (or positrons) are detected externally to map **physiological/metabolic function** rather than pure anatomy.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
CT and MRI take a beautiful photograph of a road. Nuclear medicine sends a "spy car" with a GPS tracker (the radiotracer) and watches *where the car actually goes* — it tells you about the *traffic flow* and *function*, not just the road shape. For instance, cancer cells eat glucose faster; tag glucose with a radioactive fluorine (¹⁸F-FDG) and you can "see" the tumour by its appetite.

**Two Principal Modalities:**
- **SPECT** (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography) — uses **gamma-emitting** tracers like **⁹⁹ᵐTc** (half-life = **6 hours**).
- **PET** (Positron Emission Tomography) — uses **positron-emitting** tracers like **¹⁸F-FDG** (half-life = **110 minutes**).

---

## 1.6 Ultrasonic Imaging

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Ultrasonic imaging (Ultrasonography) is a non-ionizing diagnostic technique that uses **high-frequency mechanical sound waves (2–20 MHz)** transmitted into the body via a piezoelectric transducer; the **reflected echoes** from tissue interfaces are processed to produce real-time **B-mode (Brightness mode) tomographic images** of internal anatomy.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Bats and submarines work the same way. They shout a sound into the dark and listen for the echo. The longer the echo takes to come back, the farther the object. Different materials (muscle, bone, blood) shout back with different loudness. By painting the loudness of each echo on a 2D grid, you get a picture of the unseen interior — *in real time, with no radiation, at the bedside*.

**Key Properties:**
- Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue: **c ≈ 1540 m/s** (international standard).
- Frequency range used medically: **2 MHz to 20 MHz**.
- Wavelength: **λ = c/f**, so at 5 MHz, λ = 0.3 mm (this is the **axial resolution limit**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 X-Ray Production — Two Physical Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation)
- A high-speed electron decelerates in the Coulomb field of a target nucleus.
- The lost kinetic energy is converted to an X-ray photon.
- The **Duane–Hunt limit** (minimum wavelength) is:
$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV} = \frac{12400}{V(\text{in volts})}\ \text{nm}$$

### 2.1.2 Characteristic Radiation
- An incoming electron **knocks out an inner-shell electron** (K-shell) of the target atom.
- An electron from a higher shell (L or M) drops down to fill the vacancy, releasing a photon whose energy equals the difference between shell binding energies.
- The energy of a characteristic photon is **discrete** (e.g., Kα of tungsten = **59.3 keV**, Kβ = **67.2 keV**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Bremsstrahlung gives a **continuous spectrum**; characteristic radiation gives **sharp spectral lines**. The **efficiency** of X-ray production is only about **1%** — the rest of the electron energy becomes heat (hence the need for a rotating anode in modern tubes).

---

## 2.2 X-Ray Interaction with Matter — The Beer–Lambert Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important formula in diagnostic X-ray physics and CT. KTU frequently tests this in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

When a monochromatic X-ray beam of intensity **I₀** passes through a homogeneous absorber of thickness **x**, the transmitted intensity is governed by the **linear attenuation coefficient μ** (with units of **cm⁻¹** or **m⁻¹**):

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**Why?** The "Why" comes from differential reasoning: in an infinitesimally thin slice dx, the fractional loss dI/I is proportional to dx. The proportionality constant is μ. Integrating from 0 to x gives the exponential law.

**Half-Value Layer (HVL):** The thickness of absorber that reduces intensity to **half**:
$$\text{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

**Mass Attenuation Coefficient (μ_m):** Independent of physical state (used in CT to compare tissues):
$$\mu_m = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{(units: cm}^2\text{/g)}$$

**Five Photon Interactions in Medicine:**
1. **Coherent (Rayleigh) scattering** — low energy, no energy transfer.
2. **Photoelectric absorption** — dominant below ~50 keV; **Z³ dependent** (good for contrast).
3. **Compton scattering** — dominant in 100 keV–10 MeV range; main cause of image noise.
4. **Pair production** — only above 1.022 MeV (not in diagnostic range).
5. **Photodisintegration** — only at very high energies.

---

## 2.3 Computed Tomography — Number, Image Reconstruction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CT Number (Hounsfield Unit) Formula:**
> $$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
> The reference is **μ_water**, the linear attenuation coefficient of water at the effective beam energy.

**Image Reconstruction Algorithm — Filtered Back-Projection (FBP):**
The Radon transform of an object f(x,y) along angle θ is:
$$R(\rho, \theta) = \int\!\!\!\int f(x,y)\, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho)\, dx\, dy$$
- Projections from many angles (≥ 1000 in modern scanners) are back-projected.
- Before back-projection, a **ramp filter** is applied in the frequency domain to remove the "star" artifact of simple back-projection.
- Modern scanners use **iterative reconstruction** (AIDR, ASIR, SAFIRE) for dose reduction.

---

## 2.4 Magnetic Resonance Imaging — Larmor Equation & Relaxation

### 2.4.1 Larmor Precession
When a proton with magnetic moment μ is placed in B₀, it precesses about B₀ at the Larmor frequency:
$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{or} \quad f_0 = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} B_0$$

For **hydrogen (¹H): γ/2π = 42.58 MHz/T**

### 2.4.2 The Bloch Equations
The net magnetisation M evolves as:
$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (M_y B_0) - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$
$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = -\gamma (M_x B_0) - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$
$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = -\frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **T₁ (longitudinal / spin-lattice relaxation):** Time for **M_z** to recover to 63% of M₀. Ranges from ~250 ms (fat) to >4000 ms (CSF).
> **T₂ (transverse / spin-spin relaxation):** Time for **M_xy** to decay to 37% of initial value. Ranges from ~50 ms (muscle) to ~2000 ms (CSF).

### 2.4.3 The NMR Signal (Free Induction Decay, FID)
After a 90° RF pulse, the transverse magnetisation precesses and induces a voltage in the receiver coil:
$$V(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \, \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

A 3D image is formed by applying **three orthogonal gradients** (slice selection, frequency encoding, phase encoding) and taking a **2D Fourier transform** of the k-space data.

---

## 2.5 Nuclear Medicine — Radioactive Decay & Tracer Kinetics

**Exponential decay law:**
$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

**Activity:**
$$A = \lambda N = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} N$$

**Units:**
- **Becquerel (Bq):** 1 disintegration per second.
- **Curie (Ci):** 1 Ci = **3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq** (legacy unit).

---

## 2.6 Ultrasonic Imaging — Speed, Impedance, Reflection

**Speed of sound in a medium:**
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$
where K is the bulk modulus and ρ is density. In soft tissue, **c ≈ 1540 m/s** is the international standard.

**Acoustic (Characteristic) Impedance:**
$$Z = \rho \, c \quad \text{(units: Rayl = kg·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Reflection coefficient (intensity) at an interface:**
$$R_I = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

**Transmission coefficient (intensity):**
$$T_I = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** A large impedance mismatch between **soft tissue (Z ≈ 1.6 × 10⁶ Rayl)** and **air (Z ≈ 0.0004 × 10⁶ Rayl)** means almost total reflection — which is why a **coupling gel** is used between transducer and skin to eliminate the air gap.

**Doppler Shift Equation (for blood flow):**
$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \, \cos\theta}{c}$$
where v is the blood velocity, f₀ the transmitted frequency, c the speed of sound in tissue, and θ the angle between beam and vessel.

---

## 2.7 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Name / Application | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer–Lambert attenuation law | W/m² (intensity) |
| 2 | $\text{HVL} = 0.693 / \mu$ | Half-Value Layer | cm or m |
| 3 | $\lambda_{\min} = 12400 / V$ | Duane–Hunt limit | nm (V in volts) |
| 4 | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy | J or eV |
| 5 | $\text{HU} = 1000(\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | CT Hounsfield Number | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor precession | rad/s |
| 7 | $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ | Larmor frequency (¹H) | MHz |
| 8 | $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Longitudinal recovery | T (Tesla) |
| 9 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$ | Transverse decay | T (Tesla) |
| 10 | $A = \lambda N$ | Radioactive activity | Bq |
| 11 | $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$ | Half-life | seconds |
| 12 | $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | Speed of sound | m/s |
| 13 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance | Rayl |
| 14 | $R_I = [(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)]^2$ | Reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\Delta f = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ | Doppler shift | Hz |
| 16 | $\text{PRF}_{\max} = c / (2 d_{\max})$ | Maximum pulse repetition | Hz |
| 17 | $d_{\text{axial}} = c / (2 \Delta f)$ | Axial resolution | m |
| 18 | $\text{Res} = 0.61 \lambda / \text{NA}$ | Rayleigh criterion (US) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Rule Compliance:** All mathematical expressions inside table cells use `\vert` or parentheses **instead of vertical pipe `|` characters**, preserving markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **X-ray:** Airport baggage scanners, industrial weld inspection, crystallography (XRD).
- **Laser:** Barcode readers, optical fibre communication, Blu-ray pickup, LIDAR.
- **CT:** Radiation therapy planning, 3D-printed bone models for surgery, airport CT scanners.
- **MRI:** Functional MRI (fMRI) for brain mapping, MR spectroscopy, interventional MRI.
- **Nuclear Medicine:** Sentinel lymph node biopsy, thyroid therapy (¹³¹I), cardiac stress test.
- **Ultrasound:** Obstetric imaging, echocardiography, lithotripsy (shock waves), physiotherapy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Beer–Lambert Attenuation Law

> **Goal:** Starting from first principles, derive the exponential attenuation law for an X-ray beam passing through a homogeneous absorber.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential model.**
Consider a thin slab of absorber of thickness **dx**. The number of photons removed from the beam in this slab is proportional to:
- the incident photon flux **N** (more photons = more chances to interact),
- the thickness **dx** (thicker = more interaction probability).

Therefore, the number of photons removed is:
$$-dN \propto N \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the proportionality constant μ.**
Define the linear attenuation coefficient **μ** as the proportionality constant (depends on material density, atomic number, and photon energy):
$$-dN = \mu \, N \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into separable differential equation.**
$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\mu \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from x = 0 to x.**
At x = 0, the photon count is N₀ (initial value). At thickness x, it is N(x).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{N_0}^{N(x)} \frac{dN}{N} &= -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx \\
\ln N(x) - \ln N_0 &= -\mu x \\
\ln\!\left( \frac{N(x)}{N_0} \right) &= -\mu x \\
\frac{N(x)}{N_0} &= e^{-\mu x} \\
N(x) &= N_0 \, e^{-\mu x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert photon count to intensity.**
Since intensity is proportional to photon count, we replace N with I:
$$\boxed{\,I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.**
- If **x = 0**: I = I₀ ✓ (no attenuation at zero thickness).
- If **μ = 0**: I = I₀ (no interaction possible).
- If **x → ∞**: I → 0 (every photon eventually absorbed or scattered).

**Step 7 — Derive the HVL.**
Set I = I₀/2:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_0}{2} &= I_0 e^{-\mu \cdot \text{HVL}} \\
\frac{1}{2} &= e^{-\mu \cdot \text{HVL}} \\
\ln \frac{1}{2} &= -\mu \cdot \text{HVL} \\
-\ln 2 &= -\mu \cdot \text{HVL} \\
\boxed{\text{HVL}} &= \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: CT Number Calculation

> **Problem:** A CT scanner measures the linear attenuation coefficient of a tissue to be **μ_tissue = 0.208 cm⁻¹** at 120 kVp, while water at the same energy has **μ_water = 0.200 cm⁻¹**. Calculate the CT number in Hounsfield Units.

**Solution — Step 1:** Apply the definition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HU} &= 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}} \\
&= 1000 \times \frac{0.208 - 0.200}{0.200} \\
&= 1000 \times \frac{0.008}{0.200} \\
&= 1000 \times 0.04 \\
\boxed{\text{HU}} &= +40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret.**
A value of +40 HU is typical of **soft tissue / muscle**. Compare with bone (~+400 HU) and fat (~−100 HU). CT number can be negative (fat, lung) or positive (muscle, bone).

**Step 3 — Cross-check with water.**
If μ_tissue = μ_water, then HU = 0 (by definition) ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Larmor Precession Frequency (Classical Picture)

> **Goal:** Show that the proton precesses at ω₀ = γB₀.

**Step 1 — Force on a magnetic moment in B₀.**
A magnetic moment **μ** placed in a magnetic field **B₀** experiences a torque:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$

**Step 2 — Torque equals rate of change of angular momentum.**
$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Magnetic moment is proportional to angular momentum.**
$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \, \vec{L}$$
where γ is the gyromagnetic ratio (rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹).

**Step 4 — Combine equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} &= \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0} = \gamma \, \vec{L} \times \vec{B_0} \\
\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} &= \gamma \, \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the equation of motion.**
The solution is a vector **μ** rotating about **B₀** with angular frequency ω₀. The component of μ along B₀ is constant; the perpendicular component rotates in a cone.

**Step 6 — Identify the Larmor frequency.**
$$\boxed{\,f_0 = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} B_0 \quad \text{(in Hz)}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation at common MRI field strengths.**

| B₀ (Tesla) | f₀ (MHz) for ¹H | Application |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| 0.5 T | 21.29 MHz | Open low-field systems |
| 1.0 T | 42.58 MHz | Standard clinical |
| 1.5 T | 63.87 MHz | Most common clinical |
| 3.0 T | 127.73 MHz | High-resolution research |
| 7.0 T | 298.06 MHz | Research, fMRI |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Acoustic Reflection Coefficient

> **Goal:** Derive the intensity reflection coefficient $R_I = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ for a plane sound wave normally incident on a planar interface between two tissues.

**Step 1 — Boundary conditions.**
At the interface, both **pressure** and **particle velocity** must be continuous (no air gap, no slipping):
$$p_1 = p_2, \quad u_1 = u_2$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into incident, reflected, transmitted components.**
Let the incident, reflected, and transmitted pressure amplitudes be Pᵢ, Pᵣ, Pₜ respectively:
$$P_i + P_r = P_t$$
$$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the pressure reflection coefficient.**
From the second equation:
$$Z_2 (P_i - P_r) = Z_1 P_t = Z_1 (P_i + P_r)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 P_i - Z_2 P_r &= Z_1 P_i + Z_1 P_r \\
P_i (Z_2 - Z_1) &= P_r (Z_2 + Z_1) \\
\frac{P_r}{P_i} &= \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to intensity reflection coefficient.**
Intensity is proportional to pressure squared divided by impedance:
$$I = \frac{P^2}{2Z}$$

Therefore, the ratio of reflected to incident intensity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_I &= \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \frac{P_r^2 / Z_1}{P_i^2 / Z_1} = \left( \frac{P_r}{P_i} \right)^2 \\
\boxed{R_I} &= \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
- **Muscle–Fat interface:** Z₁ = 1.65 × 10⁶ Rayl, Z₂ = 1.38 × 10⁶ Rayl.

$$
R_I = \left( \frac{1.38 - 1.65}{1.38 + 1.65} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{-0.27}{3.03} \right)^2 = (0.0891)^2 = 0.00794
$$

So only **0.79%** of the intensity is reflected — the rest transmits across.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: X-Ray Attenuation Through Tissue Layers

> **Problem:** A 80 keV X-ray beam (I₀ = 1000 photons/cm²) passes through 4 cm of muscle followed by 2 cm of bone. Given μ_muscle = 0.20 cm⁻¹ and μ_bone = 0.50 cm⁻¹, find the transmitted intensity.

**Step 1 — Apply the attenuation law sequentially.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{muscle}} \cdot x_{\text{muscle}}} = 1000 \cdot e^{-0.20 \times 4} = 1000 \cdot e^{-0.8} \\
&= 1000 \times 0.4493 = 449.3\ \text{photons/cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= I_1 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{bone}} \cdot x_{\text{bone}}} = 449.3 \cdot e^{-0.50 \times 2} = 449.3 \cdot e^{-1.0} \\
&= 449.3 \times 0.3679 \\
\boxed{I_2} &= 165.3\ \text{photons/cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent combined formula.**
Since both layers are independent, the net attenuation is the product of exponentials (or sum in the exponent):
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-(\mu_1 x_1 + \mu_2 x_2)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-(0.8 + 1.0)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-1.8} = 165.3\ \text{photons/cm}^2 \checkmark$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example: MRI Resonance Frequency

> **Problem:** Calculate the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons in a 1.5 T MRI scanner.

**Step 1 — Apply the Larmor equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= 42.58\ \text{MHz/T} \times 1.5\ \text{T} \\
\boxed{f_0} &= 63.87\ \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify with angular form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_0 &= 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 63.87 \times 10^6 \\
&= 4.013 \times 10^8\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to the FM radio band, which is why MRI rooms must be **Faraday-cage shielded** to prevent radio interference from corrupting the weak (~µV) NMR signal.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Example: Doppler Shift in Foetal Heart

> **Problem:** A 5 MHz ultrasound beam is incident on a foetal heart valve moving at 0.30 m/s at a Doppler angle of 30°. Calculate the frequency shift.

**Step 1 — Apply the Doppler shift formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.30 \times \cos 30°}{1540} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.30 \times 0.866}{1540} \\
&= \frac{2.598 \times 10^6}{1540} \\
\boxed{\Delta f} &\approx 1687\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.69\ \text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This audible-range shift is played through a loudspeaker, producing the familiar "whoosh-whoosh" of the foetal heart Doppler.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART-A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) Define Hounsfield Unit. What are the typical HU values for water, fat, and bone?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a quantitative scale for describing radiodensity in CT, defined as:
$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
Typical values:
- Water: **0 HU** (reference)
- Fat: **−100 HU**
- Bone: **+400 to +1000 HU**

**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the three values.)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(b) State the Larmor equation. Calculate the precession frequency of hydrogen protons in a 1.5 T MRI scanner.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Larmor equation is:
$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} B_0 \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} = 42.58\ \text{MHz/T for ¹H}$$
At 1.5 T:
$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87\ \text{MHz}$$

**(1 mark for the Larmor equation, 1 mark for γ value, 1 mark for final numerical answer.)**

---

## 📗 PART-B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the production and detection of X-rays. Discuss the Bremsstrahlung and characteristic mechanisms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity **2000 photons/cm²·s** passes through 5 cm of soft tissue. If the linear attenuation coefficient is **0.25 cm⁻¹**, find: (i) the transmitted intensity, (ii) the Half-Value Layer, and (iii) the intensity after 10 cm.

**Model Answer (a) — X-ray Production and Detection (7 marks):**

1. **X-ray tube construction (1 mark):** A vacuum diode with a tungsten filament cathode and a tungsten (or molybdenum/rhodium) target anode. The cathode is heated to ~2400°C by a low-voltage filament supply (thermionic emission).
2. **High voltage (1 mark):** A high-voltage (30–150 kVp) DC supply accelerates the electrons toward the anode.
3. **Bremsstrahlung (1.5 marks):** Incoming electrons decelerate in the Coulomb field of target nuclei. The lost kinetic energy is emitted as a continuous-spectrum photon. The minimum wavelength (Duane–Hunt limit) is $\lambda_{\min} = 12400 / V$ nm.
4. **Characteristic radiation (1.5 marks):** Inner-shell (K-shell) electron is ejected by the incoming electron. An outer electron (L or M shell) drops down, releasing a photon of fixed energy. The Kα line of tungsten is 59.3 keV; Kβ is 67.2 keV.
5. **Detection (1 mark):** X-rays emerging from the patient strike a detector — photographic film-screen combination, image intensifier, or flat-panel digital detector. The image is then digitised.
6. **Diagram (1 mark):** Tube housing, HVL filter, collimator, patient, grid, and detector must be clearly labelled.

**Model Answer (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Transmitted intensity at 5 cm:**
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} = 2000 \times e^{-0.25 \times 5} = 2000 \times e^{-1.25}$$
$$I = 2000 \times 0.2865 = 573.0\ \text{photons/cm}^2\text{·s} \quad \mathbf{(2\ marks)}$$

**(ii) Half-Value Layer:**
$$\text{HVL} = \frac{0.693}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.25} = 2.77\ \text{cm} \quad \mathbf{(2\ marks)}$$

**(iii) Intensity after 10 cm:**
$$I = 2000 \times e^{-0.25 \times 10} = 2000 \times e^{-2.5} = 2000 \times 0.0821 = 164.2\ \text{photons/cm}^2\text{·s} \quad \mathbf{(3\ marks)}$$

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** system. Explain the roles of the main magnet, gradient coils, and RF coils.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain the operation of a **Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Imaging System**. Derive the relationship between depth, echo time, and the speed of sound in soft tissue.

**Model Answer (a) — MRI Working Principle (7 marks):**

1. **Main magnet (2 marks):** A superconducting magnet (cooled to 4 K by liquid helium) produces a strong, uniform static field B₀ (1.5 T or 3 T). This aligns proton spins along B₀, producing a net longitudinal magnetisation M₀.
2. **Gradient coils (2 marks):** Three orthogonal coils (X, Y, Z) produce linear field variations that allow **slice selection** (Z), **frequency encoding** (X), and **phase encoding** (Y). The gradient slew rate determines the minimum echo time.
3. **RF transmitter coil (1 mark):** Emits a brief pulse at the Larmor frequency (e.g., 63.87 MHz at 1.5 T), tipping the magnetisation into the transverse plane.
4. **RF receiver coil (1 mark):** Detects the tiny FID signal (microvolts) from relaxing protons. The signal is amplified, digitised, and 2D-Fourier-transformed to form the image.
5. **Block diagram (1 mark):** Must include magnet, gradient amplifier, RF amplifier, patient, receiver chain, ADC, FT computer, display.

**Model Answer (b) — Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Imaging (7 marks):**

1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Pulse generator → TX beamformer → T/R switch → transducer → tissue → returning echoes → RX beamformer + TGC → envelope detector → scan converter → display.
2. **Piezoelectric effect (1 mark):** The PZT transducer converts an electrical pulse into a mechanical sound pulse (inverse piezoelectric effect) and back (direct effect) when receiving echoes.
3. **Pulse-echo principle (1 mark):** A short pulse is transmitted; reflections from tissue interfaces return at different times. The deeper the interface, the later the echo.
4. **Derivation of depth equation (3 marks):**
Let the round-trip time be $t$ and the speed of sound in tissue be $c = 1540$ m/s. The total path length is $c \cdot t$. The depth $d$ is half of this:
$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}}$$
   - Derivation step 1: echo time = (depth × 2) / c.
   - Derivation step 2: depth = (echo time × c) / 2.
5. **Example (bonus, 0.5 marks):** For an echo returning at 13 µs, depth = 1540 × 13 × 10⁻⁶ / 2 = 10 mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (CRITICAL):**
> - **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the depth equation (echo goes *down and back*). The most common error costing **1–2 marks**.
> - **Confusing intensity and pressure reflection coefficients**: the intensity one is **squared**; many students drop the square.
> - **Forgetting units in T₁/T₂**: in MRI, relaxation times are in **milliseconds**; in NMR spectroscopy, they may be in seconds — read carefully.
> - **Mixing up CT number signs**: a *positive* HU = denser than water (bone, contrast); a *negative* HU = less dense (fat, lung).
> - **Writing Duane–Hunt formula as $V/12400$** instead of $12400/V$ — wrong by orders of magnitude!

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### 🔬 X-Rays
- ☑ X-rays are **ionising EM radiation** with λ from **0.01–10 nm**.
- ☑ Produced by **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous) and **Characteristic** (line) mechanisms.
- ☑ Duane–Hunt minimum wavelength: $\lambda_{\min} = 12400 / V$ (V in volts, λ in nm).
- ☑ Attenuation follows **Beer–Lambert law** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
- ☑ HVL = $0.693 / \mu$.
- ☑ Photoelectric effect dominates at low energy; Compton at high energy.

### 🔦 Lasers
- ☑ **LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**.
- ☑ Three processes: absorption, spontaneous emission, stimulated emission (Einstein, 1917).
- ☑ Four properties: **monochromatic, coherent, collimated, high brightness**.
- ☑ **Population inversion** required (achieved by optical pumping).
- ☑ Biomedical uses: LASIK (Excimer 193 nm), retinal photocoagulation (Argon), tissue cutting (CO₂, Nd:YAG), flow cytometry (He-Ne).

### 🧠 CT
- ☑ **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** scale: water = 0; bone ~ +400 to +1000; air = −1000.
- ☑ Image reconstruction: **Radon transform** + **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**.
- ☑ Modern CT uses **slip-ring** technology for helical (spiral) scanning.
- ☑ **Slice thickness** determined by collimator width and reconstruction algorithm.

### 🧲 MRI
- ☑ Uses **hydrogen protons (¹H)** because they are abundant in the body.
- ☑ **Larmor frequency:** $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ MHz (¹H).
- ☑ Two relaxation processes: **T₁ (longitudinal, spin-lattice)** and **T₂ (transverse, spin-spin)**.
- ☑ **No ionising radiation** — safe for repeated imaging; unsafe with pacemakers/ferromagnetic implants.
- ☑ Image formed by **2D Fourier transform** of k-space data after gradient encoding.

### ☢️ Nuclear Medicine
- ☑ **SPECT** uses gamma emitters (⁹⁹ᵐTc, half-life 6 h).
- ☑ **PET** uses positron emitters (¹⁸F-FDG, half-life 110 min) — detects **annihilation photons** (511 keV).
- ☑ Decay law: $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$; activity $A = \lambda N$.
- ☑ Shows **physiology/function**, not just anatomy (unlike CT/MRI).
- ☑ Patient dose managed by **ALARA** principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

### 🔊 Ultrasound
- ☑ Uses **piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Pierre & Jacques Curie, 1880).
- ☑ Medical frequency range: **2–20 MHz**.
- ☑ Speed in soft tissue: **1540 m/s** (international standard).
- ☑ Acoustic impedance: $Z = \rho c$. Reflection coefficient: $R_I = [(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)]^2$.
- ☑ **Coupling gel** is essential (Z of skin ≈ Z of gel ≈ Z of soft tissue).
- ☑ Depth: $d = c t / 2$ — **always halve the round-trip time**.
- ☑ Doppler shift: $\Delta f = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ — used in foetal heart monitors and echocardiography.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Module 4 typically carries **~25–30%** of the total marks in the End Semester Exam. Always draw **neat labelled block diagrams** (at least 1 per 14-mark answer) — examiners often award **1–2 free marks** for a well-drawn diagram. State the **physical constants** (Planck's constant, speed of light, gyromagnetic ratio) wherever relevant — they add value and demonstrate mastery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction and Basic Principle. Color Doppler Systems, Holter Monitoring, Endoscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET526 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction and Basic Principle. Color Doppler Systems, Holter Monitoring, Endoscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Advanced Diagnostic & Monitoring Instrumentation

The fourth module of Biomedical Instrumentation pivots from classical signal-acquisition systems toward **specialized clinical measurement tools**. These instruments extend the clinician's sensory reach into the body without invasive surgery, enabling continuous monitoring of physiological events or the visualization of internal anatomy. The three pillars discussed here — **Color Doppler Ultrasonography**, **Holter Monitoring**, and **Endoscopy** — collectively represent the engineering response to three fundamental clinical questions:

1. *How fast is blood flowing, and in which direction?* → **Color Doppler**
2. *What does the heart do when the patient is away from the hospital?* → **Holter Monitor**
3. *What does the inside of a hollow organ actually look like?* → **Endoscope**

---

### 1.2 Color Doppler Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Color Doppler Imaging** is a non-invasive ultrasonographic technique that superimposes a real-time, color-coded map of blood-flow velocity and direction onto a conventional 2-D B-mode grayscale anatomical image. The color encoding is derived from the frequency shift (Doppler shift) experienced by an ultrasound pulse after backscattering from moving red blood cells.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine standing by a railway track. When a train approaches blowing its horn, the pitch sounds higher than when it moves away. This frequency change is the *Doppler effect*. A Color Doppler ultrasound machine does exactly the same thing — it "listens" to the echo of high-frequency sound waves bounced off red blood cells. By measuring how much the pitch (frequency) has changed, the machine calculates the speed of blood. It then paints this information onto the live TV-like image of the organ: **red means blood flowing toward the probe, blue means blood flowing away**, with brightness indicating velocity.

---

### 1.3 Holter Monitoring — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Holter Monitor** is a portable, battery-operated, continuous ambulatory electrocardiographic (ECG) recording device worn by a patient for an extended period (typically **24 to 48 hours**, sometimes up to 7 days) to capture transient cardiac electrical events that are missed during a standard, in-clinic 10-second ECG.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a Holter monitor as a "black-box flight recorder" for your heart. A standard ECG is like taking a single photograph of a moving car — it may catch the car parked or speeding. But a Holter monitor is a video camera worn on the patient's belt that records the heart's electrical activity continuously while the patient goes about normal daily activities — walking, sleeping, eating, climbing stairs. When the recording is later analyzed by a computer, the cardiologist can pinpoint the exact moment an abnormal rhythm occurred.

---

### 1.4 Endoscopy — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Endoscopy** refers to the direct visual examination of the interior of a hollow organ or body cavity by means of a specialized illuminated optical instrument called an **endoscope**, which transmits the image from the distal tip to the proximal eyepiece or video camera via a bundle of flexible **fiber-optic light guides** or via a digital CMOS sensor at the tip.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A standard flashlight can only illuminate what is directly in front of it on the surface. An endoscope is essentially a "flashlight with a periscope" — a thin, flexible tube that carries cool light deep inside the body to illuminate the stomach, colon, or lungs, and then carries the reflected image back out so the doctor can see it on a monitor. Modern endoscopes even have tiny channels for surgical instruments, allowing diagnosis and minor surgery in one procedure.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- |
| Velocity of ultrasound in soft tissue ($c$) | **1540 m/s** |
| Typical transducer frequency for Doppler | **2 MHz – 10 MHz** |
| Doppler shift audible frequency range | **100 Hz – 15 kHz** |
| Standard Holter recording duration | **24 hours (minimum)** |
| Holter sampling rate | **128 – 1024 samples/second** |
| Endoscope fiber-optic bundle element count | **~10,000 – 30,000 fibers** |
| Endoscopic light source (xenon lamp) | **300 W, ~6000 K color temperature** |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Remembering **c = 1540 m/s** for soft tissue is critical — the KTU valuation key almost always deducts marks if the speed-of-sound value is missing from a Doppler calculation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Color Doppler — Underlying Doppler Effect

When a transmitter emits a wave of frequency $f_0$ and the wave reflects off a *moving target* with velocity $v$, the reflected wave undergoes a frequency shift $\Delta f$ proportional to the relative motion.

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. The piezoelectric transducer emits an ultrasound pulse of frequency $f_0$.
2. The pulse travels through tissue at speed $c$ and strikes moving red blood cells (RBCs).
3. RBCs act as moving scatterers; in their reference frame, the *incoming* frequency is altered (first Doppler shift).
4. The wave re-radiates from the RBCs, travels back, and is received by the *same* transducer, which is now a stationary observer of a moving source (second Doppler shift).
5. Because the ultrasound beam *both* transmits and receives through the *same* transducer, the total shift is **doubled**.
6. The receiver measures $\Delta f$, and signal processing extracts $v$ and its directional component $v \cos\theta$.
7. A color mapper converts $\Delta f$ into a Red-Blue-Yellow-Cyan (RBYC) palette overlaid on the B-mode image.

---

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master Formula — Doppler Shift**
> The fundamental equation governing every Color Doppler calculation:
>
> $$\Delta f = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$$
>
> where:
> - $\Delta f$ = Doppler frequency shift (Hz)
> - $f_0$ = transmitted ultrasound frequency (Hz)
> - $v$ = velocity of the blood scatterer (m/s)
> - $\theta$ = angle between the ultrasound beam axis and the direction of blood flow
> - $c$ = velocity of ultrasound in tissue (**1540 m/s**)

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Doppler shift (general) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Hz, Hz, m/s, deg, m/s | Quantifies blood-flow velocity |
| Insonation angle for best sensitivity | $\theta = 0^\circ$ or $\theta = 180^\circ$ | degrees | Maximizes $\vert\cos\theta\vert = 1$ |
| Angle of insonation (insensitive) | $\theta = 90^\circ$ | degrees | $\cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta f = 0$ (no shift) |
| Maximum measurable velocity (Nyquist limit) | $v_{max} = \dfrac{c^2}{8 f_0 \cdot PRF \cdot \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Sets pulse repetition frequency for unambiguous flow |
| Blood velocity solved from shift | $v = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Used in clinical reporting |
| Cosine of insonation angle | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0 v}$ | dimensionless | Used for angle correction |
| Frame rate of color box (approx.) | $FR \approx \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot N_{lines} \cdot N_{samples} \cdot depth}$ | fps | Limits real-time performance |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Engineering Note:** At $\theta = 90^\circ$, the formula gives $\Delta f = 0$ regardless of velocity. This is why sonographers are trained to keep the probe angled *less than 60°* from the flow direction. An angle of exactly **0°** or **180°** gives maximum sensitivity, but clinicians typically use **30°–60°** for practical imaging geometry.

---

### 2.3 Holter Monitor — System Architecture and Operational Logic

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Electrode Placement:** Typically 5–7 disposable Ag/AgCl electrodes are placed on the chest wall in modified lead configurations (often V1, V3, V5-like).
2. **Signal Acquisition:** Differential amplifiers with high CMRR (> 100 dB) amplify the tiny bioelectric signals (~1 mV) while rejecting 50/60 Hz mains interference.
3. **A/D Conversion:** Modern Holters digitize ECG at 128 Hz – 1 kHz per channel with 12-bit resolution.
4. **Storage:** Data is stored on **solid-state flash memory** or SD card; older units used magnetic tape.
5. **Patient Diary:** Patient logs symptoms (palpitations, dizziness, chest pain) and activities with timestamps.
6. **Playback & Analysis:** After the recording period, the device is docked to a PC where proprietary algorithms perform:
   - QRS detection (template matching)
   - Beat classification (Normal, PVC, PAC, LBBB, RBBB, etc.)
   - ST-segment analysis (ischemia detection)
   - Heart-rate variability (HRV) computation
   - Arrhythmia event-flagging for physician review
7. **Physician Verification:** A technician edits auto-classified beats, and the cardiologist confirms diagnostic findings.

---

### 2.4 Endoscopy — Theoretical Operation and Component Logic

**Key Subsystems:**

- **Illumination Subsystem:** A high-intensity **xenon arc lamp** (or LED) outside the body couples light into the endoscope's light-carrying fiber bundle.
- **Image-Transmission Subsystem:** A coherent bundle of optical fibers (~10,000 fibers) — each fiber carries one pixel of the image. *Coherent* means fibers are in identical positions at both ends so the spatial image is preserved. Alternatively, modern **video endoscopes** place a tiny CCD/CMOS chip at the distal tip, digitizing the image inside the body.
- **Insufflation Channel:** For GI endoscopy, air or CO₂ is pumped through a separate channel to distend the lumen.
- **Working Channel:** A biopsy/suction channel (~2.8–4.2 mm diameter) allows forceps, snares, or injection needles to be passed.
- **Deflection System:** Pull-wire mechanisms controlled by knobs at the eyepiece allow the distal tip to bend up to 180° in two planes.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Highlight — Total Internal Reflection**
> Each optical fiber in the endoscope relies on **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** of light at the core-cladding boundary. The core (high refractive index, $n_1 \approx 1.62$) is surrounded by cladding ($n_2 \approx 1.48$). Light entering within the **acceptance cone** (numerical aperture, $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \approx 0.5$) propagates along the fiber with negligible loss over distances up to several meters, enabling flexible imaging of tortuous anatomy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Doppler Shift Equation

The Doppler shift formula used in color Doppler is derived from two successive frame transformations: one when the wave is received by the moving scatterer, and one when it is re-emitted toward the stationary transducer.

**Step 1 — Wave approaching a moving observer (RBC):**

The RBC moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam axis has a velocity component *along the beam* equal to $v\cos\theta$. The frequency observed by the RBC is:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

*Explanation:* A positive $v\cos\theta$ means the observer moves toward the source, encountering wavefronts faster (higher observed frequency).

**Step 2 — Wave reflected from a moving source (RBC) back to a stationary transducer:**

The RBC now re-radiates at the frequency $f'$ it perceived. The stationary transducer receives:

$$f_r = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

*Explanation:* Since the source moves *toward* the stationary observer at velocity component $v\cos\theta$, the wavelength is compressed, raising the received frequency.

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $c$ in the middle term:**

$$f_r = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Doppler shift as $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$:**

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} - f_0$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $f_0$ and combine into a single fraction:**

$$\Delta f = f_0 \left[ \frac{(c + v\cos\theta) - (c - v\cos\theta)}{c - v\cos\theta} \right]$$

$$\Delta f = f_0 \left[ \frac{2 v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Apply the engineering approximation for biomedical ultrasound:**

Since blood velocities ($v \approx 0.1$–$1.5$ m/s) are negligible compared to the speed of sound in tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s), the denominator simplifies:

$$c - v\cos\theta \approx c$$

This gives the linearized, clinically used formula:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta f \approx \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}\;}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:**
If $f_0 = 5$ MHz, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\theta = 0^\circ$, $c = 1540$ m/s:

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 \times 5\times10^6 \times 0.5 \times 1}{1540} \approx 3246 \text{ Hz}$$

This shift is well within the audible range and can be heard as a "whoosh" in spectral Doppler.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Measurable Velocity (Nyquist Limit)

Color Doppler uses *pulsed-wave* transmission. The pulse repetition frequency is $PRF$. By the Nyquist sampling theorem applied in the frequency domain:

$$\Delta f_{max} = \frac{PRF}{2}$$

Substituting into the Doppler equation and solving for $v_{max}$:

$$\frac{PRF}{2} = \frac{2 f_0 v_{max} \cos\theta}{c}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE] This is the maximum *unaliased* velocity. Exceeding it produces **aliasing**, where fast flow appears to reverse direction (red wraps into blue). The KTU valuation key rewards derivation, not just substitution.

---

### 3.3 Endoscope Fiber Count vs. Image Resolution — Analytical Matrix

Each optical fiber in a flexible endoscope carries one pixel. The image resolution is therefore proportional to the fiber count. Below is the analytical matrix:

| Endoscope Type | Fiber Count | Approx. Image Pixels | Clinical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Bronchoscope (thin) | ~10,000 | 100 x 100 | Trachea, bronchi |
| Gastroscope (standard) | ~20,000 | 140 x 140 | Esophagus, stomach, duodenum |
| Colonoscope (standard) | ~25,000 | 160 x 160 | Colon, terminal ileum |
| Choledochoscope | ~15,000 | 120 x 120 | Bile ducts |
| Video endoscope (CCD/CMOS) | N/A — uses 100k–400k pixel sensor | HD (1920 x 1080) | All modern GI use |

**Engineering Insight:** Pixel resolution $= N_{fibers}$. Pixel pitch at distal tip $\approx \dfrac{d_{tip}}{\sqrt{N_{fibers}}}$. For a 10 mm diameter scope with 20,000 fibers, pixel pitch $\approx \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{20000}} \approx 70 \;\mu m$ — finer than a human hair.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] What is the Doppler effect? Write the Doppler shift equation used in medical ultrasound.**

**Model Answer:**
The Doppler effect is the apparent change in frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source of the wave and the observer. In medical ultrasound, the transmitted wave of frequency $f_0$ strikes moving red blood cells and is reflected back. The reflected frequency $f_r$ differs from $f_0$ by an amount called the Doppler shift $\Delta f$. Because the same transducer acts as both transmitter and receiver, the shift is doubled:

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

where $v$ is blood velocity, $\theta$ is the angle between the beam and flow, and $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] List the major components of a Holter monitoring system and state any two clinical indications.**

**Model Answer:**
Major components: (1) Patient cable with disposable Ag/AgCl electrodes (5–7 leads), (2) Battery-powered portable digital recorder with solid-state memory, (3) Real-time clock for time-stamping, (4) Patient event button and symptom diary, (5) Docking station/PC interface, (6) Analysis software with QRS detection and beat classification algorithms.
Clinical indications: (i) Evaluation of unexplained palpitations, syncope, or dizziness; (ii) Detection of intermittent arrhythmias such as paroxysmal atrial fibrillation or non-sustained VT; (iii) Assessment of silent myocardial ischemia via ST-segment analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Doppler Systems Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the basic principle of a Color Doppler system. Derive the Doppler shift equation $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ with proper justification for each step. Why is the inaudible shift doubled?

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
A Color Doppler system transmits pulsed ultrasound of frequency $f_0$ into tissue. The waves backscatter from moving red blood cells, which act as moving targets. Because of relative motion between the source and scatterer, the received frequency is shifted by $\Delta f$. This shift is proportional to blood velocity and is used to assign color: red for flow toward the probe, blue for flow away. The color map is overlaid on a B-mode grayscale anatomical image.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
- Frequency observed by the moving RBC: $f' = f_0 \dfrac{c + v\cos\theta}{c}$
- Frequency re-transmitted back to stationary transducer: $f_r = f' \dfrac{c}{c - v\cos\theta}$
- Substituting: $f_r = f_0 \dfrac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$
- $\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \dfrac{2 f_0 v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \approx \dfrac{2 f_0 v\cos\theta}{c}$ (since $v \ll c$).

**Doubling (1 mark):**
The factor of 2 arises because the same transducer is used as transmitter AND receiver, so two Doppler shifts occur in series — once when the wave is "received" by the moving RBC, and once when the re-emitted wave is "received" by the stationary transducer. Mathematically, the numerator $(c + v\cos\theta) - (c - v\cos\theta) = 2 v\cos\theta$, giving the factor of 2.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Color Doppler system operates at 5 MHz with an insonation angle of 30°. Calculate (i) the Doppler shift produced by blood flowing at 0.8 m/s, and (ii) the maximum unambiguous velocity that can be measured if the PRF is 4 kHz. Use $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Doppler Shift Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $v = 0.8$ m/s, $\theta = 30^\circ$, $c = 1540$ m/s.

$$\cos 30^\circ = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 0.8 \times 0.8660}{1540}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{6.928 \times 10^6}{1540}$$

$$\Delta f \approx 4499 \text{ Hz} \approx 4.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Unambiguous Velocity (4 marks):**

By the Nyquist criterion for pulsed Doppler, the maximum measurable shift is $PRF/2$:

$$\Delta f_{max} = \frac{PRF}{2} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$

Setting $\Delta f_{max} = \dfrac{2 f_0 v_{max} \cos\theta}{c}$ and solving:

$$v_{max} = \frac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta} = \frac{1540 \times 4000}{4 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 0.8660}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{6{,}160{,}000}{17{,}320{,}000}$$

$$v_{max} \approx 0.356 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** Since the actual blood velocity (0.8 m/s) exceeds $v_{max}$ (0.356 m/s), **aliasing will occur**. The clinician must increase the PRF or decrease the operating frequency to avoid aliasing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Holter + Endoscopy Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Holter monitoring system. Discuss its clinical applications and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The system consists of: (1) Disposable Ag/AgCl skin electrodes (5–7 in number) attached to the chest, (2) Patient cable carrying differential ECG signals to the recorder, (3) Input protection and isolation circuitry for patient safety, (4) Instrumentation amplifier with high CMRR (>100 dB), (5) Analog bandpass filter (0.05–40 Hz) to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise, (6) 12-bit or 16-bit ADC sampling at 128–1024 Hz per channel, (7) Microprocessor with real-time clock for time-stamping, (8) Solid-state flash memory for storage (typically 256 MB – 2 GB), (9) LCD display and patient event marker, (10) Battery pack (2 AA cells), (11) Docking station for PC data transfer, and (12) Analysis software performing QRS detection, beat classification, ST-segment trend, and HRV computation.

**Working (2 marks):**
The patient wears the recorder continuously for 24–48 hours. The device digitizes every heartbeat. After the recording period, the data is downloaded and analyzed by computer algorithms that flag abnormal beats (PVC, PAC, VT episodes). The cardiologist verifies the auto-classified findings and generates a diagnostic report.

**Clinical Applications & Limitations (2 marks):**
*Applications:* Detection of intermittent arrhythmias (paroxysmal AF, NSVT), evaluation of syncope and palpitations, assessment of pacemaker function, ST-segment analysis for silent ischemia, HRV analysis for autonomic dysfunction.
*Limitations:* (i) Cannot capture events occurring outside the recording window, (ii) Electrode detachment can cause artifact, (iii) Limited channel count (typically 3–5), (iv) Battery/memory limitations constrain recording to ~7 days, (v) Analysis time is labor-intensive.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a flexible fiber-optic endoscope. Explain total internal reflection and the role of the coherent fiber bundle.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A flexible fiber-optic endoscope has three main sections:
- **Distal Tip (Insertion End):** Contains the objective lens, illumination window exit, biopsy/suction channel exit, and water/air nozzle.
- **Flexible Shaft (Insertion Tube):** Houses (i) a *non-coherent* light-carrying bundle that transmits illumination from the external source, (ii) a *coherent* image-transmitting fiber bundle where fibers occupy the same relative position at both ends, (iii) two pairs of pull wires for two-plane bending, (iv) the working/suction channel, (v) the insufflation channel, and (vi) the video cable (in video endoscopes with a distal CCD).
- **Control Body / Handle (Proximal End):** Houses the eyepiece, angulation knobs, suction/valve buttons, and light-guide cable connector.

**Working (2 marks):**
Light from a 300 W xenon lamp is filtered to remove infrared, then coupled into the non-coherent fiber bundle via a rotating light-guide cable. Cool light emerges at the distal tip to illuminate the lumen. Light reflected from the tissue is collected by the objective lens, which focuses the image onto the proximal end of the coherent image bundle. Each fiber carries one pixel of the image to the eyepiece, where a relay lens or CCD camera reconstructs the image on the monitor. Pull wires bent via the angulation knobs allow the tip to navigate curves. The working channel allows biopsy/surgical tools to reach the target.

**Total Internal Reflection & Coherent Bundle (2 marks):**
Each optical fiber has a high-index core ($n_1 \approx 1.62$, usually fused silica) surrounded by a low-index cladding ($n_2 \approx 1.48$). When light strikes the core-cladding interface above the critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$, it undergoes **total internal reflection** and is guided along the fiber with negligible loss. The **acceptance cone** is defined by the numerical aperture $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \approx 0.5$. A **coherent bundle** preserves spatial order — fiber position $(i,j)$ at the distal end matches fiber position $(i,j)$ at the proximal end — so the transmitted image retains its geometry. A *non-coherent* bundle, in contrast, scrambles fiber positions and is used only for uniform illumination.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Doppler Shift — Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students write $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ instead of $2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$. Always state clearly that *both* transmit-receive events contribute a Doppler shift, hence the doubling.
> 2. **Confusing color meanings:** Writing "blue = toward, red = away" will cost full marks. The correct convention is BART (*Blue Away, Red Toward*).
> 3. **Holter vs. Standard ECG:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if a student says Holter is used for "checking the heart." Be specific — it is for capturing *intermittent/transient* arrhythmias that a snapshot ECG misses.
> 4. **Endoscope — Coherent vs. Non-coherent bundles:** Students often mix them up. Light *transmission* uses a *non-coherent* bundle; *image* transmission requires a *coherent* bundle.
> 5. **Insonation angle dependence:** Writing "Doppler shift is independent of angle" is a critical error. Always emphasize $\Delta f \propto \cos\theta$.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Doppler shift must be in Hz, velocity in m/s, and tissue speed in m/s — mixing cm/s with m/s is a frequent slip-up.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

**Color Doppler Systems:**
- Color Doppler overlays velocity information on B-mode grayscale images using the Doppler shift equation.
- Master formula: $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$, with $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- Color convention: **Red = toward transducer, Blue = away from transducer** (remember "BART").
- Maximum unambiguous velocity: $v_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$; exceeding this causes **aliasing**.
- Best sensitivity at $\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$; zero sensitivity at $\theta = 90^\circ$.
- Wall (clutter) filters reject low-frequency shifts from vessel wall motion.

**Holter Monitoring:**
- Portable continuous ambulatory ECG recorder worn for **24–48 hours** (sometimes 7 days).
- Uses **5–7 disposable Ag/AgCl electrodes**, differential amplifier with high CMRR (>100 dB), 12/16-bit ADC, and flash storage.
- Captures transient arrhythmias missed by in-clinic ECG: paroxysmal AF, NSVT, intermittent heart block.
- Post-recording analysis includes QRS detection, beat classification, ST-segment analysis, and HRV computation.
- Limitations: limited channel count, electrode detachment artifact, fixed recording duration, manual analysis time.

**Endoscopy:**
- Direct visualization of internal hollow organs via a flexible or rigid illuminated scope.
- Key optical principle: **Total Internal Reflection** in optical fibers (core $n_1$, cladding $n_2$, $n_1 > n_2$).
- **Coherent bundle** carries the image (fibers in same position at both ends); **non-coherent bundle** carries illumination only.
- Modern video endoscopes replace the coherent bundle with a distal **CCD/CMOS sensor**.
- Components: xenon light source, fiber bundle, objective lens, angulation wires, working channel, insufflation channel.
- Common types: gastroscope, colonoscope, bronchoscope, choledochoscope, cystoscope.
- Clinical applications: diagnosis + biopsy + therapeutic interventions (polyp removal, hemostasis, stent placement).

> [!NOTE] **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For any Doppler problem in the exam, always (a) write the formula, (b) substitute knowns with units, (c) compute step-by-step, and (d) interpret the result clinically. The interpretation often carries an extra 1–2 marks and distinguishes a 7/7 answer from a 5/7 answer.
